title: [HIGH] conformance: special runner uses FsciSpecialErrorKind::DomainError for fixture-schema validation errors (191 sites) — conflates fixture bugs with domain violations
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22.

191 call sites (grep 'special_invalid_fixture_error') construct errors like:

    fn special_invalid_fixture_error(function, mode) -> FsciSpecialError {
        FsciSpecialError {
            function,
            kind: FsciSpecialErrorKind::DomainError,       // <-- WRONG KIND
            mode,
            detail: 'invalid fixture arity for special case',  // <-- SAME STRING everywhere
        }
    }

Related adjacent helpers reuse the DomainError kind for different schema violations:
    usize_from_f64_arg @ 10806: DomainError / 'fixture argument must be a finite usize integer'
    roots_component @ 10819: DomainError / 'roots fixture index is out of range'
    special_scalar_from_tensor @ 10834: NotYetImplemented / 'expected scalar output in conformance fixture'

Problems:

1. Kind conflation: DomainError means 'the input is mathematically out of the function's domain' (e.g., gamma(-1) hitting a pole). Fixture arity mismatch is NOT a domain violation -- it's a schema / harness bug. Conformance cases expecting 'DomainError' (for e.g. gammaln(0)) now PASS when the fixture has a typo that makes the Rust call arity-check fail first. Real domain-violation coverage becomes indistinguishable from fixture typos.

2. Detail string uniformity: 191 copies of 'invalid fixture arity for special case'. When a case fails, the error message doesn't say WHICH case ID, WHICH function, WHICH arity was expected vs received. Operator reading the failure log sees the same message 191 times across runs.

3. Inconsistent choices: some fixture-schema failures use DomainError (arity, usize_from_f64, roots_component index), others use NotYetImplemented (scalar-from-tensor). No principled mapping.

Fix:
1. Add FsciSpecialErrorKind::FixtureSchemaError variant (or equivalent at conformance-runner level since fsci-special types shouldn't know about fixtures -- this kind lives in fsci-conformance).
2. Have conformance-layer helpers construct a ConformanceError::FixtureSchema{ case_id, function, reason } instead of returning fsci-special's SpecialError.
3. Update special_invalid_fixture_error signature to include expected_arity and received_arity:
       fn schema_error_arity(case_id, function, expected: usize, received: usize) -> ConformanceError { ... }
       detail: 'case {case_id} function {function}: expected {expected} args, got {received}'
4. The runner then treats ConformanceError::FixtureSchema as UNJUDGEABLE (per ihex) rather than failing the case as a mismatched-domain-error.

Related: ihex (both-errored = pass -- this amplifies ihex since fixture-schema errors masquerade as matching DomainErrors), gnun (error-string equality), ivg5 (differential entry is self-check).
close_reason: Dedicated special fixture-schema errors so malformed conformance cases can no longer masquerade as matching DomainError results

title: EPIC: Wire CASP into fsci-linalg
description:
## Background

CASP (Condition-Aware Solver Portfolio) is the stated crown-jewel innovation of FrankenSciPy (README §"What Makes This Project Special", SPEC §0 Prime Directive). It performs runtime algorithm selection by inspecting conditioning diagnostics (condition number, sparsity pattern, stiffness ratio) and selecting the optimal solver from a portfolio, providing stability guarantees via formal certificates.

## Current State (2026-04-09 Reality Check)

fsci-runtime/src/lib.rs contains a FULLY FUNCTIONAL SolverPortfolio with:
- 5 solver actions (DirectLU, PivotedQR, SVDFallback, DiagonalFastPath, TriangularFastPath)
- Loss matrix (5×4) with genuine expected-loss computation
- Conformal calibrator with empirical miscoverage tracking
- select_action() method with soft condition-posterior via logistic blending
- 8 passing unit tests

BUT: ZERO callers exist. solve(), inv(), lstsq(), pinv(), eig() in fsci-linalg DO NOT consult the portfolio. The function solve_with_casp mentioned in FEATURE_PARITY.md does not exist anywhere in the codebase. CASP is vaporware — beautiful code that nobody calls.

## Goal

Wire SolverPortfolio into every major fsci-linalg entry point so that CASP is the REAL dispatch mechanism, not a decoration. This is the single most important gap between "what the project claims to be" and "what it actually is."

## Success Criteria

1. fsci-linalg::solve() calls SolverPortfolio::select_action() and dispatches to the selected algorithm
2. inv(), lstsq(), pinv() similarly consult CASP for their internal solver paths
3. eig/eigh/svd pass conditioning diagnostics through CASP for fallback selection
4. Integration tests prove CASP selects DIFFERENT solvers for well-conditioned vs ill-conditioned inputs
5. Stability certificate metadata is returned alongside numerical results
6. Conformance tests still pass (no behavioral regression)

## Serves Project Goal

Without this, the entire product thesis ("CASP: runtime algorithm selection driven by conditioning diagnostics") is marketing fiction. This epic makes the thesis real.

title: Add conditioning diagnostic extraction to fsci-linalg
description:
## What

Add a function condition_diagnostics(a: &[Vec<f64>]) -> ConditionReport in fsci-linalg that extracts the numerical properties CASP needs to select a solver:
- Estimated condition number (via cheap 1-norm estimate, not full SVD)
- Matrix structure flags: diagonal, triangular (upper/lower), symmetric, SPD, banded
- Size category (small/medium/large for CASP's size-aware selection)
- Sparsity ratio (fraction of near-zero entries)

## Why

SolverPortfolio::select_action() in fsci-runtime expects a ConditionPosterior and structure flags. Currently nothing in fsci-linalg computes these. This is the data pipeline that feeds CASP.

## Implementation Notes

- Use LU-based 1-norm condition estimate (O(n²) not O(n³)) — SciPy uses this approach
- Structure detection: check symmetry, triangularity, diagonal dominance
- Return a ConditionReport struct that maps cleanly to fsci-runtime's input types
- Do NOT compute full SVD for condition number — too expensive for the dispatch hot path

## Tests

- Diagonal matrix → reports diagonal=true, condition ~1.0
- Hilbert matrix → reports high condition number
- Identity → condition=1.0
- Random matrix → no special structure flags
- Triangular matrix → reports triangular

## Files to modify

- crates/fsci-linalg/src/lib.rs (or new diagnostics.rs module)
- crates/fsci-linalg/Cargo.toml (if new module)
close_reason: Implemented ConditionReport diagnostics and refactored solve_with_casp to consume them

title: Wire SolverPortfolio::select_action() into fsci-linalg::solve()
description:
## What

Modify fsci-linalg::solve() to: (1) call condition_diagnostics() on the input matrix, (2) call SolverPortfolio::select_action() with the diagnostics, (3) dispatch to the selected solver algorithm (LU, QR, SVD, diagonal fast-path, triangular fast-path), (4) return a result that includes the stability certificate metadata alongside the solution vector.

## Why

This is THE core integration point. solve() is the most-called linalg function and the poster child for CASP. Currently it hardcodes a single decomposition path. After this change, it dynamically selects based on conditioning.

## Implementation Notes

- SolverPortfolio lives in fsci-runtime. Add fsci-runtime as a dependency of fsci-linalg (or pass portfolio as parameter).
- IMPORTANT: The portfolio should be OPTIONAL. If no portfolio is provided, fall back to the current behavior. This preserves backwards compatibility and keeps tests working during migration.
- The selected solver action should be recorded in a SolveReport alongside the solution.
- Handle the case where CASP selects a solver that fails (e.g., Cholesky on non-SPD) — fall back to next-best per portfolio ranking.

## Tests

- Well-conditioned diagonal matrix → CASP selects DiagonalFastPath
- Well-conditioned triangular matrix → CASP selects TriangularFastPath
- General well-conditioned → CASP selects DirectLU
- Ill-conditioned → CASP selects PivotedQR or SVDFallback
- All existing solve() tests still pass (regression)

## Dependencies

- Requires: condition_diagnostics extraction task

## Files to modify

- crates/fsci-linalg/src/lib.rs
- crates/fsci-linalg/Cargo.toml
close_reason: Fully implemented: solve_with_casp calls condition_diagnostics, select_action, dispatch, returns certificates

title: Wire CASP into inv(), lstsq(), pinv()
description:
## What

Extend the CASP integration from solve() to inv(), lstsq(), and pinv(). Each of these internally uses a decomposition path that CASP should select.

## Why

CASP should drive ALL major linalg entry points, not just solve(). inv() uses LU/QR, lstsq() uses QR/SVD, pinv() uses SVD — CASP should choose between these based on conditioning.

## Implementation Notes

- inv(): condition_diagnostics → select between LU path vs. QR path for stability
- lstsq(): condition_diagnostics → select between QR (well-conditioned) vs. SVD (ill-conditioned/rank-deficient)
- pinv(): condition_diagnostics → always SVD but threshold selection informed by conditioning
- Each returns stability certificate metadata in its result

## Tests

- inv() on well-conditioned matrix → LU path, fast
- inv() on ill-conditioned matrix → QR path, stable
- lstsq() on well-conditioned overdetermined → QR path
- lstsq() on rank-deficient → SVD fallback
- All existing tests still pass

## Dependencies

- Requires: solve() CASP integration task (establishes the pattern)

## Files to modify

- crates/fsci-linalg/src/lib.rs
close_reason: Implemented inv_with_casp, lstsq_with_casp, pinv_with_casp with CASP-driven algorithm selection. inv() now delegates to inv_with_casp, lstsq() to lstsq_with_casp, pinv() to pinv_with_casp. All tests pass.

title: CASP integration tests proving dynamic solver selection
description:
## What

Write integration tests that PROVE CASP actually selects different solvers based on input properties. These tests must inspect the SolveReport to verify which algorithm was selected, not just that the answer is correct.

## Why

Without these tests, someone could add a CASP call that always returns DirectLU and all existing tests would still pass. We need tests that assert the DISPATCH decision, not just the numerical result.

## Tests to Write

1. test_casp_selects_diagonal_fastpath: input = diag([1,2,3]) → SolveReport.selected_action == DiagonalFastPath
2. test_casp_selects_triangular_fastpath: input = upper triangular → TriangularFastPath
3. test_casp_selects_lu_for_wellconditioned: input = random well-conditioned → DirectLU
4. test_casp_selects_qr_for_moderate: input = moderate condition number → PivotedQR
5. test_casp_selects_svd_for_illconditioned: input = Hilbert matrix → SVDFallback
6. test_casp_fallback_on_failure: input that causes Cholesky to fail → falls back to LU
7. test_casp_stability_certificate_populated: verify certificate fields are non-empty

## Dependencies

- Requires: CASP wired into solve(), inv(), lstsq(), pinv()

## Files to modify

- crates/fsci-linalg/src/lib.rs (tests module)
close_reason: Added CASP solver-selection tests and certificate coverage in fsci-linalg

title: [optimize] Multivariate root finding: root, fsolve
description:
## Goal
Implement multivariate root finding — solving systems of nonlinear equations F(x) = 0 where F: R^n → R^n.

## What to implement
- `root(fun, x0, method, jac)`: find root of vector function. Methods:
  - "hybr": Powell hybrid method (modified from MINPACK, default)
  - "lm": Levenberg-Marquardt (reuse from least_squares)
- `fsolve(func, x0)`: legacy interface, wraps root with method="hybr"
- RootResult: x, fun (residuals), success, message

## Why this matters
Systems of nonlinear equations arise in equilibrium calculations (chemical, economic), circuit simulation, and implicit ODE/PDE solvers. fsolve is one of SciPy's most-used functions.

## Implementation approach
- Powell hybrid: Newton's method with trust-region globalization. Compute Jacobian via finite differences, solve J*δx = -F(x), use trust-region step if full Newton step is too large.
- Reuse finite_diff_jacobian and solve_damped_normal_equations from curvefit module

## Dependencies
- Reuses least_squares infrastructure (Jacobian computation, damped solve)
- Uses existing linalg solve

## Testing requirements
- 2×2 system: x²+y²=1, x-y=0 → (1/√2, 1/√2)
- 3×3 system from chemical equilibrium
- Starting near solution: fast convergence
- Starting far: still converges for well-conditioned problems
- Singular Jacobian: graceful failure
- At least 6 tests
close_reason: Implemented fsolve (Newton + finite-diff Jacobian + line search damping) with 5 tests for 2x2, 3x3, nonlinear systems

close_reason: Added crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/FSCI-P2C-007_kstwobign_golden.json (frozen scipy reference table: 20 x-values for pdf/cdf/sf + 9 q-values for ppf, scipy 1.17.1) and crates/fsci-conformance/tests/golden_stats_kstwobign.rs that loads it offline and pins fsci-stats KsTwoBign against it. Per-family tolerances: cdf/sf 1e-13 (both fsci and scipy converge to fp epsilon), pdf 5e-8 (covers scipy cephes kolmogp's known ~1e-8 drift vs its own cdf finite-difference around x=0.85; fsci's analytical-derivative pdf is more consistent with the cdf series than scipy's separate routine), ppf 1e-9 relative. Catches future drift in either kstwobign series branch even when scipy is unavailable (e.g., on rch remote workers).

title: GAP: fsci-linalg sinm/cosm/tanm/sinhm/coshm/tanhm via funm (6/14 → 12/14)
description:
Per fgxx audit, fsci-linalg lacks 6 of 14 matrix-function primitives:
sinm, cosm, tanm, sinhm, coshm, tanhm (plus expm_cond/expm_frechet).

All six trig/hyperbolic flavors can be derived via the existing funm
mechanism with the corresponding scalar transform. The straightforward
formulas:

    sinm(A) = (exp(iA) - exp(-iA)) / (2i)
    cosm(A) = (exp(iA) + exp(-iA)) / 2
    tanm(A) = sinm(A) / cosm(A)    [or solve sinm = tanm * cosm]
    sinhm(A) = (expm(A) - expm(-A)) / 2
    coshm(A) = (expm(A) + expm(-A)) / 2
    tanhm(A) = sinhm(A) / coshm(A)

scipy derives these via funm with numpy's scalar trig functions;
ditto for fsci.

Acceptance:
- 6 new public fns: sinm, cosm, tanm, sinhm, coshm, tanhm.
- Each routes through funm or, for the complex-exponent pair, via
  expm on the 2n×2n augmented matrix.
- Tolerance contract in rustdoc matches scipy (roughly 1e-8 for
  well-conditioned inputs).
- COVERAGE.md updated (matrix_funcs family flips from 6/14 to 12/14).
- Unit tests per fn including a commuting pair (sinm²+cosm²=I).

Out of scope for this bead: expm_cond, expm_frechet (file separately).

Related: fgxx (parent audit).
close_reason: Completed in c1a606b

title: [HIGH] fuzz: p2c006_special_complex_branches EdgeF64 clamps to ±1e3 — overflow/branch-cut regimes unreachable
description:
Review-mode audit of fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_complex_branches.rs:34-48 and fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_bessel_derivatives.rs:33-47:

Both harnesses define:
    Self::Finite(value) if value.is_finite() => value.clamp(-1.0e3, 1.0e3),

This clamp means arbitrary::Arbitrary-generated f64s never reach:
- The overflow regime for gamma(z): gamma(170.624...) ≈ f64::MAX, gamma(171+) overflows. Never exercised.
- The overflow regime for Bessel I_v(z): I_0(700) overflows. Never exercised.
- The precision-loss regime for lambertw at very large x where series diverge.
- Denormals (f64::MIN_POSITIVE * 0.5, sub-normal mantissas).
- Branch cuts along the negative real axis at |z| > 1e3 for log-like / sqrt-like functions.

Only the oracle_roundtrip harness (p2c006_special_oracle_roundtrip.rs:30) uses clamp(-1.0e6, 1.0e6) — still misses overflow domains for the worst-case distributions (gamma overflows at 171, but 1e6 is not a problem for exp1 or lambertw).

Also missing in complex_branches: systematic branch-cut sweep. For complex functions with a branch cut along (-inf, 0]:
- lambertw(x + 0i) vs lambertw(x - 0i) for x < -1/e — the two should disagree by 2πi on the principal branch. Current conjugation assertion skips when z.im == 0.
- wrightomega has a branch cut at {z: Re(z) < -1, Im(z) = -π} — not tested.

Ask:
1. Expand EdgeF64 with variants: Huge (1e300), NegHuge (-1e300), Overflow (f64::MAX), Underflow (f64::MIN_POSITIVE * 0.5), GammaPole (-1.0, -2.0, ..., -5.0), BranchCut (-1.0/E), NegAxisPlusTiny, NegAxisMinusTiny (to probe branch-cut continuity).
2. Relax the Finite clamp to (-1e300, 1e300) or remove entirely; add a size guard in the harness body per skill rule 'guard size' to avoid OOM, not to clip math domain.
3. Add a systematic branch-cut sweep: for each log-like function (lambertw, wrightomega), sample z = (x, +eps*i) and z = (x, -eps*i) for x ∈ {-1.0, -0.5, -1.0/E} and assert function values are continuous within tolerance when approached from same half-plane.

Impact: current fuzz 'coverage' for these fns is a thin band near the origin. Branch-cut bugs (like z5km / x2sz in git history) will NOT be caught by this harness.
close_reason: Expanded fuzz edge domains and added branch-cut continuity sweeps

title: [reality-check] CASP for fsci-opt — solver portfolio runtime selector
description: CASP is the crown-jewel innovation per SPEC §0 but is implemented for ONLY 4 linalg routines (solve/inv/lstsq/pinv). Extend to fsci-opt: runtime selector across BFGS / L-BFGS-B / Newton-CG / trust-region driven by problem conditioning + constraint structure + gradient availability. Add scipy.special.ai_zeros(n) and scipy.special.bi_zeros(n) returning the first n zeros of Ai and Bi via Newton iteration starting from the standard asymptotic series a_k ≈ -t·(1 + 5/(48 t²) - ...) where t = (3π(4k - 1)/8)^(2/3). title: [HIGH] conformance: PacketReport (lib.rs:1399) and ConformanceReport (lib.rs:7601) are parallel result schemas — PacketReport is serialized to parity_report.json while ConformanceReport (richer: max_diff, tolerance_used, oracle_status) is never persisted; run_differential_test results lose detail at the disk boundary
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22:

Two parallel result structs coexist:

  PacketReport (line 1399):
      packet_id, family,
      case_results: Vec<CaseResult>,  -- CaseResult = {case_id, passed, message}
      passed_cases, failed_cases,
      generated_unix_ms

  ConformanceReport (line 7601):
      fixture_path, packet_id, family,
      pass_count, fail_count,
      oracle_status,
      per_case_results: Vec<DifferentialCaseResult>,
          -- DifferentialCaseResult = {case_id, passed, message, max_diff,
                                         tolerance_used, oracle_status}
      generated_unix_ms

Field naming diverges: passed_cases vs pass_count, case_results vs
per_case_results. Structurally:
- PacketReport is CaseResult-carrying (3 fields per case).
- ConformanceReport is DifferentialCaseResult-carrying (6 fields per case
  including max_diff and tolerance_used).

What each is used for:
- PacketReport is written to parity_report.json (write_parity_artifacts
  at lib.rs:6572-6597) and read by dashboard.rs:6546 via
  load_packet_reports. The artifact pipeline is PacketReport-typed.
- ConformanceReport is RETURNED by run_differential_test but never
  SERIALIZED to a persistent artifact. Its max_diff and tolerance_used
  fields stay in-memory only.

Consequences:

1. Data loss at serialization boundary. If a conformance case fails
   with 'expected=3.0, got=3.001, max_diff=1e-3', the max_diff value
   exists in ConformanceReport::per_case_results but is DROPPED when
   results get serialized to parity_report.json (which only stores
   CaseResult.message as a free-text string).

2. Dashboard consumers see message strings only -- no structured diff
   or tolerance info. Forensics classify_failure (per vp77) attempts
   substring matching on message strings because that's all that
   survives serialization.

3. Developers maintaining the suite must mentally bridge two parallel
   schemas. Adding a new result field in one struct -- e.g., elapsed_ms
   in ConformanceReport -- silently does NOT appear in the persistent
   artifact unless PacketReport is also updated. Divergence accrues.

4. No #[serde(flatten)] or shared trait links them. Independent evolution
   guaranteed.

Fix:
1. MINIMAL: Have write_parity_artifacts accept ConformanceReport OR
   derive a conversion From<ConformanceReport> for PacketReport that's
   lossless if possible.
2. BETTER: unify the two into a single DifferentialPacketReport struct
   with rich per-case fields. Dashboard adapts to consume the richer
   struct. CaseResult retires.
3. ADD: a #[serde(tag = 'schema_version')] discriminator so parity_
   report.json consumers can distinguish versions and migrate.

Related: vp77 (forensics substring matching because diff is lost),
7h9o (dashboard distorts view), fytm (evidence parity_report is
self-check), c7b4 (docstring misleads). Yet another layer of
schema-fragmentation dysfunction in the conformance stack.
close_reason: Verified the richer PacketReport schema and automatic differential parity artifact persistence already present on HEAD; validated with rch differential/dashboard tests and fsci-conformance check.

title: [code-review] [HIGH] fsci-special lambertw_scalar(1.0) returns NaN due to bad initial-guess threshold
description: lambertw_scalar at x=1 returns NaN. The initial-guess branch is 'if x < 1.0 { x } else { x.ln() - x.ln().ln() }'. At x = 1.0 (and any x where ln(x) ≤ 0), the else branch evaluates ln(ln(1)) = ln(0) = -∞, so the initial guess is +∞ and Halley's iteration diverges. Reproduce: lambertw_scalar(1.0). Expected: W(1) ≈ 0.5671432904... (Omega constant). Fix: change threshold to 'x < std::f64::consts::E' (≈ 2.718) so the regular-x branch is used until x.ln() > 0, or use a safe analytic approximation. File: crates/fsci-special/src/convenience.rs:2706-2707.
close_reason: Fixed: changed initial-guess threshold from x < 1.0 to x < e and use Bürmann seed x/(1+x) for the small-x branch. lambertw_scalar(1.0) now returns 0.5671432904 (Omega constant) and tightened MR38 metamorphic test to require finite output for all positive x including 1.0. 66/66 fsci-special metamorphic tests pass.

title: [parity-gap] spherical_in forward recurrence diverges for moderate n at small x
description:
Same defect family as the just-closed [frankenscipy-xlci0] for spherical_jn. spherical_in_nonneg (crates/fsci-special/src/bessel.rs:1649) uses forward recurrence i_{k+1}(x) = i_{k-1}(x) − (2k+1)/x · i_k(x), which catastrophically cancels when x ≪ 2k+1 because i_n(x) decays super-exponentially in n while the prefactor (2k+1)/x grows.

For x=0.1 the recurrence collapses past n=5:

  n   forward (broken)   series truth
  ─   ─────────────────  ──────────────
  5   +4.04e−9           +9.62e−10        (4× too big)
  6   −3.39e−7           +6.42e−12        (wrong sign)
  9   +1.12               +5.34e−18        (completely wrong)
  10  −213                +7.3e−21         (wrong by 23 orders)

Repro:
  let x = 0.1f64;
  let mut i_prev = x.sinh()/x;
  let mut i_curr = x.cosh()/x - x.sinh()/(x*x);
  for k in 1..10 { let next = i_prev - (2.0*k as f64 + 1.0)/x * i_curr; i_prev = i_curr; i_curr = next; }
  // i_curr now ~ +1.12 instead of +5.34e-18

Fix: switch to Miller's downward recurrence in the n ≥ 2 ∧ |x| < n regime, exactly mirroring the spherical_jn_nonneg fix. The downward recurrence for i_n is:
  i_{k-1}(x) = (2k+1)/x · i_k(x) + i_{k+1}(x)
Stable from any starting value, with normalization against the analytic i_0(x) = sinh(x)/x.

Acceptance: spherical_in(5, 0.1) within 1% of x^5/11!! · (1 + x²/26); spherical_in(10, 0.1) positive and < 1e-15; existing spherical_in tests still pass.

Current fsci-integrate exports nquad/quad_vec/etc. but no cubature API.

Scope:

- Add unit tests for empty/bad bounds, simple product integrals, vector-valued/infinite or unsupported cases as appropriate.

Suggested reservation: crates/fsci-integrate/** plus relevant fsci-conformance fixture/test files.

title: fsci-special: Bessel j0/y0 precision gap vs scipy (~3e-9 to 5.7e-8 abs)
description: Surfaced by diff_special_bessel (frankenscipy-61q26). fsci-special's j0 lands ~3e-9 off scipy.special.j0 uniformly across the small-to-moderate x regime; y0 lands ~5.7e-8 at x=1e-12 (the singularity prefactor amplifies the underlying cf/series floor). j1/y1 are tighter (~1e-11). The current diff harness uses 1e-7 abs to absorb this; tightening to scipy-comparable 1e-13 requires an fsci-special precision sweep — likely a Chebyshev-rational fit on the (0, 8] interval and a tighter asymptotic seam. P2 because the harness ships at 1e-7 which is sufficient for distribution-level conformance, but fsci-stats consumers (e.g. nakagami, rice, vonmises, rdist) inherit this floor.
notes: Expanded scope 2026-05-07h: fsci-special's expn lands ~7.2e-8 abs at n=1, x=1 (series-asymptotic seam at x≈1). Tighter at moderate x and larger n. Same pattern as Bessel/Spence/Fresnel — series-asymptotic transition needs a tighter dispatch threshold across many of the special-function modules.

title: [HIGH] conformance: execute_linalg_case_with_differential_audit at lib.rs:6801 only wires audit ledger for Solve variant — Inv/Det/Lstsq/Pinv/SolveTriangular/SolveBanded (6 operations) fall through to non-audit variant via  at 6835
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:6801-6837 on 2026-04-22:

execute_linalg_case_with_differential_audit claims 'differential audit' --
it takes a SyncSharedAuditLedger parameter and threads it into
fsci_linalg::solve_with_audit. Good for the Solve variant.

But line 6835 fallback:

    _ => execute_linalg_case(case),

falls through to the non-audit variant for every OTHER LinalgCase
variant (Inv, Det, Lstsq, Pinv, SolveTriangular, SolveBanded). That
function at lib.rs:6639 calls fsci_linalg::inv / det / lstsq / pinv /
etc. WITHOUT any audit ledger argument. fsci-linalg's bare APIs
(grep pub fn) don't accept an audit ledger -- only the solve_with_*
family does.

Consequences:
1. For conformance cases with operation != 'solve', the audit ledger
   captures ZERO events even in Hardened mode.
2. run_differential_linalg (lib.rs:7817) emits audit_ledger.jsonl at
   line 7854 only if ledger.is_empty() is false. If all cases in a
   packet are Inv/Det/Lstsq/Pinv/..., the ledger is empty and no
   file is emitted.
3. The FSCI-P2C-002 linalg fixture distribution is:
       solve: 15, inv: 9, det: 8, solve_triangular: 6,
       lstsq: 6, pinv: 6, solve_banded: 3
   So 15/53 = 28% of cases get audited; 38/53 = 72% do not.

This is a narrower instance of egba (crate-wide audit emission gap).
Fixing requires:
1. fsci_linalg to expose _with_audit variants for inv / det / lstsq /
   pinv / solve_triangular / solve_banded.
2. execute_linalg_case_with_differential_audit to dispatch each
   variant to its _with_audit counterpart.

Related: egba (11/18 crates emit zero AuditAction), mhuk (runner
wires ledger only for 3 of 12 families), xo4o (Hardened coverage
2.9%), 95ld (5 families ignore mode: String), ovxs (audit type
fragmentation). This is the 6th layer of the audit-trail bug stack.
close_reason: Added audit-aware wrappers for inv/det/lstsq/pinv/solve_triangular/solve_banded in fsci-linalg, routed all six through execute_linalg_case_with_differential_audit, and added a non-Solve differential audit regression. rch cargo test/check passed for fsci-linalg and fsci-conformance; conformance clippy remains blocked only by preexisting unrelated fsci-spatial/fsci-stats warnings.

title: fsci-conformance: scipy diff for idealfourths + iqr

Adds diff_stats_idealfourths.rs covering ideal fourths (robust quartile estimators, mstats.idealfourths) returning (qlo, qup) and the standard iqr Q3-Q1 against scipy.stats. 4 datasets × 3 arms (qlo + qup + iqr) = 12 cases. Tol 1e-12 abs (closed-form quantile interpolation).

title: fsci_ndimage::gaussian_filter radius formula differs from scipy on non-integer σ
description: fsci_ndimage::gaussian_filter uses radius=ceil(4σ); scipy.ndimage.gaussian_filter uses int(truncate*σ + 0.5) with truncate=4.0. The two agree at σ ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 2.0, ...} but diverge on non-integer σ — e.g., σ=1.3 gives fsci=6, scipy=5. This produces small numerical differences in output (kernel tails of a Gaussian) but breaks bit-exact parity. Either change fsci to int(truncate*σ+0.5) to match, or accept a tolerance loosening in conformance and document the divergence. diff_ndimage_gaussian_filter currently scopes to aligned σ only. Filed alongside diff_ndimage_gaussian_filter harness (frankenscipy-6u7yg).

title: fsci-stats: mquantiles aleph not clamped to [1, n-1] — diverges from scipy at extreme probs
description: P3: fsci's mquantiles diverges from scipy.stats.mstats.mquantiles when aleph = n*p + m falls below 1 (typical for R8/R6 variants at small p with small n). E.g. compact_n12 r8 at p=0.05: aleph=0.95, fsci computes j_usize=0.saturating_sub(1)=0, g=0.95 → result = 0.05*x[0] + 0.95*x[1] = 1.95. scipy clamps aleph to [1, n-1], giving k=1, gamma=0 → result = x[0] = 1.0 (abs diff 0.95). Fix: at line 15637 of crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs, clamp j_float to [1, n-1] before splitting into (j, g), matching scipy's clip(aleph, 1, n-1) → floor / clip-to-[0,1].

title: Oracle capture coverage is incomplete across P2C packet families
description:
Review/testing sweep after DB recovery on 2026-04-28 compared packet fixtures, python_oracle scripts, and artifact captures. There are 17 packet fixtures (FSCI-P2C-001 through FSCI-P2C-017) and oracle scripts for many domains, but committed oracle_capture.json artifacts exist only for P2C-002, P2C-003, P2C-005, P2C-006, P2C-007, P2C-012, P2C-014, P2C-015, P2C-016, and P2C-017. No oracle_capture.json was found for P2C-001, P2C-004, P2C-008, P2C-009, P2C-010, P2C-011, or P2C-013.

Why this matters:
- README.md still frames real oracle capture and drift diffs as a core conformance workflow.
- The repo has scipy_sparse_oracle.py, scipy_cluster_oracle.py, scipy_spatial_oracle.py, scipy_signal_oracle.py, and scipy_integrate_oracle.py, so several missing capture families have an apparent oracle path.
- Without an explicit coverage matrix, it is unclear which packet families are genuinely differential, intentionally self-checking, or simply missing capture artifacts.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Add a machine-readable or test-enforced oracle-capture coverage matrix for all FSCI-P2C packet families.
2. Generate and commit oracle_capture.json for the missing families where a real SciPy oracle script exists and fixture semantics permit it.
3. For families that should not have oracle capture, document the reason in the matrix and ensure dashboard/report wording does not call them oracle-backed.
4. Add a regression test that fails when a packet fixture has an oracle script but neither an oracle capture artifact nor an explicit documented exemption.
close_reason: Added oracle_topology.rs with coverage matrix: 10 packets with oracle capture, 7 with documented property-based exemptions. Regression test prevents gaps. Commit 93b60e0.

CASP should pick branch by argument magnitude + precision target + parameter-stability check, with explicit fallback chain and convergence tracking. reality-check-2026-05-03.md gap C.9.

title: Implement closed-form moments for Burr12, InvWeibull, TukeyLambda, GenHalfLogistic distributions
description:
## Context
Audit found 7 distribution moment methods returning f64::NAN as stubs where closed-form
formulas exist using only Gamma/Beta functions and harmonic numbers (already available in
fsci-special).

## Scope
All changes in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:

1. **Burr12::var()** (line ~3258) — Currently returns NaN with comment "Complex formula,
   return NaN for now". Formula: var = E[X²] - (E[X])², where E[X^r] =
   d·Γ(d-r/c)·Γ(1+r/c)/Γ(d+1) when c·d > r. Guard: return NaN when c·d ≤ 2.

2. **InvWeibull::var()** (line ~3712) — Formula: Γ(1-2/c) - Γ(1-1/c)² when c > 2.
   Guard: return NaN when c ≤ 2 (infinite variance).

3. **TukeyLambda::var()** (line ~3668) — Formula:
   var = 2·(1/(1+2λ) - Γ(λ+1)²/Γ(2λ+2))/λ² for λ ≠ 0.
   Special case λ=0: var = π²/3.

4. **GenHalfLogistic::mean()** (line ~3599) — Formula: mean = (1/c)·(H(1/c) + γ) where
   H is harmonic number and γ is Euler-Mascheroni. Alternatively:
   mean = (1-1/c)·∫₀¹ t^(1/c-1)·(-ln(1-t)) dt — use digamma: mean = (ψ(1/c+1) + γ)/c.

5. **GenHalfLogistic::var()** (line ~3604) — Formula: var = E[X²] - mean².
   E[X²] = (ψ₁(1/c+1) + (ψ(1/c+1)+γ)²)/c² where ψ₁ is trigamma.

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests comparing each moment to SciPy reference values (≥3 parameter combos each)
- Test boundary cases (e.g., Burr12 with c·d near 2, InvWeibull with c near 2)
- Test that existing pdf/cdf/ppf behavior is unchanged
- Verify var() ≥ 0 for all valid parameters via property test
- Add to existing e2e_stats.rs as new scenario: e2e_011_closed_form_moments

## Validation
```
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- burr12
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- inv_weibull
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- tukey
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- gen_half_logistic
cargo test -p fsci-conformance --test e2e_stats
```
close_reason: Already implemented: Burr12::var(), InvWeibull::var(), TukeyLambda::var(), GenHalfLogistic::mean()/var() all have closed-form formulas in current code.

title: fsci-integrate: quad has reference tests but no metamorphic invariants
description: Skill: /testing-metamorphic. quad.rs has 8+ tests pinning specific reference values (constant, linear, polynomial, sin, exp, negative interval, reversed/equal bounds, non-finite) but ZERO tests of the three classical metamorphic invariants of definite integration: (1) additivity ∫_a^b f = ∫_a^c f + ∫_c^b f for c ∈ (a, b); (2) linearity ∫(α·f + β·g) = α∫f + β∫g; (3) translation invariance ∫_a^b f(x) dx = ∫_{a+t}^{b+t} f(x−t) dx. These don't depend on a hard-coded scipy value, so they remain pinned even as quad's adaptive tolerance schedule changes — exactly the kind of invariant that catches subtle adaptive-step regressions. Same tick.
close_reason: Shipped 3 metamorphic invariant tests for quad: additivity (split at 25/50/75% of [a,b] across exp/sin/cubic functions), linearity (α∫f + β∫g = ∫(αf+βg) on x²/sin over [0,π]), and translation invariance (∫_a^b f(x) = ∫_{a+t}^{b+t} f(x-t) for shifts t∈{0,1,-2.5,5} on Gaussian/sin/Cauchy). No hard-coded scipy references — adaptive-tolerance regressions in either half of an interval would now fail. fsci-integrate now 169 tests (+3).

title: fsci-stats: CrystalBall skew/kurt closed forms

close_reason: done

title: fsci-stats: port scipy.stats.yulesimon (discrete)
description: Port scipy.stats.yulesimon as YuleSimon struct (Yule-Simon, discrete). One parameter alpha > 0. Support k = 1, 2, 3, .... Closed forms: pmf(k; α) = α · B(k, α+1) where B is Beta function; cdf(k; α) = 1 - k · B(k, α+1). Mean = α/(α-1) for α > 1; var = α²/((α-1)²(α-2)) for α > 2. Use ln_gamma for log-Beta to avoid overflow at large k. 3 anchor tests at scipy 1.17.1 tol 1e-12: pmf/cdf at 6 anchors; moments at 2 alpha anchors; pmf telescoping cdf(k) - cdf(k-1) = pmf(k).
close_reason: Ported scipy.stats.yulesimon as YuleSimon struct (discrete). One parameter alpha > 0. Support k = 1, 2, 3, .... All formulas closed-form via Beta function (computed in log-space using ln_gamma to avoid overflow): pmf(k; alpha) = alpha * B(k, alpha+1); cdf(k; alpha) = 1 - k * B(k, alpha+1) [clamped to 0..1]. Mean = alpha/(alpha-1) for alpha > 1 else inf. Var = alpha^2/((alpha-1)^2 * (alpha-2)) for alpha > 2 else inf. 3 tests at scipy 1.17.1 tol 1e-12: pmf/cdf at 6 (alpha, k) anchors; mean/var at 2 alpha anchors plus boundary checks; pmf telescoping pmf(k) = cdf(k) - cdf(k-1) across 5 alphas x 10 k. fsci-stats --lib: 1122 -> 1125.

title: [parity-gap] missing scipy.special.eval_chebyc / eval_chebys (Chebyshev C, S)
description:
scipy.special.eval_chebyc(n, x) and eval_chebys(n, x) evaluate the Chebyshev polynomials of the first/second kind on the interval [-2, 2] (vs T_n, U_n on [-1, 1]). They satisfy the simple identities

  C_n(x) = 2 · T_n(x/2)
  S_n(x) = U_n(x/2)

fsci ships eval_chebyt and eval_chebyu but not the C/S variants. Filing as P1 because the C/S forms appear in classical scattering and quantum-mechanical applications (dimer chain Green's functions, spectral methods on [-2, 2]).

Acceptance: pub fn eval_chebyc(n: u32, x: f64) -> f64 and pub fn eval_chebys(n: u32, x: f64) -> f64; reference-pinned tests covering n in 0..=4 against the closed-form low-degree polynomials (C_0=2, C_1=x, C_2=x²-2, C_3=x³-3x, …; S_0=1, S_1=x, S_2=x²-1, S_3=x³-2x, …).

For balanced_n14 fixture: scipy two-sided p=0.962 vs fsci p=1.0 (abs 0.038); 'less' p=0.519 vs 0.534 (0.015); 'greater' p=0.481 vs 0.500 (0.019). title: fsci_ndimage::gaussian_laplace + gaussian_gradient_magnitude diverge from scipy
description: fsci_ndimage::gaussian_laplace = laplace(gaussian_filter(.)) and gaussian_gradient_magnitude use composed filters. Probed against scipy.ndimage equivalents at σ ∈ {0.5, 1, 2} (where gaussian_filter is aligned per bead 0venh): both diverge significantly — max diff up to 13 for gradient_magnitude and 3.2 for laplace on 1-D inputs. Root cause likely differs from σ-radius mismatch: scipy's gaussian_gradient_magnitude uses gaussian_filter1d(order=1) per axis (derivative-of-Gaussian convolution), while fsci computes finite differences after blurring. scipy's gaussian_laplace uses laplace via the 2nd-derivative Gaussian filter; fsci uses a separate Laplacian stencil after blurring. These are mathematically different. Either reimplement to match or accept the docstring divergence and document explicitly.

title: fsci-stats: Arcsine.pdf wrong at x=0 and x=1 (should be +inf)
description: Arcsine.pdf in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:8753 short-circuits to 0.0 for x ≤ 0 || x ≥ 1, but the formula 1/(π·√(x(1-x))) diverges to +∞ at the boundaries x=0 and x=1. scipy.stats.arcsine returns inf there. Same defect class as the BetaDist boundary fix in 9e4bfd9. Fix: only return 0 for x strictly outside [0, 1]; return +∞ at boundaries (the limit). Add boundary regression test.
close_reason: [LOW] Fixed Arcsine.pdf boundary defect at x=0 and x=1, found via /mock-code-finder sweep generalizing the BetaDist boundary fix in 9e4bfd9. Old code returned 0.0 at both boundaries; the formula 1/(π·√(x(1-x))) actually diverges to +∞ there (a singularity). scipy.stats.arcsine.pdf(0) and pdf(1) both return inf. Fix: only return 0 for x strictly outside [0, 1]; return +∞ exactly at the boundary. Discovered an existing test arcsine_pdf_boundaries that asserted the WRONG value (pdf=0 at boundary, with comment 'PDF should be 0 (singularity)' — which is the opposite of what a singularity means); updated it to assert is_infinite() && positive. Plus a new arcsine_pdf_boundaries_match_scipy test pinning the symmetry around 0.5 and out-of-support behavior. fsci-stats --lib: 1039 → 1040.

title: fsci-special: Carlson elliprf missing
description: Skill: /porting-to-rust. After elliprc (mxxij), the Carlson family is still incomplete — elliprf (the symmetric integral of the first kind) is the canonical building block of scipy.special's elliptic suite. RF(x, y, z) = (1/2) ∫₀^∞ [(t+x)(t+y)(t+z)]^(-1/2) dt. Will port using Carlson's duplication algorithm (iterate xn = (xn + λ)/4 with λ = √(xy) + √(yz) + √(xz) until convergence, then 5th-order Taylor series correction). Closed-form anchors: RF(x, x, x) = 1/√x; RF(0, 1, 1) = π/2; RF(0, y, y) = π/(2√y); RF(1, 2, 4) ≈ 0.6850858 (scipy reference value).
close_reason: Shipped pub fn elliprf(x, y, z) using Carlson's duplication algorithm with 5th-order Taylor correction. Iterates (xn, yn, zn) → ((xn+λ)/4, ...) with λ = √(xy)+√(yz)+√(xz) until max(|1-x/μ|) < 2e-3, then returns μ^(-1/2) · (1 - E2/10 + E3/14 + E2²/24 - 3·E2·E3/44). 5 tests: diagonal RF(x,x,x)=1/√x for 6 x; zero-argument RF(0,y,y)=π/(2√y) for 4 y; scipy reference RF(1,2,4)≈0.6850858; argument symmetry x↔y, x↔z; negative/NaN/double-zero rejection. fsci-special now 769 tests (+5).

title: fsci-conformance: scipy diff for stats.foldcauchy
description: FoldedCauchy has anchor tests in fsci-stats/src/lib.rs but no dedicated scipy diff harness. Add crates/fsci-conformance/tests/diff_stats_foldcauchy.rs that drives 5 c-values × 7 x-values for pdf/cdf/sf and 5 c × 5 q-values for ppf through scipy.stats.foldcauchy via subprocess. close_reason: Added crates/fsci-conformance/tests/diff_stats_foldcauchy.rs (5 c-values × 7 x-values × 3 families + 5 c × 5 q-values for ppf = 130 cases through scipy.stats.foldcauchy via subprocess at tol 1e-12 abs / 1e-9 rel ppf). REAL DEFECT FOUND: fsci's FoldedCauchy.cdf had a stray '+ 0.5' term (leftover from the unfolded-Cauchy form) that made cdf off by exactly 0.5 at c=0 across all x. Fixed: cdf = (arctan(x-c) + arctan(x+c)) / π without the spurious offset. Derivation: P(|Y| ≤ x) for Y ~ Cauchy(c,1) = F_Y(x) - F_Y(-x), the +1/2 terms cancel. After fix all 130 diff cases pass at 1e-12. fsci-stats --lib still 1128 passing (no anchor tests broken because the previous anchors apparently never tested c=0 specifically). Closes [frankenscipy-20ssp].

title: fsci-special::j0(0.0) returns 1.0000000028 instead of exact 1.0
description: crates/fsci-special/src/bessel.rs:2376 — j0_core uses a Numerical Recipes-style rational approximation that returns 57568490574/57568490411 ≈ 1.0000000028310... at ax=0 instead of the exact value J_0(0) = 1.0. SciPy's scipy.special.j0(0.0) returns exactly 1.0 (Cephes special-cases the origin). Symptom: any test or downstream code expecting J_0(0) == 1.0 to f64-eps tolerance silently fails. Discovered while writing the metamorphic test suite for fsci-special. Fix: special-case ax == 0.0 to return 1.0 in j0_core (and audit y0_core / yn / similar approximations for the same pattern, though most have an inherent  factor that makes them exact at 0).

title: [HIGH] fsci-sparse: 5 iterative solvers violate Hardened-mode contract — cg/gmres gate NaN check on check_finite ALONE (ignoring Hardened), while bicg/cgs/bicgstab have ZERO finiteness check at all; direct solve at line 420 correctly uses 'check_finite || mode==Hardened' pattern, so author knew the right code but failed to propagate
description:
Review of crates/fsci-sparse/src/linalg.rs on 2026-04-23.

Five public iterative solvers violate the Hardened-mode fail-closed contract
to varying degrees. Three of them have ZERO input-finiteness validation.

Hardened-mode contract (per fsci-runtime semantics and the workspace pattern):
  In Hardened mode, NaN/Inf inputs MUST fail-closed with audit ledger entry.
  check_finite is a perf knob for Strict mode; Hardened mode should always
  validate regardless of check_finite.

Correct pattern (seen at line 420):
  let must_check = options.check_finite || options.mode == RuntimeMode::Hardened;
  if must_check { ... reject NaN/Inf ... }

Iterative solver validation inventory:

    cg (line 549-656):
        line 567: `if options.check_finite && (NaN check)` -> reject
        HARDENED IGNORED: setting mode=Hardened but check_finite=false
        still allows NaN through.

    gmres (line 790-1000ish):
        line 808: `if options.check_finite && (NaN check)` -> reject
        HARDENED IGNORED: same bug as cg.

    bicg (line 1326-1450ish):
        NO FINITENESS CHECK AT ALL. Validates shape (line 1333),
        b.len (line 1339), x0.len (line 1349). Proceeds directly to
        iteration with whatever NaN/Inf is in a.data() or b.

    cgs (line 1471-1600ish):
        NO FINITENESS CHECK AT ALL. Same structure as bicg.

    bicgstab (line 1617-1700ish):
        NO FINITENESS CHECK AT ALL. Same structure as bicg/cgs.

Consequences:

For cg / gmres in Hardened mode with check_finite=false:
    - NaN matrix entry propagates into residual computation
    - `r_norm / b_norm < tol` becomes `NaN < tol` = false -> never converges
    - Loop runs full max_iter iterations (default n*10)
    - Returns Ok(IterativeSolveResult {
          solution: [NaN; n],
          converged: false,
          residual_norm: NaN
      })
    - No audit ledger entry signals Hardened-mode rejection.

For bicg / cgs / bicgstab (regardless of mode):
    - Same behavior as above. Hardened mode gives NO additional safety.
    - Users migrating from scipy expecting Hardened rejection are silent-
      failure surprised.

Additional context:

- The direct solver path at line 420 correctly implements the OR pattern:
  `options.check_finite || options.mode == RuntimeMode::Hardened`. So the
  crate author KNEW the correct pattern but failed to propagate it to
  iterative solvers. This is a copy-paste-drift bug, not a design oversight.

- Per mhuk (audit ledger not emitted for 9 families), sparse iterative
  solvers are already in the Hardened-audit-gap list. This bead adds
  granularity: sparse direct solve has CORRECT check; sparse iterative
  solve has INCORRECT check.

- IterativeSolveOptions::default() sets check_finite = true (line 525),
  so default callers are safe via the Strict-mode path. Only explicit
  check_finite=false triggers the bug for cg/gmres. For bicg/cgs/bicgstab,
  check_finite is never consulted so default doesn't help.

- For bicg/cgs/bicgstab, the IterativeSolveOptions.check_finite field IS
  still declared but unread. That's a dead-field defect (like 95ld's
  mode: String for cluster cases).

Fix:

1. For cg and gmres: replace
       if options.check_finite && (...)
   with
       if (options.check_finite || options.mode == RuntimeMode::Hardened) && (...)

2. For bicg, cgs, bicgstab: add the same finiteness guard as cg, with
   the OR-Hardened gate:
       let must_check = options.check_finite || options.mode == RuntimeMode::Hardened;
       if must_check && (a.data().iter().any(|v| !v.is_finite())
                         || b.iter().any(|v| !v.is_finite())) {
           return Err(SparseError::NonFiniteInput { ... });
       }

3. Consider extracting a shared `validate_iterative_inputs(a, b, x0, &options)`
   helper and calling it from each of the 5 solvers. Eliminates drift.

4. Add regression tests: construct a NaN-containing CSR matrix, call each
   iterative solver with mode=Hardened and check_finite=false. Each should
   return Err(NonFiniteInput). Currently all return Ok with NaN solution.

5. Emit audit-ledger fail-closed entries (addresses mhuk's sparse portion).

Related: mhuk (audit ledger for 9 families), i9vw/y0z8/ljmg/4q0g/48tv/1dpl
(NaN fail-open class), 95ld (mode String dead for cluster/etc), z1vz
(solve_circulant silent singularity masking, no Hardened path). This bead
is the sparse-iterative branch of the Hardened-mode contract defect tree.
close_reason: Added a shared iterative finite-input validator for cg/gmres/bicg/cgs/bicgstab, added Hardened-mode regression tests, and verified with rch cargo test/check/clippy for fsci-sparse.

title: [CRITICAL] correctness: complex_sqrt in fs-special violates principal-branch convention for z with -0 imaginary (sqrt(-1 - 0i) returns +i instead of -i)
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22 found an identical bug in TWO copies of complex_sqrt:

- crates/fsci-special/src/error.rs:450-462
- crates/fsci-special/src/elliptic.rs:1029-1041

Both use:
    let imag = if z.im < 0.0 { -imag_mag } else { imag_mag };

In Rust, `-0.0_f64 < 0.0_f64` returns `false` (IEEE 754: -0 == +0). So for z = (x, -0.0) with x < 0, the imaginary-sign branch is WRONG: imag gets +imag_mag instead of -imag_mag.

Verification:
    buggy: complex_sqrt(-1.0 - 0.0i) = (0, +1.0)   # i.e. +i
    correct: numpy.sqrt(-1-0j) = -1j, cmath.sqrt(complex(-1,-0.0)) = -1j

This violates the principal-branch convention for sqrt along the negative real axis (the branch cut). The C99 / IEEE 754 convention — and what scipy/numpy implement — is:
    sqrt(-x + 0i) = +i*sqrt(x)   (upper half-plane approach)
    sqrt(-x - 0i) = -i*sqrt(x)   (lower half-plane approach)

Our impl collapses both onto the upper-half-plane branch, losing the ±0 distinction.

Impact (cascades through every caller of complex_sqrt):
- lambertw_complex_initial_guess (elliptic.rs:569-588) uses complex_sqrt(branch_delta*2) near z = -1/e. For z = (-1/e - 0i)+ε where ε has negative imag part, the initial guess's sign of imag is flipped → Newton iterates to the wrong branch.
- erfinv_complex_initial_guess (error.rs:375-390) uses complex_sqrt twice in the Winitzki formula. For y = (x, -0) with |x| near 1, the initial guess is conjugated incorrectly.
- complex_gauss_legendre_elliptic_f / _e (elliptic.rs:1043-1058) uses complex_sqrt to compute elliptic integrand. Conjugation symmetry ellipk(m̄) = ellipk(m)* is broken when m has -0 imag.
- Any function downstream of these (lambertw, wrightomega_complex, erfinv, erfcinv, ellipe, ellipk, ellipeinc, ellipkinc) fails the canonical identity f(z̄) = f(z)* when the input carries -0.

Fix:
1. Replace `z.im < 0.0` with `z.im.is_sign_negative()` in BOTH copies:
       let imag = if z.im.is_sign_negative() { -imag_mag } else { imag_mag };

2. Consolidate the two copies into a shared helper crate or a shared module in fsci-special to prevent future divergence.

3. Add regression tests for the principal-branch convention:
       assert_eq!(complex_sqrt(Complex64::new(-1.0, -0.0)), Complex64::new(0.0, -1.0));
       assert_eq!(complex_sqrt(Complex64::new(-1.0,  0.0)), Complex64::new(0.0,  1.0));
       // Conjugation symmetry:
       for sample z with z.im != 0: assert complex_sqrt(z.conj()) == complex_sqrt(z).conj();

Related: frankenscipy-0acr (fuzz EdgeF64 range) and frankenscipy-tmti (new complex fuzz harnesses) — once fixed, these harnesses should include ±0-imag sweeps to catch any re-regression.
close_reason: Fixed signed-zero branch handling in both complex_sqrt copies and added regression coverage

title: fsci-stats: Burr12.pdf and LogLaplace.pdf wrong at x=0 boundary
description: Burr12.pdf and LogLaplace.pdf in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs short-circuit to 0 for x <= 0, but at x=0 the formulas have shape-dependent limits per scipy. Burr12.pdf(0) = c·d·x^(c-1)/(1+x^c)^(d+1) → +∞ for c<1, → d for c==1, → 0 for c>1. LogLaplace.pdf(0) = c/2·x^(c-1) for x<1 → +∞ for c<1, → 1/2 for c==1, → 0 for c>1. Continuation of the BetaDist (9e4bfd9), Arcsine (39431bb), Fisk+Mielke (5ce4906) boundary fix rotation. Add boundary regression tests for both distributions covering all three regimes.
close_reason: [LOW] Fixed Burr12.pdf and LogLaplace.pdf boundary defects at x=0 in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs (continuation of BetaDist 9e4bfd9, Arcsine 39431bb, Fisk+Mielke 5ce4906 rotation). Both short-circuited to 0 for x<=0 missing the shape-dependent limit at x=0. Burr12.pdf(0) = c·d·x^(c-1)/(1+x^c)^(d+1) → +∞ for c<1, → d for c=1, → 0 for c>1. LogLaplace.pdf(0) = (c/2)·x^(c-1) for x<1 → +∞ for c<1, → 1/2 for c=1, → 0 for c>1. New code branches on shape vs 1 at x=0. 2 regression tests pin all three regimes plus outside-support behavior. fsci-stats --lib: 1042 → 1044.

title: [HIGH] correctness: wrightomega_complex_scalar computes via lambertw(exp(z)) — overflows for z.re>~709 and uses wrong branch for arbitrary complex z
description:
Review of crates/fsci-special/src/convenience.rs:2012-2036:

    fn wrightomega_complex_scalar(z: Complex64, mode: RuntimeMode) -> Result<Complex64, SpecialError> {
        ...
        let lambertw = crate::elliptic::lambertw(&SpecialTensor::ComplexScalar(z.exp()), mode)?;
        ...
    }

Two distinct bugs:

1. **Overflow**: `z.exp()` overflows for z.re > ~709 (since exp(709) ≈ f64::MAX). For z = (1000, 1), `exp(z)` returns `Complex(inf, inf*sin(1))`. Then lambertw(inf) → NaN. But wrightomega(z) for large z.re has a clean asymptote: Ω(z) ≈ z - ln(z). Instead of computing asymptotically, our impl returns NaN or wrong values.

2. **Wrong branch**: The identity Ω(z) = W₀(exp(z)) ONLY holds on a specific strip of the principal branch of Ω. Outside that strip — specifically when Im(z) ∈ (-∞, -π] ∪ [π, ∞) — Ω(z) uses OTHER branches of W (not W₀). Our impl uses ONLY W₀ via the principal lambertw call.

Concrete example: For z = (0, 2π), scipy wrightomega(0 + 2πi) gives a specific value (on the closure of the principal strip). But lambertw(exp(0 + 2πi)) = lambertw(1 + 0i) = Ω (a constant ≈ 0.5671), which is Ω(0), NOT Ω(0 + 2πi). These are DIFFERENT values. The current impl returns the wrong one.

3. **Minor**: line 2017 handles only (Inf, 0) for z.re infinite; misses (Inf, y≠0), (-Inf, y), (y, ±Inf). All return complex_nan silently.

Verification (run in scipy):
    from scipy.special import wrightomega
    # Principal strip:
    wrightomega(0 + 0j)     ≈ 0.5671 + 0j    (principal branch Ω(0))
    # Outside principal strip:
    wrightomega(0 + 2*3.14j) ≈ <specific complex value, NOT Ω(0)>
    # Large real:
    wrightomega(1000 + 0j)   ≈ 1000 - ln(1000) = 993.1  (asymptotic)

Fix:
1. Port scipy's actual wrightomega algorithm (Lawrence/Corless/Jeffrey series). It has explicit branch handling and asymptotic forms.
2. At minimum, handle large |z.re| asymptotically: if z.re > ~700, return z - z.ln() with a few Newton refinement steps.
3. Do NOT delegate to lambertw — the branch structure differs.

Impact: all conformance tests that exercise wrightomega in complex domain with large |z| or outside the principal strip are silently wrong. frankenscipy-tmti new harness, when built, will catch this immediately.

Related: jthj (wrightomega_scalar clamp bug), 276r (complex_sqrt branch bug), tmti (new complex fuzz harness).
close_reason: Replaced lambertw(exp(z)) shortcut with direct omega solve for overflow-safe complex branch handling

title: fsci-stats: Lomax missed in vngzr entropy fix despite clean closed form
description: REVIEW MODE finding cross-reviewing 0433c65/vngzr. Lomax was in the missing-entropy list but skipped in the batch. Closed form for unit-scale Lomax(c): H = 1 + 1/c − ln(c). Derivation: pdf = c/(1+x)^(c+1) for x ≥ 0; -∫ pdf log pdf dx = -log(c) + (c+1)·E[log(1+x)]; E[log(1+x)] = 1/c via the substitution u = 1+x; so H = -log(c) + (c+1)/c = 1 + 1/c - log(c). Will add the entropy override and pin the closed form.
close_reason: Cross-reviewing 0433c65/vngzr found Lomax was in the missing-entropy list but skipped in the batch. Added closed-form H(Lomax(c)) = 1 + 1/c − ln(c). Derivation: pdf = c/(1+x)^(c+1) for x ≥ 0; E[log(1+x)] = 1/c via the substitution u=1+x; so H = -log(c) + (c+1)·(1/c) = 1 + 1/c - log(c). Sanity: c=1 (exponential limit) → H = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2 (matches Exponential(λ=1) entropy). Regression test pins the formula at c=2 and c=1. fsci-stats --lib: 1025 → 1026 passing.

Will pin: cdf at loc+scale/2 = erfc(1); cdf at loc+scale = erfc(1/√2); ppf↔cdf roundtrip; mean=∞ and var=∞ (heavy-tail divergence); pdf(loc) = 0 (boundary); pdf integrates over a finite x range.

title: fsci-stats: pin BetaPrime ≡ Beta(X/(1+X)) metamorphic identity
description: Pin the textbook metamorphic identity BetaPrime(α,β) ≡ Beta(α,β) under Y = X/(1+X). cdf relation: BetaPrime(α,β).cdf(x) = Beta(α,β).cdf(x/(1+x)). pdf via Jacobian: BetaPrime(α,β).pdf(x) = Beta(α,β).pdf(x/(1+x)) / (1+x)². Independent verification across two distinct implementations: BetaPrime.cdf and Beta.cdf both go through regularized_incomplete_beta but with different parameterization; pdf paths differ entirely. Pin pdf+cdf+sf at multiple (a, b, x) triples.
close_reason: Pinned the textbook metamorphic identity BetaPrime(α, β) ≡ Beta(α, β) under Y = X/(1+X) in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs. Test betaprime_is_beta_under_x_over_one_plus_x sweeps 4 (a, b) shape pairs {(0.5, 1.5), (1, 1), (2, 3), (5, 0.7)} × 6 x-values and verifies: pdf relation BetaPrime.pdf(x) = Beta.pdf(x/(1+x))/(1+x)²; cdf/sf relations BetaPrime.cdf(x) = Beta.cdf(x/(1+x)) and BetaPrime.sf(x) = Beta.sf(x/(1+x)). Tol 1e-10. Independent verification — BetaPrime.cdf and Beta.cdf use regularized_incomplete_beta but with different parameterization, and the pdf paths differ entirely (BetaPrime via direct power-form, Beta via ln_beta + powf). fsci-stats --lib: 1045 → 1046.

title: fsci-stats: anderson() applies Stephens correction to statistic instead of critical values; critical values N-independent
description:
fsci-stats anderson() at lib.rs:18596 has TWO defects vs scipy.stats.anderson(dist='norm'):

DEFECT A (statistic):
  fsci line 18645: a2_star = a2 * (1.0 + 0.75/N + 2.25/N^2)
  scipy 1.17.1 _morestats.py: returns RAW A2 as the statistic; the (1+0.75/N+2.25/N^2) factor is applied to the *critical values* (divide), NOT to the statistic.

DEFECT B (critical_values):
  fsci anderson_critical_values_norm() returns FIXED [0.576, 0.656, 0.787, 0.918, 1.092] regardless of N.
  scipy: critical = around(_Avals_norm / (1.0 + 0.75/N + 2.25/N^2), 3)

REPLICATION (N=12 bimodal data [-2.5, -2.4, -2.2, -2.0, -1.8, -1.5, 1.5, 1.8, 2.0, 2.2, 2.4, 2.5]):
  scipy: stat=1.147 crit=[0.520, 0.585, 0.698, 0.810, 0.960]
  fsci:  stat=1.237 crit=[0.576, 0.656, 0.787, 0.918, 1.092]

FIX:
  1. lib.rs:18645 — change `statistic: a2_star` to `statistic: a2` (drop the multiplier).
  2. anderson_critical_values_norm(n) — apply / (1.0 + 0.75/n + 2.25/n^2), then round to 3 decimals; take n into account.

Existing fsci-stats unit tests at lib.rs:30040+ are loose threshold assertions and will continue to pass after the fix.

Surfaced by diff_stats_anderson.rs harness which has been REMOVED until fixed (see frankenscipy-3hunc closure).
notes: Fixed in same tick. Two changes to lib.rs:18596+ region: (1) drop the (1+0.75/N+2.25/N^2) multiplier on the statistic; statistic now returns raw A^2. (2) anderson_critical_values_norm(n) now applies the n-dependent scale + 3-decimal rounding using scipy 1.17's refined Avals_norm table [0.561, 0.631, 0.752, 0.873, 1.035] (replacing the historical Stephens 1974 [0.576, 0.656, 0.787, 0.918, 1.092]). diff_stats_anderson.rs (24 cases) ships green; existing 7 fsci-stats anderson unit tests still pass.

title: [interpolate] BSpline + make_interp_spline foundation
description:
## Goal
Implement the BSpline class and make_interp_spline factory — the foundation for all modern scipy.interpolate functionality. SciPy deprecated interp1d in favor of BSpline-based APIs.

## What to implement
- `BSpline` struct: stores knots t, coefficients c, degree k. Methods: `__call__(x)` (evaluate), `derivative(nu)`, `antiderivative(nu)`, `integrate(a, b)`.
- `make_interp_spline(x, y, k=3)`: construct interpolating BSpline from data points. Solves banded linear system for coefficients.
- `make_lsq_spline(x, y, t, k=3)`: least-squares BSpline with given knots.

## Why this matters
BSpline is the backbone of scipy.interpolate. CubicSpline, UnivariateSpline, and 2D splines all use B-splines internally. Without this, we cannot build the rest of the interpolation stack.

## Implementation approach
- B-spline basis evaluation via de Boor recursion (Cox-de Boor algorithm)
- Knot insertion for make_interp_spline: not-a-knot BCs by default
- Banded matrix solve for coefficient computation (reuse fsci-linalg solve_banded)
- Store in crates/fsci-interpolate/src/bspline.rs

## Testing requirements
- Unit tests: BSpline evaluation at knots, between knots, outside domain
- Interpolation: make_interp_spline reproduces polynomial data exactly for degree <= k
- Derivative: d/dx of cubic spline matches analytic derivative of test functions
- Integration: integrate() matches quad() for simple polynomials
- Edge cases: k=0 (piecewise constant), k=1 (linear), single-segment spline
- Round-trip: make_interp_spline then evaluate at original x recovers y
- At least 12 tests with descriptive names and tolerance documentation
close_reason: Implemented BSpline, make_interp_spline, make_lsq_spline with derivative/antiderivative/integrate. 15 new tests, 43 total.

Will port pub struct Cosine implementing ContinuousDistribution and add reference tests pinning pdf(0)=1/π, cdf(0)=0.5, cdf(π)=1, cdf(-π)=0, plus the analytic mean and variance values.

Shipped: bisection-based ppf override on CosineDistribution + 4 reference tests. fsci-stats now 949 tests (+4).

title: [signal] Chebyshev, Elliptic, Bessel IIR filter design
description:
## Goal
Implement the remaining IIR filter design functions beyond Butterworth, AND add bandpass/bandstop support to the filter design infrastructure. Currently we only support lowpass and highpass.

## What to implement
**New filter types (analog prototypes):**
- `cheby1(N, rp, Wn)`: Chebyshev Type I (equiripple passband, monotonic stopband). rp = max passband ripple in dB.
- `cheby2(N, rs, Wn)`: Chebyshev Type II (monotonic passband, equiripple stopband). rs = min stopband attenuation in dB.
- `ellip(N, rp, rs, Wn)`: Elliptic/Cauer filter (equiripple both bands). Sharpest transition for given order but has ripple everywhere.
- `bessel(N, Wn)`: Bessel/Thomson filter — maximally flat group delay (best for pulse-shape preservation).
- `iirfilter(N, Wn, btype, ftype)`: general IIR filter design dispatcher.

**Bandpass/bandstop support (extends ALL filter design functions including existing butter):**
- Add `FilterType::Bandpass` and `FilterType::Bandstop` variants
- Implement lowpass-to-bandpass frequency transformation: s → (s² + ω₀²)/(s·BW) where ω₀ = √(ω_low·ω_high) and BW = ω_high - ω_low
- Implement lowpass-to-bandstop transformation: s → (s·BW)/(s² + ω₀²)
- These double the filter order (N poles → 2N poles)
- Update Wn to accept 2-element array [low, high] for bandpass/bandstop

## Why this matters
Different applications need different filter characteristics. Audio often uses Butterworth, comms uses Chebyshev, control uses Bessel. Bandpass filters are essential for isolating frequency bands (radio tuning, EEG band extraction). Without bandpass/bandstop, users cannot do basic frequency-band isolation.

## Implementation approach
- All follow the same pattern as butter(): compute analog prototype poles → frequency transform → bilinear transform
- Chebyshev I: poles on ellipse. ε = √(10^{rp/10}-1), poles at sinh(asinh(1/ε)/N)*sin(θ_k) + j*cosh(asinh(1/ε)/N)*cos(θ_k)
- Chebyshev II: inverse of Type I (zeros on imaginary axis at ±j/cos(θ_k))
- Elliptic: requires complete elliptic integrals (use fsci-special ellipk/ellipe/ellipj) for pole/zero computation via Landen transformation
- Bessel: poles from reverse Bessel polynomial B_N(s). Use recursion: B_0=1, B_1=s+1, B_{n+1}=(2n+1)B_n + s²B_{n-1}

## Dependencies
- Extends existing butter() pattern in fsci-signal
- Elliptic filter depends on fsci-special elliptic integrals (ellipk, ellipe)
- Should output SOS form via filter conversions bead (preferred for stability)

## Testing requirements
- Chebyshev I: passband ripple ≤ rp dB, verify at cutoff frequency
- Chebyshev II: stopband attenuation ≥ rs dB
- Elliptic: both passband ripple and stopband attenuation meet specs
- Bessel: group delay is approximately flat in passband (verify via finite-difference phase derivative)
- Bandpass butter: pass frequencies between [wn1, wn2], reject outside
- Bandstop butter: reject frequencies between [wn1, wn2], pass outside
- Order doubling: bandpass butter(2, [0.2, 0.4]) produces order-4 filter
- iirfilter dispatcher: ftype="butter" matches butter(), ftype="cheby1" matches cheby1()
- At least 14 tests (2 per filter type + 4 for bandpass/bandstop)
close_reason: Implemented elliptic (Cauer) IIR filter design with pole/zero computation, iirfilter dispatch, and 4 tests. All IIR filter families now complete: Butterworth, Chebyshev I/II, Bessel, Elliptic with lowpass/highpass/bandpass/bandstop support.

title: fsci-fft: hfft and ihfft diverge from scipy — element ordering/conjugate convention
description: fsci_fft::hfft and ihfft both diverge from scipy.fft equivalents on probe inputs. hfft([(1+2j), (3+4j), (5+6j), (7+8j)]) returns [24, -25.32, 3.46, -2, -3.46, 9.32] vs scipy's [24, 9.32, -3.46, -2, 3.46, -25.32] — looks like a time-reversal modulo the DC bin. ihfft sign of the imaginary part is flipped vs scipy: fsci returns (-0.5, +0.866) where scipy returns (-0.5, -0.866). The DC and Nyquist components (which are real) match. Likely fsci computes the conjugate variant — scipy.fft.ihfft is defined as rfft(x).conj()/n; fsci may be returning just rfft(x)/n. Investigate both fsci_fft::hfft and ihfft to align with scipy's sign convention.

title: [HIGH] conformance: scipy_special_oracle.py has no per-function tolerance contract — Rust comparator is on its own
description:
Review-mode audit of scipy_special_oracle.py emission (lines 211-234):

The oracle emits bit-exact float values with no tolerance hint. The schema is:
    { case_id, status, result_kind, result: {value|values}, error }

No 'tolerance' / 'abs_tol' / 'rel_tol' / 'regime' fields. The Rust conformance runner (crates/fsci-conformance/tests/e2e_special.rs + evidence_p2c006.rs) must invent tolerances per case. This has two failure modes:
- Tolerance too tight: oracle emits a value near epsilon of representable precision (e.g., gammainc(huge_a, tiny_x) ≈ 0), Rust impl is within ulp budget but diverges by 1 ulp, runner reports mismatch.
- Tolerance too loose: covers real bugs (e.g., branch-cut error of ~1e-4 looks like noise).

What SciPy does upstream: its test suites ship per-function 'rtol' values (see scipy/special/tests/test_data.py). For complex-domain and near-singularity cases, tolerances are explicit (1e-10 for gamma near poles, 1e-13 for erf, etc.).

Ask:
1. Extend the oracle output schema with a 'tolerance' block per case:
     "tolerance": { "abs": 1e-12, "rel": 1e-10, "regime": "near_pole" | "tail" | "bulk" }
2. Populate per-function defaults from a constants table seeded from scipy's own rtol declarations. Over-ride per-case when the fixture provides an explicit tol.
3. Update Rust comparators (crates/fsci-conformance/src/comparison.rs or equivalent) to consume the tolerance block instead of hard-coding constants.
4. Document in DISCREPANCIES.md any function where the Rust impl must use a looser tol than scipy's (with the reason).

This also unblocks frankenscipy-n3au (CI wiring) since a per-case tolerance makes fail/pass decisions deterministic across workers.
close_reason: Special differential currently compares against fixture expectations directly; there is no live special-oracle tolerance consumer to extend

title: fsci-signal: correlation_lags(Same) panics when in2=0 and in1 odd
description: correlation_lags(in1, in2, Same) in crates/fsci-signal/src/lib.rs panics when in2=0 and in1 is odd (and similar edge cases). Computation: full = arange(-(in2-1)..in1), mid = full.len()/2, lag_bound = in1/2. For odd in1 the slice is full[(mid-lag_bound)..(mid+lag_bound+1)]. For (in1=5, in2=0): full=[1,2,3,4], mid=2, lag_bound=2, slice = full[0..5] — out of bounds → panic. scipy's correlation_lags clamps to the array size and returns [1,2,3,4]. Fix: clamp the upper slice index to full.len(). Add regression test for the (in1>0, in2=0) and odd-in1 corner. Found via REVIEW MODE cross-review of port 28244b7.
close_reason: [LOW] REVIEW MODE finding in correlation_lags port (28244b7): when in2=0 and in1 is odd (e.g., (5, 0) or (3, 0)), the Same-mode slice upper bound exceeded full.len() and Rust panicked. scipy.signal.correlation_lags handles this via numpy's lazy slicing (returns the truncated tail), so the in1=5, in2=0 case returns [1, 2, 3, 4] in scipy. Fix: clamp the slice indices in Same mode to full.len() via .min(n) and saturating_add. Also added two REVIEW MODE regression tests confirming N>D behavior for lp2bp and lp2bs (which scipy handles cleanly — fsci already matches; the tests lock that contract in). 3 new tests: (1) lp2bp_ba_handles_n_greater_than_d; (2) lp2bs_ba_handles_n_greater_than_d; (3) correlation_lags_handles_zero_in2. fsci-signal --lib: 444 → 447.

title: [special] Orthogonal polynomial roots + quadrature weights: roots_legendre, roots_chebyt, etc.
description:
## Goal
Implement root-finding and Gauss quadrature weight computation for orthogonal polynomials. These are essential for high-accuracy numerical integration.

## What to implement
- `roots_legendre(n)` → (x, w): nodes and weights for Gauss-Legendre quadrature on [-1,1]
- `roots_chebyt(n)` → (x, w): Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature (closed-form: x_k = cos(π(2k+1)/(2n)))
- `roots_hermite(n)` → (x, w): Gauss-Hermite quadrature (for ∫f(x)e^{-x²}dx)
- `roots_laguerre(n)` → (x, w): Gauss-Laguerre quadrature (for ∫f(x)e^{-x}dx on [0,∞))
- `roots_jacobi(n, alpha, beta)` → (x, w): general Gauss-Jacobi quadrature

## Why this matters
Gauss quadrature with n points integrates polynomials of degree 2n-1 exactly. This is the gold standard for numerical integration accuracy. roots_legendre is used by any high-accuracy quadrature code.

## Implementation approach
- Golub-Welsch algorithm: construct symmetric tridiagonal matrix from recurrence coefficients, then compute eigenvalues (= roots) and eigenvectors (→ weights). Uses fsci-linalg eigh_tridiagonal or direct tridiagonal eigensolver.
- Chebyshev roots have closed-form expressions (no eigenvalue solve needed)

## Dependencies
- Depends on eval_* polynomial functions for verification
- Uses fsci-linalg eigenvalue routines

## Testing requirements
- roots_legendre: nodes are in (-1,1), weights sum to 2, symmetric about 0
- Quadrature accuracy: ∫x^k dx from -1 to 1 using Gauss-Legendre with n points exact for k < 2n
- Chebyshev: roots match closed-form cos() values
- Hermite: ∫x^{2k} e^{-x²} dx via Gauss-Hermite matches Γ((2k+1)/2)
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Completed

title: fsci-stats: ExponWeibull.pdf wrong at x=0 boundary
description: ExponWeibull.pdf in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:20122 short-circuits to 0 for x<=0, missing the shape-dependent limit at x=0. Formula: a·c·(1-exp(-x^c))^(a-1)·exp(-x^c)·x^(c-1). As x→0+: (1-exp(-x^c)) ~ x^c, so the product behaves as a·c·x^(c·a - 1). Limit: c·a < 1 → +∞; c·a == 1 → a·c (= 1 when both=1); c·a > 1 → 0. scipy.stats.exponweib agrees on the clean cases (returns inf for a=c=0.5; 1 for a=c=1; 0 for a=c=2). REVIEW MODE finding generalizing the boundary-pdf rotation.
close_reason: [LOW] REVIEW MODE finding generalizing the boundary-pdf rotation: ExponWeibull.pdf in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs short-circuited to 0 for x<=0, missing the shape-dependent limit at x=0. Mathematical limit: as x→0+, (1-exp(-x^c)) ~ x^c so the formula a·c·(1-exp(-x^c))^(a-1)·exp(-x^c)·x^(c-1) reduces to a·c·x^(c·a-1). Limit governed by c·a: <1→+∞, =1→a·c (=1 for a=c=1 case), >1→0. New code branches on c·a vs 1 at x=0. scipy.stats.exponweib agrees on the clean cases: pdf(0; 1, 1)=1, pdf(0; 0.5, 0.5)=inf, pdf(0; 2, 2)=0. Note scipy returns nan for some mixed regimes (a=3,c=0.5 etc.) where the mathematical limit is well-defined; fsci is more correct than scipy in those corners. Regression test pins all three c·a regimes plus outside-support. fsci-stats --lib: 1044 → 1045.

title: fsci-stats: IrwinHall.cdf can drift outside [0, 1] for large n — clamp
description: IrwinHall.cdf can return values slightly outside [0, 1] for large n due to floating-point error in the alternating-sign sum (1/n!)·Σ(-1)^k·C(n,k)·(x-k)^n. Concrete fuzz finding (frankenscipy-mtg5c): IrwinHall(19).cdf(17.1) = 1.0000000037. Same defect class as SkewNorm.cdf (frankenscipy-r7dbb). Fix: clamp the cdf result (and pdf) to valid range. Found via fuzz harness.
close_reason: Fixed via three changes in IrwinHall (commit shipped with fuzz harness frankenscipy-mtg5c): cdf clamps to [0, 1] (drift up to 4e-9 outside for n=19 alternating-sum fp accumulation); pdf evaluates on the symmetry-mirrored x = min(x, n-x) for the better-conditioned half; ppf bracket now uses full support [0, n] instead of a too-narrow ±4σ band. Regression test irwinhall_cdf_clamps_for_large_n sweeps n ∈ {15, 17, 19, 20} × 21 x-values asserting cdf ∈ [0, 1] and pdf finite ≥ 0.

title: fsci-stats: brunnermunzel_alternative one-sided pvalues are inverted vs scipy
description:
fsci brunnermunzel_alternative at lib.rs:20384 calls student_t_alternative_pvalue(w, ...) with the standard t-test convention: 'less' → cdf(w), 'greater' → sf(w). But scipy.stats.brunnermunzel uses the inverted convention because W > 0 corresponds to mean_ry > mean_rx (y stochastically greater than x):
  scipy 'less' (H1: x < y)    → sf(w)   → small for positive w
  scipy 'greater' (H1: x > y) → cdf(w)  → large for positive w

REPLICATION (x=1..10, y=5..14, w≈3.43):
  scipy 'less' pvalue=0.0015,    fsci pvalue=0.9985 (diff 0.997)
  scipy 'greater' pvalue=0.9985, fsci pvalue=0.0015 (diff 0.997)

Two-sided pvalues match (sf(|w|) is symmetric).

FIX: at lib.rs:20384, before calling student_t_alternative_pvalue,
swap 'less' ↔ 'greater':
  let scipy_alt = match alternative {
      "less" => "greater",
      "greater" => "less",
      other => other,
  };

This preserves the standard t-test helper for other callers.
notes: Fixed in same tick. lib.rs:20384 swap 'less' ↔ 'greater' before passing to student_t_alternative_pvalue, since scipy's brunnermunzel uses inverted alternative semantics from the standard t-test (W > 0 means mean_ry > mean_rx). Updated unit test test_brunnermunzel_alternative to assert the corrected scipy-aligned semantics. All 4 brunnermunzel unit tests pass; diff_stats_brunnermunzel.rs (24 cases × 4 fixtures × 3 alts × 2 arms) ships green.

title: [HIGH] stats: shapiro_normal conformance case fails vs scipy oracle — fsci returns 0.9916 vs scipy 0.9780 (5% divergence, ~50000x rtol)
description:
Ironic finding on 2026-04-22: during pass-10 review-mode bead creation, cargo tests triggered by the rch hook surfaced the FIRST real conformance divergence caught by the newly-landed scipy_stats_oracle.py (cpgl implementation).

Failing case from differential_test_stats_fixture (crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:12988):

    case_id: 'shapiro_normal'
    expected (scipy.stats.shapiro statistic): 0.9780016294384768
    actual (fsci-stats shapiro statistic):    0.991567124662354
    max_diff: 0.05070328095208054
    tolerance: atol=1e-6, rtol=1e-6  (diff is ~50,000x tolerance)
    oracle_status: Available
    verdict: FAIL

Context: the shapiro test statistic W is scipy.stats.shapiro-specific and ranges in [0, 1]. 0.978 vs 0.992 is a real numerical difference, not a formatting issue. The p-value match wasn't shown in the log but the statistic alone already flips the test.

The scipy_stats_oracle.py landed today (14:01 UTC) as an implementation of frankenscipy-cpgl. Running differential tests against it reveals that fsci-stats::shapiro diverges from scipy.stats.shapiro on at least one sample input (named 'shapiro_normal', likely a normally-distributed sample).

Also: differential_stats_quota_and_structured_logs (line 13053) panicked with left=1, right=0 — likely asserting fail_count == 0 at line 12672 pattern. Same root cause.

Note: this bug was INVISIBLE until scipy_stats_oracle.py existed. Per ivg5, prior to today the differential_test_stats_fixture was self-checking against hand-typed fixture values; someone had hand-typed 0.9916 (matching the Rust impl) and the test passed. When the oracle landed and was invoked (via the resolve_differential_oracle_config path in run_differential_stats at 8055), it replaced the hand-typed value with scipy's 0.9780 and the mismatch was exposed.

Ask:
1. Investigate fsci-stats::shapiro implementation. scipy.stats.shapiro uses the Royston (1995) algorithm with specific polynomial coefficients for the W statistic. Check the coefficients against scipy source (scipy/stats/_morestats.py / _stats_py.py shapiro implementation).
2. Identify the sample data for 'shapiro_normal' case and reproduce the divergence locally.
3. Add a regression test in crates/fsci-stats/src/ that asserts a specific shapiro output for a canonical sample, matched against scipy.stats.shapiro.
4. Update fixture case 'shapiro_normal' expected value ONLY if the Rust impl is determined to be correct after reconciling with scipy source.

This validates the value of scipy-backed oracles: one oracle discovered a real bug in its first working run. Related: ivg5 (when oracles are missing, bugs stay hidden), cpgl (stats oracle now landed), p3be/pqka/m9jp (other oracle correctness issues that make similar findings possible).
close_reason: Replaced placeholder Shapiro approximation with SciPy-aligned swilk translation and exact canonical regression

title: fsci-linalg: helmert returns (n, n) but scipy default is (n-1, n)

Our impl matches scipy's full=True only. No production callers (only the d1jml regression tests I just shipped — would need updating). Will: (1) introduce helmert_full(n) returning (n, n); (2) change helmert(n) to return scipy's default (n-1, n) submatrix; (3) update the d1jml tests to use helmert_full or adjust their indexing.
close_reason: Shipped helmert parity fix: helmert(n) now returns scipy's default (n-1, n) submatrix (full=False). Added pub fn helmert_full(n) returning the (n, n) form (full=True). Updated 3 prior tests (renamed _ → _full where they assumed n-row output) plus added helmert_default_excludes_first_row_per_scipy verifying the new shape and the relationship helmert(n) = helmert_full(n)[1..]. fsci-linalg 298 lib + 59 metamorphic, all green.

title: fsci-stats: riemann_zeta truncation at k=10000 yields large tail error for small a
description: Surfaced by diff_stats_zipf (frankenscipy-54h9n). fsci's riemann_zeta in lib.rs caps the series sum at k=10000 with relative-tolerance criterion 1e-15. For slow-convergent a near 1 the tail integral dominates: a=1.1 yields ~37% error (abs_diff up to 0.376 vs scipy.zeta), a=1.5 yields ~3e-3, a=2 yields ~4e-5, a=3 yields ~3.5e-9. Fix candidates: (a) raise the loop cap to 1e6 with adaptive Euler-Maclaurin tail correction; (b) replace with scipy-style hurwitzeta + identity ζ(s) = sum_{k=1}^N k^{-s} + N^{1-s}/(s-1) - N^{-s}/2 + … . The defect is real but only matters at a≤3; the standing diff harness for stats.zipf restricts to a≥4 to bypass this.
notes: Expanded scope 2026-05-07i: fsci-special's hurwitz_zeta(s, a) — separate from the riemann_zeta defect frankenscipy-3u8ze — also has a slow-convergence floor: ~2e-5 abs at s=1.1 (heaviest tail, near divergence), ~5e-7 at s=1.5, ~5e-9 at s=2. Suggests Euler-Maclaurin tail correction is too low order. Same fix path as for riemann_zeta but separate code path; might be cleanest to consolidate via a shared kernel.
close_reason: Fixed via full Euler-Maclaurin (j=4) in hurwitz_zeta; fsci-stats local riemann_zeta now delegates to fsci_special::zeta, and zeta_positive routes through hurwitz_zeta(s, 1.0). Accuracy at s=1.1 lifted from ~37% (riemann sum truncation) and ~1e-5 (hurwitz integral-only tail) and ~5e-11 (zeta j=2) all to ~1e-15. Anchor unit tests in fsci-special/src/lib.rs cover the small-s regime.
