title: fsci-sparse: lgmres infinite loop on identity / eigenvector-friendly systems (k=0 lucky breakdown)
description: lgmres_inner detects 'lucky breakdown' when h[k+1][k] ≈ 0 (the next Arnoldi candidate becomes the zero vector). When this happens at k=0 — e.g. solving Ix = b where r0 is itself the only direction needed — it breaks before incrementing k, so y is empty, z is the zero vector, and the function returns (zeros, false, 0). The outer lgmres loop then adds 0 to total_iter and spins forever (while total_iter < max_iter). lgmres_identity_system and lgmres_diagonally_dominant_converges both hang in cargo test. Fix: solve the k=0 system explicitly (y[0] = g[0]/h[0][0], z = y[0]·v[0]) and report converged=true, since g[0]/h[0][0] reduces the residual to floor.
close_reason: Shipped c30a45ba: lgmres_inner now increments k before breaking on lucky breakdown, so the upper-triangular solve covers the final Krylov dimension. The outer lgmres loop terminates cleanly with converged=true and the correct solution. Previously hung indefinitely on A=I and similar eigenvector-friendly systems. Full fsci-sparse: 275/275 ok in 0.09s.

title: fsci-special: hyp2f1(1, 2, 3, -0.99) returns NaN
description: Surfaced by diff_special_hyp2f1 (frankenscipy-6cbyo). fsci's hyp2f1 returns NaN at z=-0.99 (within the |z|<1 series convergence radius) for (a=1, b=2, c=3). scipy.special.hyp2f1 returns ~0.5288 here. Suggests a truncation-vs-Pfaff-transformation seam triggers a degenerate path. Fix candidates: (a) widen the explicit Pfaff-transform branch threshold; (b) tighten the early-termination criterion in the |z|→1 branch. Diff harness keeps to z=-0.9 to bypass.
close_reason: Fixed by routing z<0 inside unit disk through Pfaff transform (where |z/(z-1)| ≤ 1/2 always) and bumping hyp2f1_series cap to 5000 for the positive |z|→1 case. Anchor tests against scipy.special.hyp2f1 across z ∈ [-0.99, 0.99] all pass at <1e-12 abs.

title: PARITY: scipy.optimize global vs local solver parity benchmark suite
description:
fsci-opt currently has fixture coverage for local solvers (Bfgs, ConjugateGradient, Powell, TrustExact, Brentq, Brenth, Bisect, Ridder, Toms748). Stochastic globals (differential_evolution, basinhopping, dual_annealing, brute) are tracked separately under 9n5j.

This bead is the PARITY contract: pick a small benchmark suite (Rosenbrock-2D, Ackley-2D, Rastrigin-2D, Himmelblau, Sphere) and assert that every scipy.optimize.minimize / minimize_scalar / root variant we expose returns within tolerance of the scipy reference for ALL of them.

Acceptance:
- New fuzz harness p2c003_optimize_global_local_parity that drives both fsci-opt minimize and scipy.optimize.minimize on identical objectives + initial points and asserts result.x within rtol=1e-4 (loose for global solvers, tight for local).
- 12+ fixture cases in FSCI-P2C-003 covering each method x objective combination.
- COVERAGE.md row per method documenting which functions converge globally vs locally.

Related: 9n5j (stochastic seed coverage), okyl (trust-constr KKT parity), rkk2 (TNC/SLSQP/trust_constr unit coverage).
close_reason: Completed in dc91719

title: [HIGH] fsci-io: mmread always allocates a DENSE rows*cols f64 buffer even for sparse Coordinate-format files — a 60-byte Matrix Market file declaring 1M×1M triggers 8 TB allocation; DoS vector from untrusted MM input AND scipy parity break (scipy.io.mmread returns coo_matrix for sparse)
description:
Review of crates/fsci-io/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22.

mmread (line 221-411) parses Matrix Market files but allocates a DENSE rows*cols
f64 buffer regardless of whether the file declares 'coordinate' (sparse) or
'array' (dense) format. For a sparse file declaring a large matrix with few
non-zeros, this produces enormous allocations from tiny inputs.

Concrete reproducer (a legal Matrix Market file):

    %%MatrixMarket matrix coordinate real general
    1000000 1000000 1
    1 1 1.0

Parsing this 60-byte input triggers at line 237:

    let dense_len = checked_matrix_len(rows, cols, ...)?;  // 1e6 * 1e6 = 1e12
    let mut data = vec![0.0; dense_len];                    // 8 TB allocation

checked_matrix_len uses checked_mul which only catches arithmetic OVERFLOW, not
'unreasonably large product.' 1e6 * 1e6 = 1e12 doesn't overflow usize on 64-bit
(usize::MAX is ~1.8e19), so the 8 TB vec![] is attempted. Most machines OOM or
swap-death before completing.

For smaller but still problematic cases:
    rows=100_000 cols=100_000 nnz=1:  80 GB allocation
    rows=1_000_000 cols=10_000 nnz=1:  80 GB allocation

Scope of issue:

1. DoS vector from untrusted MM input. A 60-byte file triggers arbitrary memory
   demand. If fsci-io is used in a service that accepts user-uploaded MM files
   (e.g., a sparse matrix loader for a compute service), a single small upload
   crashes the service.

2. scipy parity break. scipy.io.mmread for a coordinate format returns a COO
   sparse matrix (scipy.sparse.coo_matrix) with only the non-zero entries
   materialized. Our mmread returns an MmMatrix with dense data: Vec<f64>, so
   downstream callers that expected sparse efficiency get dense costs.

3. Silent format coercion. The MmMatrix.info.format field is preserved (tells
   you the file was 'coordinate'), but the data field is ALWAYS dense. A
   reviewer might reasonably assume MmMatrix represents sparse matrices
   sparsely; it doesn't.

4. Interacts with 95ld (integrate mode: String dead) indirectly: here the
   info.format field is stored but doesn't change the internal representation.
   The data layout ignores what info.format claims.

5. mmwrite / mmwrite_sparse have the same architectural assumption in the
   write direction: they take a `data: &[f64]` of length rows*cols, so the
   API forces callers to materialize a dense matrix before writing.

Additional finding near line 237: when info.symmetry is Symmetric / Hermitian /
SkewSymmetric, the loop may ADD values to both (r, c) and (c, r) positions
(lines 319-328). For a truly sparse file in symmetric format, the dense output
doubles the memory cost by materializing both triangles (already implied by
dense format but worth noting).

Fix options (pick one):

1. (Minimal) Add a sanity cap in checked_matrix_len OR in mmread specifically:
       const MAX_MM_DENSE_ELEMENTS: usize = 128 * 1024 * 1024;  // 1 GiB of f64
       if rows * cols > MAX_MM_DENSE_ELEMENTS { return Err(TooLarge); }
   This turns the OOM into an early IoError::TooLarge rejection.

2. (Correct) Introduce a sparse output variant:
       pub enum MmMatrixData { Dense(Vec<f64>), Sparse { indices: Vec<(usize, usize)>, values: Vec<f64> } }
   For info.format == Coordinate, populate the Sparse variant. Matches scipy
   semantics and avoids dense blow-up entirely. MmMatrix.data type becomes
   MmMatrixData.

3. (Migration) Add mmread_sparse() that returns (rows, cols, Vec<(row, col, val)>),
   leaving mmread as the dense loader but with a documented upper bound.

Fix priority: option 1 first (security / DoS mitigation), then option 2 as a
proper API fix.

Regression test:
    let mm = "%%MatrixMarket matrix coordinate real general\n1000000 1000000 1\n1 1 1.0\n";
    assert!(mmread(mm).is_err());  // currently OOMs or succeeds with 8 TB

Related: r5tv (complex field rejection, closed -- same fn), the generic 'API
claims sparse but allocates dense' class is new here. 9vmw (FFT plan cache
Potemkin), 95ld (String mode dead), xjav (contract tables dead) are all
surface-claim-vs-reality bugs; mmread fits the same class at the I/O layer.
close_reason: Added a dense Matrix Market read safety cap for coordinate and array loads plus a hostile 1M x 1M coordinate regression test; validated with rch fsci-io targeted test, package check, and package clippy.

title: fsci-fuzz: robustness oracle for stats.kappa3

Asserts: constructor never panics; pdf finite ≥ 0 inside support, 0 outside; cdf monotone in [0, 1]; cdf(≤0)=0; far-tail cdf approaches 1; ppf round-trip; cdf + sf ≈ 1. Wire into fuzz/Cargo.toml. 15-seed corpus covering small-a / linear-a / large-a regimes.
close_reason: Added fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_kappa3_robustness.rs (Arbitrary a in [0.1, 20], x in [1e-6, 100]) + 15-seed corpus + Cargo.toml entry. Surfaced 2 issues on first run: (1) bad far-tail probe x=1e10 unrealistic for small a (cdf(1e10; a=0.15) is correctly ~0.97 because Kappa3 has heavy tail for small a) -- fixed by scaling probe with 10^(20/a) and tolerance 1e-2 for a<0.5; (2) REAL LIB DEFECT: cdf(66.66; a=19) returned 1.0 + 2 ulp (closed form x*(a+x^a)^(-1/a) drifts above 1 when x^a >> a) -- fixed by clamp(0, 1) in Kappa3.cdf, plus ppf fallback to +inf when 1-q^a is non-positive. 30000 fuzz runs clean post-fix; fsci-stats --lib still 1103 passing; 3 anchor tests still hold.

title: [code-review] [MEDIUM] fsci-ndimage histogram_labels panics for zero bins
description: histogram_labels allocated empty per-label histograms for nbins=0, then continued to compute bin indices and clamp with nbins - 1. With labeled finite input this underflowed and could panic. Fixed by returning empty histograms for nbins=0 and for invalid histogram ranges, with a zero-bin regression.
close_reason: Fixed: zero bins and invalid ranges return empty per-label histograms, with a zero-bin regression.

title: fsci-special: elliprc(x, y<0) returns NaN — scipy supports Cauchy PV
description: Skill: /mock-code-finder (discovery) → fix. crates/fsci-special/src/elliptic.rs:2539 has a deferred-path comment for y<0 that just NaN-rejects: 'y<0 (Cauchy PV path, deferred) → NaN'. scipy.special.elliprc supports y<0 via the Cauchy principal value: RC(x, y) = √(x/(x−y)) · RC(x−y, −y) for y<0, x≥0 (Carlson 1995). This is a real parity gap. Fix: detect y<0 branch in elliprc, recursively call with mapped args, and update the negative test to assert real-valued behavior. The transform leaves x' = x−y ≥ x > 0 and y' = −y > 0 so the recursion lands in the existing positive path with no further specialization needed.
close_reason: Closed parity gap: elliprc now supports y<0 via Carlson's Cauchy-principal-value identity RC(x, y) = √(x/(x−y)) · RC(x−y, −y), which always lands in the existing positive y' = −y > 0 branch. Updated docstring to mark the branch as a real Cauchy PV (not deferred). Replaced the stale 'returns NaN for y<0' assertion with a positive 3-case Cauchy-PV test covering: (1) RC(1, -1) = √(1/2)·arccosh(√2); (2) RC(0, -1) = 0 (degenerate prefactor); (3) RC(3, -1) = √(3/4)·arccosh(2)/√3. fsci-special --lib: 778 → 779 passing.

title: [HIGH] fsci-signal: kaiserord / chirp / DLTI::new all use unguarded 'foo <= 0.0' predicates on f64 parameters (ripple, width, t1, dt) — NaN and Inf both pass validation; extends workspace-wide NaN-fail-open class (i9vw/y0z8/ljmg/4q0g); contrast with fsci-special/fsci-fft/fsci-opt/fsci-cluster which correctly use is_finite/is_nan guards
description:
Review of crates/fsci-signal/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-23, extending the
4q0g/i9vw/y0z8/ljmg NaN-fail-open chain into fsci-signal.

Workspace-wide sweep identified at least FOUR public sites in fsci-signal that
use the unguarded `foo <= 0.0` or `foo < 0.0` predicate on f64 parameters from
user input, all vulnerable to NaN fall-through:

Site 1 -- kaiserord (line 5247-5257):
    pub fn kaiserord(ripple: f64, width: f64) -> Result<(usize, f64), SignalError> {
        if ripple <= 0.0 { return Err(...); }
        if width <= 0.0 || width >= 1.0 { return Err(...); }
        ...
    }
    For NaN ripple: NaN <= 0.0 is false -> not rejected.
    For NaN width: NaN <= 0.0 is false AND NaN >= 1.0 is false -> BOTH branches false,
                   not rejected.
    Downstream: ripple is used in beta = 0.1102 * (a - 8.7) etc. For NaN,
                comparisons at lines 5262-5268 all false, beta = 0.0.
                Then numtaps computation uses width (NaN) -> NaN result silently.

Site 2 -- chirp (line 5617):
    pub fn chirp(t: &[f64], f0: f64, t1: f64, f1: f64, method: ChirpMethod)
                 -> Result<Vec<f64>, SignalError> {
        if t1 <= 0.0 { return Err(...); }
        if method == ChirpMethod::Logarithmic && (f0 <= 0.0 || f1 <= 0.0) { return Err(...); }
        ...
    }
    For NaN t1: not rejected -> line 5631 `k = (f1 - f0) / t1` = NaN -> output all NaN.
    For Inf t1: not rejected -> k = 0 -> output is constant cos(2pi*f0*t), misleading.
    For NaN f0 or f1 (non-Log): not caught at all -> passthrough to computation.

Site 3 -- DLTI::new (line 7825):
    if dt <= 0.0 { return Err("sampling period dt must be positive"); }
    Same pattern. NaN dt and Inf dt pass, stored in self.dt, used in eval_at_freq
    where angle = omega * self.dt -> angle = NaN, cos(NaN) = NaN silently.

Site 4 -- find_peaks_cwt / chirp / other spots in the 86KB lib.rs grep likely
to reveal more. This bead groups the three confirmed sites; a full audit may
find 2-4 more.

Fix is the same pattern as fsci-opt::approx_fprime (line 40):

    if !foo.is_finite() || foo <= 0.0 { return Err(SignalError::InvalidArgument(...)); }

or refuse Inf too where it's not legit:

    if foo.is_nan() { return Err(...); }
    if foo <= 0.0 { return Err(...); }

Regression tests:
    kaiserord(NaN, 0.1)          -> Err
    kaiserord(0.1, NaN)          -> Err
    chirp(&[...], 1.0, NaN, 2.0, Linear)      -> Err
    chirp(&[...], NaN, 1.0, 2.0, Logarithmic) -> Err
    DLTI::new(vec![1.0], vec![1.0], NaN)      -> Err

NaN fail-open chain now spans 5 beads across 3 crates:
  i9vw (fsci-integrate validate_first_step/max_step/tol)
  -> y0z8 (fsci-integrate quad/quad_vec/cubature epsabs/epsrel)
  -> ljmg (fsci-integrate BdfSolver unvalidated + RkSolver is_finite guard)
  -> 4q0g (fsci-ndimage gaussian_filter sigma)
  -> THIS BEAD (fsci-signal kaiserord/chirp/DLTI)

Workspace-wide counter-examples (crates that do it correctly):
  - fsci-special (gamma.rs/beta.rs/elliptic.rs -- is_nan() first, then <= 0.0)
  - fsci-fft (validate_finite_complex/real at 2123-2141)
  - fsci-opt (approx_fprime line 40)
  - fsci-cluster dbscan/mean_shift (line 770/1310 -- !foo.is_finite() || foo <= 0.0)

Recommendation: add a clippy lint (or a regression test that scans source) that
flags `if PARAM <= 0.0` on function parameters of type f64 without a preceding
`PARAM.is_finite()` or `PARAM.is_nan()` check. Prevents future drift.

Related: i9vw, y0z8, ljmg, 4q0g (NaN class), wofb / sf() default (precision
loss), s9tt (stringly-typed errors). All symptoms of 'scipy port treats float
parameters as if they're never NaN/Inf' — a workspace-wide cultural gap.
close_reason: Added finite-argument guards for kaiserord, chirp, and Dlti::new; validated with rch fsci-signal non_finite tests, package check, and package clippy.

title: [signal] FIR filter design: firwin, firwin2, kaiserord
description:
## Goal
Implement FIR (Finite Impulse Response) filter design functions. FIR filters are guaranteed stable and can achieve exact linear phase — essential properties for many applications.

## What to implement (core)
- `firwin(numtaps, cutoff, window, pass_zero)`: window-based FIR filter design. Creates lowpass/highpass/bandpass/bandstop FIR filters by windowing the ideal sinc response.
- `firwin2(numtaps, freq, gain)`: FIR filter with arbitrary frequency response specification via frequency-sampling method.
- `kaiserord(ripple, width)`: estimate Kaiser window parameters (numtaps, beta) for given specifications.

## Stretch goal (if time permits)
- `remez(numtaps, bands, desired)`: Parks-McClellan optimal equiripple FIR filter design via Remez exchange algorithm. This is significantly more complex than firwin (iterative minimax optimization) and can be deferred.

## Why this matters
FIR filters are the default choice when linear phase is needed (audio, biomedical, communications). firwin is one of the top-5 most used scipy.signal functions. The window method is simpler but remez gives optimal (minimax) designs.

## Implementation approach
- firwin: generate ideal lowpass impulse response (sinc function), multiply by window, normalize for unity gain at DC (lowpass) or Nyquist (highpass). For highpass: spectral inversion. For bandpass: difference of two lowpass filters. For bandstop: spectral inversion of bandpass.
- firwin2: evaluate desired frequency response at DFT frequencies, inverse FFT to get impulse response, multiply by window.
- kaiserord: empirical formulas from Kaiser-1974 relating transition width δ and ripple A to (numtaps, beta). A = -20*log10(δ), beta from Kaiser formula.
- Use existing window functions (hann, hamming, blackman, kaiser) and sinc from fsci-special

## Dependencies
- Uses existing window functions from fsci-signal
- Uses sinc from fsci-special/convenience

## Testing requirements
- firwin lowpass: magnitude at cutoff ≈ -6dB, passband flat, stopband attenuated
- firwin highpass: verify passband at high frequencies
- firwin bandpass: two cutoff frequencies respected
- firwin bandstop: notch at center frequency
- firwin2 arbitrary: matches specified magnitude points
- kaiserord: estimated numtaps reasonable for typical specs (ripple=60dB, width=0.1 → numtaps≈37)
- Linear phase: verify impulse response is symmetric (h[n] = h[N-1-n])
- At least 10 tests
close_reason: Implemented firwin (lowpass/highpass/bandpass/bandstop), firwin2 (arbitrary freq response), kaiserord. 12 new tests, 86 total.

title: fsci-stats: cvm_cdf uses asymptotic Bessel series; deviates from scipy at small n
description:
fsci cvm_cdf at lib.rs:17722 returns the asymptotic CDF via the Bessel-K series for ALL n. scipy 1.17 uses a Csörgő-Faraway finite-sample correction at small n that gives noticeably different tail probabilities. Empirical drift on near-normal n=14 sample: scipy_pvalue=0.4856, fsci_pvalue=0.4932, diff=7.6e-3. Statistic itself matches scipy to 1e-15 since it's a closed-form sum.

REPLICATE: see diff_stats_cramervonmises.rs (4 datasets × 2 arms). Statistic arms pass; pvalue arms exceed 1e-9 tolerance.

FIX OPTIONS:
  (a) Port scipy's _cdf_cvm_inf finite-sample correction (Csörgő & Faraway 1996, Table I) — recommended.
  (b) Document the asymptotic precision floor in cvm_cdf docstring and bump cramervonmises tolerance in tests to ~1e-2 for small n.

CURRENT WORKAROUND: diff_stats_cramervonmises.rs uses PVALUE_TOL=1e-2 abs. Tighten once the finite-sample correction lands.
close_reason: Ported scipy's _psi1_mod (Csörgő-Faraway 1996) for finite-n cvm correction. title: [CRITICAL] correctness: complex_jv_scalar and complex_iv_scalar produce INFINITY for negative-integer orders via Γ(v+1) pole instead of using J_{-n}(z) = (-1)^n J_n(z) identity
description:
Review of crates/fsci-special/src/bessel.rs:2501-2578:

Both complex_jv_scalar and complex_iv_scalar compute the series:
    sum = (z/2)^v / Γ(v+1) * Σ terms

via:
    let log_first = v_c * log_half_z - crate::gamma::complex_gammaln(Complex64::new(v + 1.0, 0.0));
    let mut sum = log_first.exp();

For negative-integer v = -n (n ∈ ℕ), this evaluates complex_gammaln(Complex64::new(1 - n, 0.0)) — a Γ pole. Per frankenscipy-d218, complex_gammaln returns (−∞, φ) silently at such poles. Then:
    log_first = v * log(z) - (-∞) = +∞
    sum = exp(+∞) = ∞

But the correct identity for integer v is J_{-n}(z) = (-1)^n * J_n(z) and I_{-n}(z) = I_n(z), both finite for finite z.

Verification (scipy):
    jv(-1, 1+0j) = -0.4401 - 0j  (our impl returns ~∞)
    jv(-2, 1+0j) = +0.1149 + 0j  (our impl returns ~∞)
    iv(-1, 1+0j) =  0.5652 + 0j  (our impl returns ~∞)

Impact:
- ANY complex-domain call to jv/iv with negative-integer order produces INFINITY instead of the correct finite value.
- yv and kv (at 2583 / 2674) may have similar patterns; audit recommended.
- No fuzz target currently exercises this path (order is treated as f64 and the existing harnesses don't pick negative integers specifically).
- Conformance fixture cases with negative-integer orders in complex domain would expose this — but per v2tz, such cases either don't exist or can't be oracled.

Fix:
1. In complex_jv_scalar: if v < 0 and v.fract() == 0.0 (integer), compute as sign * complex_jv_scalar(-v, z) where sign = if (-v as i64) % 2 == 0 { 1 } else { -1 }.
2. In complex_iv_scalar: if v < 0 and v.fract() == 0.0 (integer), just compute complex_iv_scalar(-v, z) — I_v symmetric under v → -v for integer v.
3. Audit complex_yv_scalar (line 2583) and complex_kv_scalar (line 2674) for similar Γ-pole hazards.
4. Add regression tests:
       assert_close(complex_jv_scalar(-1.0, C(1,0)), C(-0.44005, 0), tol=1e-14);
       assert_close(complex_jv_scalar(-2.0, C(1,0)), C(0.11490, 0), tol=1e-14);
       assert_close(complex_iv_scalar(-1.0, C(1,0)), C(0.56516, 0), tol=1e-14);

Related: d218 (complex_gammaln pole bug — underlying cause), tmti (new bessel_base complex fuzz harness catches this immediately), v2tz (oracle gap means this would slip past conformance).
close_reason: Fixed negative-integer-order handling in complex_jv_scalar/complex_iv_scalar and added regression coverage

title: [signal] Filter conversions: tf2zpk, zpk2tf, tf2sos, sos2tf, zpk2sos, sos2zpk
description:
## Goal
Implement the filter representation conversion functions. These are the plumbing that connects all filter design and analysis functions. SciPy users routinely convert between transfer function (b,a), zero-pole-gain (z,p,k), and second-order sections (sos) forms.

## What to implement
- `tf2zpk(b, a)` → (zeros, poles, gain): find roots of numerator/denominator polynomials
- `zpk2tf(z, p, k)` → (b, a): expand zeros/poles into polynomial coefficients
- `tf2sos(b, a)` → sos: convert to second-order sections (cascade of biquads) — this is the numerically preferred form
- `sos2tf(sos)` → (b, a): expand SOS back to transfer function
- `zpk2sos(z, p, k)` → sos: pair zeros/poles into biquad sections
- `sos2zpk(sos)` → (z, p, k): extract zeros/poles from SOS
- `SosSection` struct: [b0, b1, b2, a0, a1, a2] for one biquad section

## Why this matters
SOS form is essential for numerical stability of high-order filters. Without tf2sos, users cannot safely implement filters above order ~8. Every signal processing textbook recommends SOS form for implementation.

## Implementation approach
- tf2zpk: use companion matrix eigenvalue approach (polynomial root finding via eigenvalues of companion matrix)
- zpk2tf: use polynomial multiplication from roots
- zpk2sos: pair complex conjugate poles, pair with nearest zeros, sort by distance from unit circle for numerical stability
- SOS is a Kx6 array where each row is [b0, b1, b2, 1, a1, a2]

## Testing requirements
- Roundtrip: tf → zpk → tf, tf → sos → tf, zpk → sos → zpk (all within tolerance)
- Known filter: butter(4, 0.3) should give same sos as SciPy reference values
- Stability: tf2sos for high-order filter (order 10+) should preserve magnitude response
- Edge: first-order filter (odd-order sections), purely real zeros
- At least 10 tests
close_reason: Implemented tf2zpk, zpk2tf, tf2sos, sos2tf, zpk2sos, sos2zpk with companion matrix eigenvalue root finding and proper SOS pairing. 11 new tests, 60 total. Unblocks freqz, sosfilt, and advanced IIR beads.

title: [signal] iirnotch and iirpeak: second-order notch/peak filters
description:
## Goal
Implement notch and peak IIR filters used for removing/isolating specific frequencies (e.g. 60Hz hum removal).

## What to implement
- `iirnotch(w0, Q)`: Design second-order notch filter. w0 = frequency to remove (normalized 0-1), Q = quality factor.
- `iirpeak(w0, Q)`: Design second-order peaking filter. Same parameters but boosts instead of rejecting.

## Implementation
- Notch: H(z) = (1 - 2cos(ω₀)z⁻¹ + z⁻²) / (1 - 2r·cos(ω₀)z⁻¹ + r²z⁻²) where r = 1 - BW/2
- Peak: same structure with zeros and poles swapped
- BW = ω₀/Q (bandwidth)

## Testing
- Notch at w0: response at w0 should be near zero
- Peak at w0: response at w0 should be maximum
- At least 4 tests
close_reason: Implemented iirnotch and iirpeak second-order IIR notch/peak filters. Fixed cos(2w0) → cos(w0) bug during testing. 4 tests, all 165 signal tests passing.

title: [HIGH] fsci-ndimage: gaussian_filter + gaussian_filter_nd use unguarded 'sigma <= 0.0' predicate — NaN sigma returns array-of-NaN silently; Inf sigma triggers vec![0.0; usize::MAX] = 147 EB allocation / OOM; empirically verified NaN and Inf both bypass the reject branch; same class as i9vw/y0z8/ljmg
description:
Review of crates/fsci-ndimage/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-23.

Two public Gaussian filter functions use the unguarded `sigma <= 0.0` predicate
that NaN and Inf silently pass, producing OOM or silently-corrupted output:

Site 1 -- gaussian_filter (line 683-721):

    pub fn gaussian_filter(input: &NdArray, sigma: f64, mode, cval) -> Result<...> {
        if sigma <= 0.0 {
            return Err(...);
        }
        ...
        let radius = (4.0 * sigma).ceil() as usize;
        let ksize = 2 * radius + 1;
        let mut kernel_1d = vec![0.0; ksize];
        ...
    }

Site 2 -- gaussian_filter_nd (or similar, line 2177):

    for (axis, &sigma) in sigmas.iter().enumerate() {
        if sigma <= 0.0 {
            continue;  // skip this axis
        }
        let radius = (4.0 * sigma).ceil() as usize;
        let ksize = 2 * radius + 1;
        let mut kernel_1d = vec![0.0; ksize];
        ...
    }

Empirically verified (rustc probe, 2026-04-23):

    NaN <= 0.0 = false   (NaN sigma NOT rejected)
    Inf <= 0.0 = false   (Inf sigma NOT rejected)

Consequences:

NaN sigma path:
    radius = (4.0 * NaN).ceil() as usize = 0 (NaN saturates to 0 in f64-as-usize cast)
    ksize = 1
    kernel = [exp(-0 / (2 * NaN^2))] = [exp(-0/NaN)] = [exp(NaN)] = [NaN]
    total = NaN
    kernel /= total = [NaN]
    convolve input with [NaN] -> output is all NaN
    Returns Ok(output) with silently-corrupted NaN array.

Inf sigma path:
    radius = (4.0 * Inf).ceil() as usize = usize::MAX (saturating cast)
    ksize = 2 * usize::MAX + 1
      - debug build: panics at arithmetic overflow
      - release build: wraps to usize::MAX
    vec![0.0; usize::MAX] on release tries to allocate 147 EB
      - immediate OOM on any machine
      - DoS vector from a single f64::INFINITY argument

The fix is the same pattern fsci-opt::approx_fprime uses correctly (line 40):
    if !sigma.is_finite() || sigma <= 0.0 { return Err(...); }

For site 2 (multi-sigma), replace continue with return Err(...) OR explicit
is_finite guard, since NaN/Inf should never silently skip an axis vs. legit
zero (which intentionally skips).

Compounds with the general NaN-fail-open chain (i9vw / y0z8 / ljmg in
integrate). Same architectural failure in ndimage.

Scope:

- gaussian_filter is a heavily-used scipy function; any caller passing a
  user-controlled sigma (CLI arg, config value, API parameter) can trigger
  OOM with sigma=Inf or silent NaN output with sigma=NaN.
- Downstream callers in fsci-signal (via scipy_fft_oracle-like usage) may
  propagate NaN through further signal-processing pipelines, masking the
  root cause.

Fix:

1. Replace both sites with is_finite-first predicate.

2. Add regression tests:
       gaussian_filter(input, f64::NAN, mode, 0.0)      -> Err
       gaussian_filter(input, f64::INFINITY, mode, 0.0) -> Err
       gaussian_filter_nd(input, &[1.0, f64::NAN])      -> Err

3. Do a repo-wide audit for the same `foo <= 0.0` / `foo < 0.0` pattern
   on float parameters — this is the fourth appearance of the class:
   - i9vw (validate_first_step / validate_max_step / validate_tol)
   - y0z8 (quad / quad_vec / cubature epsabs/epsrel)
   - ljmg (RkSolver is_finite guard lumps Inf with NaN)
   - THIS bead (gaussian_filter sigma)

Related: i9vw (validate_* NaN fail-open), y0z8 (quad NaN fail-open), ljmg
(BDF/RK asymmetry), 42z1 (mmread DoS), l15l (procrustes divergence).
Pattern: public API surfaces that accept f64 parameters without
is_finite() guards form a workspace-wide class defect.
close_reason: Added finite sigma guards in gaussian_filter and gaussian_filter_multi_sigma plus non-finite regression tests; validated with rch fsci-ndimage non_finite_sigma tests and package check. Package clippy remains blocked by preexisting fsci-spatial warnings unrelated to this bead.

title: fsci-stats: NoncentralT skewness/kurtosis return NaN for all params; scipy has finite values for df>3 and df>4
description: fsci_stats::NoncentralT::skewness() and kurtosis() unconditionally return f64::NAN. scipy.stats.nct(df, nc).stats(moments='sk') yields finite values whenever df>3 (skew) and df>4 (kurt) via the closed-form raw moments E[T^k] = (df/2)^{k/2} · Γ((df−k)/2)/Γ(df/2) · Σ_{j=0..⌊k/2⌋} C(k, 2j) · nc^{k−2j} · (2j)!/(2^j · j!). Implement these and return NaN only when the moment doesn't exist (df ≤ k).
close_reason: Shipped 94678ff6: closed-form raw-moment NoncentralT::skewness/kurtosis (nct_raw_moment helper computes E[T^k] = (df/2)^{k/2}·Γ((df−k)/2)/Γ(df/2)·M_k(nc)). Matches scipy to ~1e-6 across 3 (df, nc) cases. Returns NaN only at df ≤ 3 (skew) / df ≤ 4 (kurt). Note: scipy.stats.nct(df, 0) special-cases kurt to 1.0; fsci returns 6/(df−4) which matches the central-t limit.

title: [conformance] live SciPy special gamma/beta lacks pole and broadcast coverage
description: The new live_scipy_core ULP suite covers finite scalar gamma, gammaln, rgamma, beta, betaln, betainc, gammainc, and gammaincc cases. Current conformance remains insufficient for poles/negative-integer behavior, sign handling near reflection boundaries, vector broadcasting, complex inputs, and extreme-tail underflow/overflow regimes. Add live SciPy oracle cases with explicit discrepancy records for intentional strict/hardened deviations.

title: extend fsci-linalg with matrix-function metamorphic tests + non-symmetric logm precision finding
description: Added 3 metamorphic relations to crates/fsci-linalg/tests/metamorphic_tests.rs covering expm/logm/sqrtm: expm(0)=I, sqrtm(A)²=A for SPD A via M^T M + I, logm(expm(A))=A for symmetric A. While developing the test suite discovered that the non-symmetric general logm path has weak precision (~1 digit) — filed as bead frankenscipy-cgzb.
close_reason: Added 3 metamorphic relations: MR10 expm(0)=I (matrix exp of zero is identity), MR11 sqrtm(A)²=A for SPD A, MR12 logm(expm(A))=A for symmetric A. While developing this suite discovered non-symmetric logm general path has weak precision (~1 digit error on small ||A||), filed as bead frankenscipy-cgzb (P3, follow-up). All 13 linalg metamorphic tests passing.

title: fsci-signal: vectorstrength does two iter passes + redundant phase computation
description: Skill: /profiling-software-performance. crates/fsci-signal/src/lib.rs:1980 vectorstrength iterates events twice (once for sin_sum, once for cos_sum) and recomputes two_pi·t/period twice per event. Tightening: single-pass loop + sin_cos() (one argument reduction). For N events, saves: 1 iter (was 2 → 1), 1 mul/div per event (was 2), 1 trig argument reduction per event (sin_cos shares it). Equivalence test against the dual-pass naïve form on a 50-event sample.
close_reason: Tightened vectorstrength in fsci-signal/src/lib.rs:1980. Pre-fix: two iter passes (one for sin_sum, one for cos_sum) and the phase angle two_pi*t/period was computed twice per event. After: single-pass loop with sin_cos() so the argument reduction is shared between sin and cos. For N events this saves: 1 full iteration (was 2 → 1), 1 mul/div per event (was 2 → 1), 1 trig argument reduction per event. Equivalence test diffs the optimized form against an explicit naïve dual-pass implementation across 4 periods × 50 events. fsci-signal --lib: 417 → 418 passing.

title: fsci-stats: missing metamorphic Laplace symmetry around loc
description: Skill: /testing-metamorphic. Laplace is symmetric around loc: Laplace(loc, s).cdf(loc+d) + Laplace(loc, s).cdf(loc-d) = 1, equivalently Laplace.cdf(loc+d) = 1 - Laplace.cdf(loc-d). Same for sf with the just-shipped 6uo0s overrides: Laplace.sf(loc+d) + Laplace.sf(loc-d) = 1. The pdf is symmetric: Laplace.pdf(loc+d) = Laplace.pdf(loc-d). No tests pin these. Independent verification of the cdf/sf split: a sign error in either branch of cdf or sf would surface as a mismatch. Will pin pdf/cdf/sf reflection at 4 (loc, scale) tuples × 6 d values.
close_reason: Pinned Laplace's symmetry around loc in fsci-stats/src/lib.rs::laplace_is_symmetric_around_loc. For any (loc, scale) and any d ≥ 0: (1) cdf(loc+d) + cdf(loc-d) = 1; (2) sf(loc+d) + sf(loc-d) = 1; (3) pdf(loc+d) = pdf(loc-d). Tests 4 (loc, scale) tuples × 6 d values × 3 method-relations = 72 implicit assertions. Independent verification of the cdf/sf piecewise z<0 vs z>=0 split (the just-shipped 6uo0s sf override) — a sign error in either branch would surface as a mismatch. fsci-stats --lib: 1003 → 1004 passing.

title: fsci-conformance: scipy diff for stats.zipf

close_reason: diff_stats_zipf shipped: 6 a x 13 k = 156 cases pmf+cdf vs scipy.stats.zipf via subprocess. a restricted to {4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 15} (a≤3 blocked by upstream defect frankenscipy-3u8ze: fsci's riemann_zeta truncates at k=10000 with too-aggressive 1e-15 rel tol, yielding 37% error at a=1.1, 3e-3 at a=1.5, 4e-5 at a=2, 3.5e-9 at a=3). Tol 5e-12 abs absorbs a=4 truncation floor (~3.3e-13).

title: fsci-special: smirnov asymptotic too imprecise at small n (~0.3 abs at n=1)
description: Surfaced by diff_special_ks (frankenscipy-7ps6s). fsci's smirnov uses O(1/n)-corrected asymptotic exp(-2nd²) which lands ~0.05 abs off scipy at n=1, ~0.03 at n=50. scipy uses the exact Birnbaum-Tingey series for small n. Fix: implement Birnbaum-Tingey exact series for n < 1000 (n+1 binomial-like terms), keep the asymptotic for n ≥ 1000.
close_reason: Replaced asymptotic-with-correction with the exact Birnbaum-Tingey series for n<1000, computed in log-space. smirnov now matches scipy to floating-point precision for n ≤ 100 and to ~1e-9 rel for n ≤ 500. Asymptotic remains for n ≥ 1000 per bead scope. Anchor test smirnov_matches_scipy_at_small_n covers 14 cases.

close_reason: Landed 9 metamorphic tests (16 #[test]s after distribution variant expansion): cdf-ppf roundtrip x7 distributions, cdf monotonicity, cdf+sf=1, pearsonr/spearmanr symmetry, pearsonr identical/anti, ttest_ind sign symmetry, ks_2samp swap symmetry, ttest_1samp shift invariance, pdf>=0 and cdf in [0,1]. All passing. No bugs surfaced.

title: fsci-stats: HalfNormal missing skewness/kurtosis (closed forms)
description: skewness = sqrt(2)*(4-pi)/(pi-2)^1.5 ≈ 0.99527, excess kurt = 8(pi-3)/(pi-2)^2 ≈ 0.86918. scipy.stats.halfnorm provides both.
close_reason: Closed-form skewness+kurtosis added for HalfNormal, Rayleigh, Maxwell. All scale-invariant. Verified vs scipy at 1e-12 abs.

title: fsci-stats: port scipy.stats.skewcauchy
description: scipy.stats.skewcauchy(a) is missing in fsci-stats. Closed-form distribution with skewness parameter -1 < a < 1. pdf(x) = 1 / (π·(x²/(a·sign(x)+1)² + 1)). cdf has piecewise atan form. ppf has piecewise tan form. mean/var/skew/kurt all NaN (heavy tails like Cauchy). Reduces to Cauchy at a=0. Add the distribution + 4 anchor tests verified against scipy: pdf/cdf at 0 and ±1 for a=0.3 and a=0.7, plus the a=0 reduction to Cauchy.
close_reason: Ported scipy.stats.skewcauchy(a) to crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs as a new SkewCauchy struct with skewness parameter a ∈ (-1, 1). pdf(x) = 1/(π·(x²/(a·sign(x)+1)² + 1)). cdf has piecewise atan form with split at x=0. ppf has piecewise tan form. mean/var = NaN (heavy tails like Cauchy; no finite moments). Reduces to standard Cauchy at a=0. 4 anchor tests: (1) skewcauchy_pdf_matches_scipy with 10 (a, x) pairs verified against scipy; (2) skewcauchy_cdf_matches_scipy with 9 cases; (3) skewcauchy_at_zero_reduces_to_cauchy comparing against fsci's Cauchy::new(0, 1) at 7 x-values; (4) skewcauchy_ppf_inverts_cdf round-trip across 4 a-values × 5 x-values. Test expectation for (-0.5, 1.0).pdf had a typo (initially 1/(2π) which is the WRONG conjugate value); REVIEW caught it pre-ship — corrected to scipy's actual 1/(5π) = 0.0637. fsci-stats --lib: 1051 → 1055.

title: [HIGH] conformance: scipy_linalg_oracle.py covers 7 of 119 public fsci-linalg operations (5.9%); no complex-matrix cases in fixture + oracle forces dtype=float64
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_linalg_oracle.py and FSCI-P2C-002_linalg_core.json on 2026-04-22:

Oracle dispatcher covers 7 operations:
    solve, solve_triangular, solve_banded, inv, det, lstsq, pinv

fsci-linalg public API (grep -cE '^pub fn ' crates/fsci-linalg/src/lib.rs):
    119 public functions

112 of 119 are NEVER oracled. Notable gaps:
- Decompositions: lu, lu_factor, lu_solve, qr, svd, svdvals, cholesky, cho_factor, cho_solve, cho_solve_banded, solveh_banded, ldl
- Eigenproblems: eig, eigvals, eigh, eigvalsh, eig_banded, eigh_tridiagonal, schur, hessenberg
- Generalized: qz, ordqz, sylvester
- Matrix functions: expm, logm, sqrtm
- CASP flagship paths: solve_with_casp, inv_with_casp, lstsq_with_casp, pinv_with_casp -- the entire conditioning-aware-solver-portfolio code has NO scipy parity check.
- condition_diagnostics, randomized_rcond_estimate

Fixture (FSCI-P2C-002_linalg_core.json) has 53 cases -- all of them use one of the 7 oracled operations. So oracle 'coverage' is 100% at the fixture level, but the fixture itself is deliberately narrow and does not exercise 94% of fsci-linalg.

Separately, every dispatcher path coerces np.asarray(..., dtype=np.float64) -- complex matrices are SILENTLY CAST to real, losing imaginary parts. Verified: 0 of 53 fixture cases have complex-structured inputs, so the coercion has no current impact, but this limits future complex-matrix coverage.

Ask:
1. Extend oracle dispatcher with at minimum: lu, qr, svd, cholesky, eig, eigh, expm (covers the 7 most-used decompositions).
2. Add CASP-path coverage: solve_with_casp needs scipy oracle but also needs the CASP decision to be verifiable -- may need a shadow portfolio in the oracle that replicates the CASP loss matrix logic. File separately if complex.
3. Remove dtype=np.float64 coercion. Accept complex input by letting np.asarray infer dtype, or split into two dispatcher variants (solve_real / solve_complex).
4. Add at least 10 complex-matrix fixture cases to FSCI-P2C-002_linalg_core.json covering: real well-conditioned solve, complex Hermitian eigvals, complex LU, singular complex, etc.

Related: v2tz (special oracle 35% coverage -- same class of illusion), cpgl (no stats oracle), di9p (10 of 15 families have no oracle at all).

title: defect: sproot returns different roots than scipy.sproot for cubic spline of sin
description: fsci_interpolate::sproot returns root list with different length/values than scipy.interpolate.sproot when applied to a cubic spline through y=sin(x) on x=[0,7]. Length mismatch (inf abs_diff in conformance harness). Likely a subinterval scan algorithm difference.

title: SmoothBivariateSpline implementation uses a single global polynomial rather than a piecewise B-spline
description: In crates/fsci-interpolate/src/lib.rs, SmoothBivariateSpline is implemented as a global polynomial least-squares fit across the entire bounding box (up to degree kx, ky). SciPy's SmoothBivariateSpline wraps Dierckx's surfit to construct a true piecewise B-spline with automatically placed internal knots based on the smoothing factor 's'. The current Rust implementation only produces a single polynomial with (kx+1)*(ky+1) coefficients, failing to fit complex scattered data.
close_reason: Completed

title: [stats] Discrete distributions: Binomial, NegBinomial, Geometric, Hypergeometric, Bernoulli
description:
## Goal
Implement the core discrete probability distributions and a DiscreteDistribution trait. Currently we only have Poisson with basic pmf/cdf. Discrete distributions are used everywhere in hypothesis testing, reliability analysis, and combinatorics.

## What to implement
- `DiscreteDistribution` trait: pmf(k), cdf(k), sf(k), ppf(q), mean(), var(), std(), rvs(n)
- `Binomial(n, p)`: number of successes in n trials. PMF: C(n,k)*p^k*(1-p)^(n-k)
- `NegBinomial(n, p)`: number of trials until n successes. PMF involves gamma function.
- `Geometric(p)`: number of trials until first success. PMF: p*(1-p)^(k-1)
- `Hypergeometric(M, n, N)`: draws without replacement. PMF: C(n,k)*C(M-n,N-k)/C(M,N)
- `Bernoulli(p)`: single binary trial (special case of Binomial with n=1)
- Enhance existing `Poisson` to implement the trait

## Why this matters
Binomial is used in A/B testing, clinical trials, and quality control. Hypergeometric is used in Fisher exact test. These are among the most commonly used distributions outside of the continuous ones.

## Testing requirements
- Binomial: PMF sums to 1 over k=0..n, mean=np, var=np(1-p)
- NegBinomial: PMF sums to 1, mean=n/p
- Geometric: memoryless property, mean=1/p
- Hypergeometric: PMF sums to 1, reduces to binomial in limit
- ppf/cdf roundtrip for each distribution
- Edge cases: p=0, p=1, n=0
- At least 12 tests
close_reason: Implemented DiscreteDistribution trait + Binomial, Bernoulli, Geometric, NegBinomial, Hypergeometric. Retrofitted Poisson to implement trait. 20 new tests, 136 total.

title: fsci-stats: port scipy.stats.exponpow
description: scipy.stats.exponpow(b) is missing in fsci-stats. Closed-form distribution on x ≥ 0 with shape b > 0. pdf(x) = b·x^(b-1)·exp(1 + x^b − exp(x^b)). cdf(x) = 1 − exp(1 − exp(x^b)) = -expm1(-expm1(x^b)). sf(x) = exp(1 − exp(x^b)) = exp(-expm1(x^b)). ppf(q) = (log1p(-log1p(-q)))^(1/b). Boundary at x=0 is shape-dependent: b<1 → ∞, b=1 → 1, b>1 → 0. Add 4 anchor tests verified against scipy: pdf at multiple (b, x), cdf, ppf round-trip, boundary regimes. Note: NOT to be confused with the generalized-normal a.k.a. gennorm distribution.
close_reason: Ported scipy.stats.exponpow(b) to crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs as a new ExponPow struct. Closed-form pdf/cdf/sf/ppf using the numerical-stability-friendly forms: cdf via -expm1(-expm1(x^b)), sf via exp(-expm1(x^b)), ppf via (ln(1-ln(1-q)))^(1/b) with ln_1p. Boundary at x=0 is shape-dependent: b<1→∞, b=1→1, b>1→0. mean/var inherited as NaN (no clean closed form). Note: NOT to be confused with gennorm (the generalized-normal). 3 anchor tests verified against scipy: pdf+cdf at 12 (b, x) values; boundary at x=0 across all three regimes; ppf↔cdf round-trip on the interior of support (skip when cdf saturates near 1, which is a numerical limit of any closed-form inverse). fsci-stats --lib: 1057 → 1060.

title: GAP: fsci-linalg solve_continuous_are / solve_discrete_are (3/6 → 5/6)
description:
Per fgxx audit, fsci-linalg lacks solve_continuous_are and
solve_discrete_are. Both are required by control-theory consumers
(LQR, Kalman filter covariance equations).

scipy's implementation uses the Schur decomposition of a Hamiltonian
(continuous) or symplectic (discrete) pencil, then extracts the
stable invariant subspace. fsci has the underlying schur + ordqz
primitives, so the missing layer is the pencil-construction +
stable-subspace extraction.

Acceptance:
- solve_continuous_are(A, B, Q, R[, S, E]) — continuous-time Riccati:
    AᵀX + XA − (XB + S)R⁻¹(BᵀX + Sᵀ) + Q = 0
- solve_discrete_are(A, B, Q, R[, S, E]) — discrete-time Riccati.
- Pencil construction + ordqz-based stable-subspace extraction.
- Unit tests: 3 well-conditioned problems from Benner et al.'s
  CAREX/DAREX benchmark set.
- COVERAGE.md updated (special_solve family flips from 3/6 to 5/6).

Out of scope: solve_lyapunov deprecated alias (declined in
DISCREPANCIES.md).

Related: fgxx (parent audit).
close_reason: solve_continuous_are (sign-fn) + solve_discrete_are (SDA) shipped with 7 tests; COVERAGE.md updated 3/6→5/6 (commit f1e8698)

description: Performance regression check: the new closed-form trunc_weibull_min_moment via incomplete gamma should be ~100x faster than the Simpson-on-4000-panels it replaced. Measure with cargo bench or perf_*.rs harness and document gain.

title: fsci-stats: pin GenExtreme(c=0) ≡ Gumbel(0,1) metamorphic identity
description: Pin the textbook metamorphic identity GenExtreme(c=0) ≡ Gumbel(0, 1). The generalized extreme-value distribution at c=0 is the standard Gumbel (Type I EV). fsci's GenExtreme.pdf has explicit c=0 branch using the Gumbel form exp(-(x + exp(-x))). Independent verification: Gumbel uses (1/scale)·exp(-z - exp(-z)) where z = (x - loc)/scale; GenExtreme uses the limiting form. Pin pdf/cdf/sf agreement at multiple x.
close_reason: Pinned the textbook metamorphic identity GenExtreme(c=0) ≡ Gumbel(0, 1) — the standard Type I extreme-value distribution. Test genextreme_c0_is_gumbel sweeps 9 x-values comparing pdf/cdf/sf at tol 1e-12. Independent verification: GenExtreme.pdf has an explicit c→0 branch that uses the Gumbel form exp(-(x + exp(-x))); Gumbel.pdf computes the same expression via the standardized z = (x - loc)/scale path. The algebraic equivalence holds only if both implementations are correct. fsci-stats --lib: 1055 → 1056.

title: fsci-integrate: nquad fix in 819a64c misses NaN-propagation failure mode
description: Self-found defect via stale-rch test feedback. The recent 819a64c fix added a RefCell<Option<IntegrateValidationError>> channel, but only captures Err results from inner nquad_inner calls. The most common silent-failure mode is NaN propagation: when the integrand returns NaN, inner quad returns Ok with NaN integral (not Err). My channel sees no Err, so nquad still returns Ok with NaN result — same defect as before. Fix: also check finiteness of the inner result; capture a typed error in the channel if NaN/Inf. Update regression test (was running 60s+ because adaptive subdivision iterated maxiter times).
close_reason: Fixed the NaN-propagation gap in 819a64c's nquad fix. Pre-fix vetrv missed the case where the user integrand returns NaN: inner quad returns Ok with non-finite integral (no Err for the channel to capture), the outer adaptive loop iterates to maxiter producing NaN, and nquad returned Ok with NaN result — same silent-distortion defect the original ticket addressed. Added a post-loop finiteness guard: if !result.is_finite(), return IntegrateValidationError::QuadInvalidBounds with detail naming the failure mode. Updated regression test to use unconditional NaN integrand (was using conditional NaN that exhausted maxiter; the new test exits as soon as the first NaN propagates). cargo build -p fsci-integrate clean (23s).

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: all existing harnesses use crash-only oracle for non-trivial kernels — no structured metamorphic assertions beyond conjugation symmetry
description:
Review-mode audit of 13 fuzz harnesses under fuzz/fuzz_targets/:

Oracle strength breakdown (per /testing-fuzzing skill, hierarchy from strongest to weakest):
- Reference implementation (strongest): NONE. No harness compares Rust output vs scipy or a trusted reference.
- Simplified shadow model: NONE.
- Inverse operation (round-trip): partial in p2c005_sparse_formats (format conversion round-trip).
- Metamorphic relation: used in p2c006_special_complex_branches for conjugation symmetry + real-axis reduction.
- Crash oracle (weakest): used everywhere else as the primary contract.

Weakness: per skill rule 'Use the strongest oracle available. A crash-only harness is acceptable only when no stronger oracle exists.' Examples:

- p2c001_validate_tol / p2c001_step_bounds: just let _ = fn(input); no oracle.
- p2c002_solve: no check that A @ x ≈ b after solve(A, b) succeeds. This is THE first metamorphic relation for any linear solver.
- p2c002_factorizations: no A ≈ L @ U invariant. Same for QR, SVD, Cholesky.
- p2c004_sparse_helpers: no invariant on output (e.g. tril(A).nnz <= A.nnz).
- p2c003_add (broken): the _properties variant had commutativity + associativity checks but the underlying fn doesn't exist (see frankenscipy-gvl2).

Per skill's strength-2 'simplified shadow model': for sparse, a BTreeMap<(usize, usize), f64> is a natural shadow. For solve, a direct naive Gaussian-elimination solver in the fuzz harness itself can serve as the reference.

Ask:
1. Add A @ x ≈ b invariant to p2c002_solve (measure residual, assert below tolerance scaled by κ(A) from fsci-linalg::cond).
2. Add A ≈ L @ U, A ≈ Q @ R invariants to p2c002_factorizations for LU, QR, SVD, Cholesky.
3. Add tril/triu idempotence and sparsity non-increase invariants to p2c004_sparse_helpers.
4. Add integer-arithmetic-style property checks (commutativity, associativity) wherever they hold mathematically for the kernel being fuzzed (e.g., sparse matrix addition).
5. For fsci-integrate quadrature: add the 'integral over [a,b] + integral over [b,c] = integral over [a,c]' metamorphic relation (fuzz [a,b,c] and check).

Every new harness added via tmti / bead plyj should include at least one strength-2+ oracle, not a bare let _ = fn(...).
close_reason: Re-closing (previously closed in session 2 via e974120). 5 asks all shipped: 762db09 / 8a229d9 + 79ef919 / 231a93a / 1cae9e5 / 88c4e4a.

title: fsci-special: yv at half-integer orders returns NaN
description: Surfaced by diff_special_bessel_jv_yv (frankenscipy-ucedr). fsci-special's yv returns NaN at v=1.5, 2.5 across all tested x ∈ {0.5, ..., 30}. Likely reflection formula Y_v(x) = (J_v cos(vπ) − J_{-v}(x)) / sin(vπ) doesn't gracefully handle the cos(vπ)=0 / sin(vπ)=±1 case for half-integer v — should special-case to closed forms or short-circuit through asymptotic at half-integer. jv at v=1.5, x=30 also lands 4.8e-3 abs (asymptotic seam). Fix candidates: (a) detect half-integer v and use Y_{1/2}(x) = -√(2/πx)·cos(x), Y_{3/2}(x) = -√(2/πx)·(cos(x)/x + sin(x)) closed forms; (b) tighten the asymptotic transition.
notes: Expanded scope 2026-05-07f: not just half-integer; fsci-special yv returns NaN for ALL v>1 across tested x ∈ {0.5, …, 15}. Specifically v=1.3, 1.5, 1.7, 2.3, 2.5, 3.7 all NaN; v=0.5, 0.7 work. yv path broken in fsci for any non-integer order >1. Updated diff harness restricts to v ∈ {0.5, 0.7} for both jv and yv; jv at v>1 may work but harness doesn't probe (until yv ships these orders).
close_reason: Fixed jv_series sign and magnitude recurrence (it crashed on log of negative v+k+1 and ignored sign(Γ(v+k+1))). yv now matches scipy at non-integer v across v ∈ {0.5, 1.3, 1.5, 1.7, 2.3, 2.5, 3.7, 5.5} to floating-point precision. title: fsci-stats: standard_normal_cdf precision loss in deep-tail (use erfc for x<0)
description: standard_normal_cdf in fsci-stats lib.rs:18460 uses 0.5·(1 + erf(x/√2)) which suffers catastrophic cancellation for x < -3 or so: erf returns close to -1 and 1 + (-1 + ε) = ε with massive precision loss. This has produced the cdf-drift-outside-[0,1] defect in multiple distributions: SkewNorm.cdf (fixed via clamp cc83022), IrwinHall.cdf (fixed clamp 7612637), RecipInvGauss.cdf (fixed clamp 814af81). Fix: use Φ(x) = 0.5·erfc(-x/√2) for x ≤ 0; keep current form for x > 0. fsci-special::erfc_scalar already exists. Pin precision improvement at e.g. Φ(-6) where current gives ~9.86e-10 with ~1e-9 error and erfc gives ~9.866e-10 to full machine precision.
close_reason: Fixed root-cause precision issue in standard_normal_cdf at crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:18460. Pre-fix Φ(x) = 0.5·(1 + erf(x/√2)) suffered catastrophic cancellation for x ≲ -3 because erf returns close to -1 and 1 + (-1 + ε) = ε loses most trailing bits. This produced the cdf-drift-outside-[0,1] defect family that previously required clamps in SkewNorm.cdf (cc83022), IrwinHall.cdf (7612637), and RecipInvGauss.cdf (814af81). Fix: switch to numerically-stable Φ(x) = 0.5·erfc(-x/√2) for x < 0; keep current form for x ≥ 0. Drop-in change. Φ(-7) goes from 0 (precision-lost) → 1.28e-12. Φ(-10) goes from 0 → 7.6e-24. Symmetry test relaxed to 1e-6 because 1 - Φ(x) for large positive x is itself catastrophic-cancellation form (the bound retained even with clamps was already tighter than the fundamental fp limit there). The previously-shipped clamps remain as defense in depth. fsci-stats --lib: 1091 → 1093.

title: fsci-signal: order_filter allocates N Vec<f64>'s when one buffer suffices
description: Skill: /profiling-software-performance. lib.rs:2003 order_filter allocates a fresh Vec<f64> for each output sample (window.to_vec() inside the loop), giving N allocations per call. The window size is bounded by window_size; we can pre-allocate one Vec outside the loop and reuse via clear+extend_from_slice. Same big-O O(N·W·log W) for sort, but eliminates per-iteration Vec alloc/free overhead — meaningful for large N with small window. title: [special] Statistical CDFs (ndtr, ndtri, btdtr, fdtr, gdtr) + kl_div
description:
## Goal
Implement the low-level statistical CDF/inverse-CDF functions from scipy.special, plus kl_div. These are used internally by scipy.stats but also called directly for performance.

## What to implement
- `ndtr(x)`: Normal distribution CDF (= Φ(x)). Standard normal CDF.
- `ndtri(y)`: Inverse normal CDF (= Φ⁻¹(y)). Quantile function.
- `btdtr(a, b, x)`: Beta distribution CDF (= regularized incomplete beta I_x(a,b)).
- `btdtri(a, b, y)`: Inverse beta CDF (find x such that I_x(a,b) = y). Uses Newton iteration on btdtr.
- `fdtr(dfn, dfd, x)`: F-distribution CDF.
- `fdtri(dfn, dfd, y)`: Inverse F CDF.
- `gdtr(a, b, x)`: Gamma distribution CDF (= regularized lower incomplete gamma P(a, x/b)).
- `gdtri(a, b, y)`: Inverse gamma CDF.
- `kl_div(x, y)`: Kullback-Leibler divergence element: x*log(x/y) - x + y. NOTE: this is DIFFERENT from the already-implemented rel_entr(x,y) = x*log(x/y). kl_div adds the -x+y correction term making it non-negative and equal to 0 only when x==y.

## Why this matters
These are the building blocks for statistical testing. Many SciPy users call ndtr/ndtri directly for performance-critical code. The inverse CDF functions (btdtri, fdtri, gdtri) are needed for confidence interval computation and p-value inversion.

## Implementation approach
- ndtr/ndtri: thin wrappers around existing Normal.cdf/Normal.ppf, but operating on plain f64 (no SpecialTensor overhead)
- btdtr: existing regularized_incomplete_beta in stats
- btdtri: Newton-Raphson on btdtr — start with initial guess from Normal approximation, refine
- fdtr/fdtri: use btdtr via relationship F_CDF(x|d1,d2) = I_{d1*x/(d1*x+d2)}(d1/2, d2/2)
- gdtr/gdtri: existing lower_regularized_gamma, inversion via Newton
- kl_div: x*log(x/y) - x + y with cases for x=0, y=0, x<0

## Testing requirements
- ndtr: Φ(0)=0.5, Φ(1.96)≈0.975, Φ(-∞)=0, Φ(∞)=1
- ndtr/ndtri roundtrip: ndtri(ndtr(x)) = x for x in [-4, 4]
- btdtr: known values Beta(2,5).cdf(0.3) vs reference
- btdtr/btdtri roundtrip
- fdtr/fdtri roundtrip
- gdtr/gdtri roundtrip
- kl_div: kl_div(p,p)=0, kl_div(0,q)=q, kl_div(p,0)=∞ for p>0
- kl_div vs rel_entr: kl_div(x,y) = rel_entr(x,y) - x + y
- At least 12 tests
close_reason: Completed

title: BUG: fsci-cluster Centroid/Median linkage tie-break differs from scipy on first merge
description:
Surfaced during br-7kxr fixture work (commit 1873b5e). For Centroid and Median linkage on the 5-point 2D dataset [[0,0],[1,0.5],[0.5,1.5],[3,3.5],[3.5,3]], scipy and fsci agree on cluster sizes and merge distances but pick different cluster indices in the first column of the linkage matrix. Specifically, the very first merge has scipy=1, fsci=0 in column 0 (cluster index of one of the merged pair).

Centroid and Median produce non-monotonic merge distances (a known Lance-Williams property), and the order in which the algorithm iterates over candidate min-distance pairs influences which index lands in column 0 vs column 1. scipy's tie-break is 'first index encountered in row-major upper-triangular scan'; fsci's is 'first index encountered in our find_min_active inner loop' which differs.

Acceptance:
- fsci_cluster::linkage with Centroid/Median on the canonical 5-point dataset matches scipy's exact (i, j) ordering in linkage matrix columns 0 and 1.
- Re-add 'linkage_centroid_5pts' and 'linkage_median_5pts' fixture cases to FSCI-P2C-009 with tight tolerance.

Lower priority because Single/Complete/Average/Ward/Weighted all agree exactly; only the two non-monotonic methods diverge in tie-break.
notes: Fixed via 7d6dd3c. Ported scipy's fast_linkage (Mullner 2011 generic clustering) — indexed min-heap, Lance-Williams Centroid/Median, neighbor reassignment + lower-bound tightening. 2 fixture cases on the 5-point dataset match scipy element-for-element at rtol=1e-9.

title: [HIGH] conformance: 'golden_journeys' test battery has NO golden-file comparison — 14 journeys only write snapshots, never read/diff against reference, CI gate G3 runs them as glorified smoke tests
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/tests/golden_journeys.rs (867 lines, 14 journeys) on 2026-04-22:

The file defines 14 #[test] functions named journey_01..journey_14 each exercising an end-to-end workflow (linalg pipeline, optimization, special, FFT, etc.). Each:
1. Constructs inputs in Rust code
2. Calls fsci-* functions
3. Asserts hand-coded tolerance conditions (e.g., 'max_residual < 1e-10' at line 113)
4. Calls write_journey(...) at lines 115, 188, 229, 279, 332, 372, 417, ... to write a JSON snapshot to fixtures/artifacts/golden_journeys/gj_<name>.json

But grep for fs::read, read_to_string, assert_golden, or any deserialization: ZERO matches. The snapshots are WRITE-ONLY. There is no comparison against a committed golden reference.

Per /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 2 (Golden File Testing), a golden workflow needs:
1. UPDATE_GOLDENS=1 env var support to regenerate golden files.
2. Normal-run path that reads the golden and diffs against current output.
3. Failure when output diverges from golden by more than tolerance.

None of these exist in golden_journeys.rs. The 'golden' name is aspirational, not descriptive.

What the journeys actually verify: each scenario fails only if:
- An fsci-* function returns an Err where success was expected (via .expect() at lines 86, 90, 148, 165, ...)
- A hand-coded assertion fails (e.g., assert!(max_residual < 1e-10))

That's a SMOKE TEST -- run the code, crash on panic, otherwise pass. Not conformance.

Impact:
- G3Conformance CI gate (per zi0q) runs this test. A reviewer sees 'G3 Differential Conformance passed' and infers scipy parity was checked. It wasn't.
- The snapshot JSONs accumulate at fixtures/artifacts/golden_journeys/gj_*.json and are git-ignored or committed but never consulted. They exist purely to crash on write failure.
- Hand-coded tolerance thresholds (1e-10 etc.) mask regressions within their band: if fsci-linalg::solve starts returning residuals of 1e-9 (100x worse), journey_01 still passes.

Fix:
1. Implement proper golden-file workflow per the skill:
   - On first run (or UPDATE_GOLDENS=1), write gj_*.json to tests/golden/gj_*.json (committed dir, NOT artifacts/ which is gitignored).
   - On normal runs, read the committed golden, deserialize, compare every snapshot.value against golden.value with allclose_scalar (once i0hm is fixed).
   - Add a script to run UPDATE_GOLDENS=1 + git diff to allow reviewers to inspect changes.
2. OR rename the file from golden_journeys.rs to smoke_journeys.rs to match its actual behavior, and strip the misleading 'snapshot' language.
3. The 14 scenarios are reasonable smoke tests -- keep them but stop marketing them as 'golden'.

Related: ivg5 ('differential' is self-check), zi0q (G3Conformance commands don't match gate name), /testing-conformance-harnesses Pattern 2.
close_reason: Implemented a real golden-file workflow in crates/fsci-conformance/tests/golden_journeys.rs: UPDATE_GOLDENS=1 now writes 14 committed goldens under tests/golden_journeys/, normal runs read and compare exact canonical JSON bytes, duration_ns is normalized to zero, mismatches write *.actual.json under fixtures/artifacts/golden_journeys/, and both local cargo test plus rch cargo test/check for fsci-conformance now pass.

title: fsci-stats: energy_distance e_xx/e_yy are O(N²)+O(M²) but can be O(N log N)
description: Skill: /profiling-software-performance. lib.rs:10308 energy_distance currently does three O(N²)-class loops: e_xy = O(N·M) (cross sum), e_xx = O(N²) (within-X sum), e_yy = O(M²) (within-Y sum). The within-set sums admit a sorted closed form: for sorted u of length n, sum_{i<j} (u_j - u_i) = sum_i u_sorted[i] · (2i - n + 1) — O(N) after one O(N log N) sort. This drops e_xx and e_yy from N² to N log N. For N=M=1000 that's ~2M operations saved (the cross sum stays O(N·M) for now; optimizing it requires a merge sweep — leaving as follow-up). Will preserve output exactly via mathematical identity.
close_reason: Shipped: energy_distance e_xx and e_yy now use within_set_l1_pair_sum() with the sorted closed form Σ_i s[i]·(2i−n+1), dropping each from O(N²)/O(M²) to O(N log N)/O(M log M). For N=M=1000 that's ~2M ops saved (2 of 3 quadratic loops). The cross e_xy stays O(N·M) for now; merge-sweep optimization deferred. Regression test energy_distance_within_set_pair_sum_matches_naive verifies the closed form matches naïve O(N²) on a 20-point sample plus boundary cases (constant, empty, singleton). All 4 prior energy_distance tests still pass. fsci-stats now 964 tests (+1).

title: fsci-stats: Laplace/Gumbel/GumbelLeft inherit lossy sf default
description: Skill: /modes-of-reasoning-project-analysis. Continuation of [frankenscipy-h36zm] (Logistic/Pareto/Lomax) and [wofb] (Normal/Weibull): three more common distributions still inherit the precision-lossy default sf = 1 - cdf despite having one-line closed forms. (1) Laplace.sf for z >= 0 = 0.5·exp(-z) instead of 1 - (1 - 0.5·exp(-z)). (2) Gumbel.sf = -expm1(-exp(-z)) instead of 1 - exp(-exp(-z)). (3) GumbelLeft.sf = exp(-exp(z)) instead of 1 - (1 - exp(-exp(z))). All three preserve significant digits in the deep right tail where the default rounds to 0. Will add sf overrides + tail-precision regression tests asserting the deep-tail value is strictly positive and matches the closed form to <1e-12 relative.
close_reason: Extended the lossy-default-sf systemic fix from [h36zm]/[wofb] to 3 more distributions: Laplace, Gumbel, GumbelLeft — each had a one-line closed-form sf available but inherited 1-cdf, which catastrophically cancels in the deep right tail. (1) Laplace.sf for z>=0 = 0.5·exp(-z); for z<0 = 1 - 0.5·exp(z). (2) Gumbel.sf = -expm1(-exp(-z)). (3) GumbelLeft.sf = exp(-exp(z)). Added 3 tail-precision regression tests asserting (a) deep-tail sf is strictly positive and matches closed form to <1e-12 relative, (b) sf + cdf = 1 near the centre. fsci-stats --lib: 999 → 1002 passing.

title: fsci-conformance: no scipy diff harness for distribution pdf/cdf/sf/ppf
description: Skill: /testing-conformance-harnesses. diff_stats covers aggregate scipy.stats functions (circmean, ttest, etc.) and diff_stats_correlation covers correlation, but no harness drives distribution-object methods (pdf, cdf, sf, ppf) against scipy.stats.{distribution}. The three I just refined (h36zm sf overrides, covaj try_fit) — Logistic, Pareto, Lomax — should especially get scipy parity coverage on the just-shipped sf closed forms. Will add diff_stats_distributions.rs following the diff_special_carlson pattern: per (distribution, method, x) case driven through scipy.stats subprocess oracle, abs/rel tol 1e-10, skip cleanly if scipy unavailable, fixture-artifact emission. Coverage: Logistic, Pareto, Lomax × pdf/cdf/sf × multiple x including deep-tail (sf precision check).
close_reason: Added crates/fsci-conformance/tests/diff_stats_distributions.rs with 167 curated cases: Logistic (3 param-tuples × 7 x-grid × 3 methods = 63), Pareto (3 × ≤7 × 3 = 54 after below-scale filter), Lomax (4 × 6 × 3 = 72) — total 189 candidate cases (some skipped where x < scale). Each case driven through scipy.stats subprocess oracle with abs/rel tol 1e-10. The x-grid intentionally includes deep-right-tail values (z=40 for logistic, x=1e6 for pareto/lomax) where the pre-h36zm default sf would have rounded to 0 — the closed-form sf overrides ship parity here. Mirrors diff_signal_hilbert/diff_special_carlson skip pattern: silent skip when scipy unavailable, hard fail under FSCI_REQUIRE_SCIPY_ORACLE. Emits fixture artifact under FSCI-P2C-007/diff/. title: fsci-stats: Normal.cdf bypasses standard_normal_cdf erfc path
description: Normal.cdf in fsci-stats lib.rs:631 uses 0.5·(1 + erf(z·FRAC_1_SQRT_2)) directly, NOT the standard_normal_cdf wrapper. So the deep-tail precision improvement from 095a947 doesn't propagate to Normal. Same defect class — Normal(0, 1).cdf(-10) returns 0 due to catastrophic cancellation. Fix: Normal.cdf delegates to standard_normal_cdf(z), or duplicates the erfc-for-negative branch. Pin precision regression at multiple negative z values.
close_reason: Fixed Normal.cdf precision in deep tail by delegating to standard_normal_cdf instead of recomputing 0.5·(1 + erf(z·FRAC_1_SQRT_2)) directly. The previous bypass meant the 095a947 erfc-path improvement didn't propagate to Normal — Normal(0, 1).cdf(-10) returned 0. Post-fix it returns ~7.6e-24 (full precision via the standard_normal_cdf erfc branch). Regression test normal_cdf_deep_tail_precision_via_standard_normal_cdf verifies positivity at z=-7 and z=-10 plus ~1e-6 relative precision against scipy reference values. Translation-scaled instance Normal(5, 2).cdf(-9) (= Φ(-7)) also benefits. fsci-stats --lib: 1093 → 1094.

title: [code-review] [HIGH] fsci-signal group_delay_from_ba returns zero for pure delays
description: Review-mode finding in crates/fsci-signal/src/lib.rs:2382. group_delay_from_ba computes dbr*br + dbi*bi, the real part of the analytic derivative quotient, while group delay must follow the phase derivative. A pure one-sample delay b=[0,1], a=[1] has H=e^(-jw) and group delay 1 at every frequency, but the current wrapper returns 0 across the grid. The canonical group_delay path later in the same file already has the correct weighted-polynomial helper; fix the wrapper to reuse that path and add a pure-delay regression.
close_reason: Fixed group_delay_from_ba by reusing the canonical group_delay_at_frequency helper; added pure one-sample delay regression and cleaned same-file clippy blockers. Validated with rch cargo test -p fsci-signal group_delay and rch cargo clippy -p fsci-signal --all-targets -- -D warnings.

title: PARITY: scipy.optimize.differential_evolution NonlinearConstraint + LinearConstraint coverage
description:
9n5j-1 added unconstrained differential_evolution coverage but scipy.optimize.differential_evolution supports two constraint paths:
1. NonlinearConstraint(fun, lb, ub) — arbitrary callable returning a bound vector.
2. LinearConstraint(A, lb, ub) — linear constraints expressed as A·x ∈ [lb, ub].

The constraint-handling path uses a specialized penalty / Lagrangian technique that's sensitive to seed and regularization. A regression here is invisible without explicit constraint tests.

Acceptance:
- Extend OptimizeCase (or its differential_evolution sub-variant) with a constraints field accepting a sum type: {kind: 'linear', A, lb, ub} | {kind: 'nonlinear', fn_name, lb, ub}.
- 6+ cases in FSCI-P2C-003:
  * unconstrained Rosenbrock (already exists, leave as control).
  * Rosenbrock + linear constraint x[0] + x[1] = 1.
  * Rastrigin + nonlinear constraint x0^2 + x1^2 <= 1.
  * Sphere + box constraint via existing bounds field.
  * Mixed linear + box.
  * Edge case: infeasible constraint (lb > ub) -> typed error in Hardened mode.
- scipy_optimize_oracle.py extended to route NonlinearConstraint / LinearConstraint based on case schema.
- Tolerance: 1e-3 for x (DE is stochastic-ish even with seed); seed=42 fixed.

Related: 9n5j parent and -1/-2/-3/-4 children, okyl (trust-constr KKT — same problem class but different solver).
notes: Fixed via 68dc8c1. differential_evolution_constrained wrapper applies quadratic exterior penalty (1e6 * Σ excess²) to objective; DeConstraintSpec sum type routes linear and nonlinear constraints through the conformance dispatch. 4 fixture cases at relaxed tolerances (Rosenbrock+linear, Rastrigin+nonlinear unit_disk, Sphere+empty as control, Sphere+linear sum≥2). scipy bit-parity at rtol=1e-3 left as P3 follow-up — blocked on PRNG mismatch (scipy PCG64 vs fsci StdRng) and Lampinen-tournament vs penalty algorithmic difference.

title: fsci-arrayapi: CoreArrayBackend recovers poison without clear_poison
description: Skill: /deadlock-finder-and-fixer. CoreArrayBackend in crates/fsci-arrayapi/src/backend.rs has 4 lock() sites on dtype_dispatch_logs and shape_mismatch_logs, all using the recovery pattern .lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()). This recovers the data but does NOT call clear_poison(), so the mutex stays in the Err state forever — every future caller hits the Err branch and the Mutex.is_poisoned() flag remains true permanently. The rest of the workspace's recovery sites (fsci-arrayapi audit, fsci-fft trace_log, fsci-stats audit, fsci-special audit, fsci-opt trace_log, fsci-fft plan cache) all use the symmetric pattern: clear_poison() + poisoned.into_inner(), restoring lock health. Will switch to the canonical pattern via a small lock_or_recover_logs helper, plus add a regression test asserting is_poisoned()=false after a recover sequence.
close_reason: Canonicalized 4 lock sites in CoreArrayBackend (dtype_dispatch_logs read+write, shape_mismatch_logs read+write) onto a single CoreArrayBackend::lock_or_recover helper that mirrors the workspace audit-ledger pattern: clear_poison() + poisoned.into_inner(). Pre-fix the inline .lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) recovered data but left the mutex permanently poisoned, so is_poisoned() stayed true forever and every future call walked the Err branch. Regression test dispatch_log_reads_clear_poison_after_recovery force-poisons the dtype_dispatch_logs mutex, calls the public read, and asserts is_poisoned()==false afterward — would have failed before the fix. fsci-arrayapi --lib: 50 → 51 passing.

title: fsci-special: Carlson elliprg missing
description: Skill: /porting-to-rust. After elliprc (mxxij), elliprf (1ww0j), and elliprd (xdi1c), the Carlson family still lacks elliprg — the symmetric integral that links the Carlson family to the standard elliptic integrals E(k) and complete elliptic integrals. RG(x, y, z) = (1/4π) ∫∫_{S²} √(x·u² + y·v² + z·w²) dω. Closed-form anchors: RG(x, x, x) = √x; RG(0, 1, 1) = π/4 (= half of E(0)=π/2). Carlson identity: RG = (z·RF + √(xy/z))/2 + (z−x)(z−y)·RD/(−6) for z > 0; permute arguments to put a positive value last when any are zero. Will port + 4 reference tests.
close_reason: Shipped pub fn elliprg(x, y, z) using Carlson's stable identity RG = (z·RF + √(xy/z))/2 + (z-x)(z-y)·RD/(-6) for z>0, with argument permutation when any input is zero (RG is fully symmetric, unlike RD). 5 tests: diagonal RG(x,x,x)=√x; RG(0,1,1)=π/4 (= half E(0)); RG(0,0,c)=√c/2 (degenerate two-zero case + all-zero=0); full argument symmetry x↔y, x↔z, cyclic; negative/NaN rejection. Carlson family is now complete except elliprj. fsci-special now 778 tests (+5).

title: EPIC: Repo Hygiene Reset
description:
## Background

AGENTS.md §"No Script-Based Changes" explicitly forbids running scripts that process/change code files. §"No File Proliferation" forbids creating variations and test files outside proper locations. Both rules are being actively violated.

## Current State (2026-04-09 Reality Check)

The repo root contains ~70+ untracked files from multi-agent debugging sessions:
- fix_*.py (5 files): fix_ndimage.py, fix_ppf.py, fix_ppf_robust.py, fix_sparse.py, fix_svd.py — these are REGEX-BASED CODE MUTATION SCRIPTS, exactly what AGENTS.md forbids
- patch_*.py (2 files): patch_formats.py, patch_stats.py — more script-based code mutation
- test_*.rs (~30+ files): standalone reproducer files that should be proper bin/ targets or tests/
- test_*.out, test_*.py, *.txt: assorted scratch output
- Compiled binaries: test_clamp, test_clamp2, test_clamp_nan, test_delaunay_nan, etc.
- ndimage_patch.diff, reproduce_airy.rs, etc.

## Goal

Clean up ALL scratch files from the repo root. Move any valuable reproducer .rs files into proper crate bin/ or tests/ directories. Delete the fix_*.py/patch_*.py scripts (their changes have already been applied). Remove compiled binaries and scratch output.

## Success Criteria

1. Zero untracked .py, .rs, .out, .txt scratch files at repo root
2. Zero compiled test binaries at repo root
3. Any valuable reproducers preserved in proper test locations
4. AGENTS.md discipline re-established

## Serves Project Goal

A clean repo root signals project discipline. The scratch file mess makes the project look chaotic and makes it harder for agents to understand what's production vs. temporary.

title: Remove fix_*.py and patch_*.py scripts from repo root
description:
## What

Delete the 7 Python scripts at repo root that perform regex-based code mutation:
fix_ndimage.py, fix_ppf.py, fix_ppf_robust.py, fix_sparse.py, fix_svd.py, patch_formats.py, patch_stats.py

## Why

AGENTS.md §"No Script-Based Changes" EXPLICITLY FORBIDS running scripts that process/change code files. These scripts exist because agents violated this rule during NaN-fixing sprints. Their changes have already been applied to the codebase (the .rs files are modified). The scripts serve no further purpose and their presence signals that the rule isn't enforced.

## Note

Ask for explicit deletion permission per AGENTS.md §"RULE NUMBER 1: NO FILE DELETION". These are scratch files created by agents, not production code, but the rule applies universally.

title: Remove remaining scratch output files from repo root

title: PARITY: scipy.special elliptic integrals + Jacobi elliptic functions (ellipk/ellipe/ellipj) in P2C-006
description:
fsci-special covers a wide range but elliptic integrals + Jacobi elliptic functions are systematically thin: ellipk / ellipkm1 / ellipkinc / ellipe / ellipeinc / ellipj / ellipfun. These are required by orbital-mechanics, antenna-design, and pendulum consumers.

Acceptance:
- 16+ fixture cases in FSCI-P2C-006 covering:
  * ellipk(m) at m = 0, 0.5, 1-eps; ellipkm1(p) at p = 1.0 to validate the 'log near 1' branch.
  * ellipkinc(phi, m) and ellipeinc(phi, m) at (pi/4, 0.5) and (pi/2, 0.99) and (0, 0.5).
  * ellipe(m) at m = 0, 0.5, 1.
  * ellipj(u, m): the four-tuple sn, cn, dn, ph at (0.5, 0.3) and (1.0, 0.99).
- scipy_special_oracle.py dispatchers for each (current oracle has at most a partial ellip dispatch — file blocker if missing).
- Rust runner: execute_special_ellip already exists per the existing fixture surface; verify and extend.
- Tolerance: 1e-12 for ellipk / ellipe (closed-form Carlson form); 1e-10 for ellipkinc / ellipj (iterative AGM convergence).
- Hardened cases: ellipk(m=1) -> typed error (singular), ellipkinc(phi=pi/4, m=1) -> typed error.

Related: vn0o (bessel/gamma corner sweep — sibling), tguh (broader special edge sweep), r9st (oracle missing dispatchers), pkdy (cast-to-real bug in oracle helpers).
close_reason: Completed in a353b7e

title: fsci-conformance: add diff_signal_peak_widths — peak widths parity
description: fsci_signal::peak_widths(x, peaks, rel_height) returns (widths, width_heights, left_ips, right_ips). Probe against scipy.signal.peak_widths. 1e-10 abs.

title: fsci-stats: scipy.stats.logser (LogSeries) discrete distribution missing
description: Skill: /porting-to-rust. fsci-stats has 60+ distributions but lacks LogSeries (scipy.stats.logser, the logarithmic-series distribution). Closed forms with shape p ∈ (0, 1): pmf(k;p) = -p^k / (k·ln(1-p)) for k ≥ 1; mean = -p / ((1-p)·ln(1-p)); var = -p·(p+ln(1-p)) / ((1-p)²·ln(1-p)²). Will port pub struct LogSeries implementing DiscreteDistribution + reference tests at p=0.5 (closed form gives mean = -0.5/(0.5·ln(0.5)) = 1/ln(2) ≈ 1.4427; pmf(1) = -0.5/ln(0.5) = 0.7213).
close_reason: Audit found that LogSeries already exists at lib.rs:3520 with full DiscreteDistribution impl — what was missing was reference-value pinning of the closed-form pmf/mean. The existing logseries_pmf_cdf_match_scipy_reference_values, logseries_mean_var_and_mass_match_closed_form, and logseries_ppf_matches_scipy_reference_values do exist already; the 4 NEW tests I added pin the additional closed-form anchors at p=0.5 (pmf(k) = -p^k/(k·ln(1-p)) for k=1,2,3 with mathematical-form expectations), cdf tail convergence to 1, mean = 1/ln(2), and var positivity sanity check. fsci-stats now 963 tests (+4).

title: fsci-stats: weightedtau missing rank='auto' symmetrisation — diverges on non-monotone fixtures
description: P3: fsci's weightedtau (crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:16403) uses ONLY the x-array ranking (single hyperbolic weigher) where scipy.stats.weightedtau defaults to rank='auto' which symmetrises by averaging the two τ values computed with each side as the ranking variable. For monotone (x, y) the two are equal so fsci matches; for uncorrelated permutations they diverge — uncorrelated_n11 fixture: scipy=-0.0497, fsci=-0.1713 (abs diff 0.122). Fix: average τ(x-ranked) and τ(y-ranked) at the end, or take the (x-ranked + y-ranked)/2 weight. Surfaces in diff_stats_correlation_basic.rs uncorrelated_weightedtau arm.

title: fsci-conformance: add diff_signal_detrend — detrend parity

Adds diff_stats_pmean_loglik.rs covering generalised power mean (scipy.stats.pmean) and normal log-likelihood (closed-form Gaussian) against scipy.stats.pmean and numpy. 4 datasets × 4 power values + 4 (data, mu, sigma) fixtures × norm_loglikelihood = 20 cases. title: feat(stats): add multiscale_graphcorr stub for distance correlation
