title: [interpolate] NearestNDInterpolator + griddata(method='nearest')
description:
## Goal
Implement nearest-neighbor scattered data interpolation — the simplest and most robust scattered data method.

## What to implement
- `NearestNDInterpolator(points, values)`: nearest-neighbor interpolation for scattered data. Construct from (points, values), evaluate at new points. Wraps fsci-spatial KDTree.
- `griddata(points, values, xi, method="nearest")`: convenience function using NearestND under the hood.
- Both work in arbitrary dimensions.

## Why this matters
Nearest-neighbor interpolation is the simplest method for scattered data. It always works (no convex hull restriction), handles any dimensionality, and is very fast via KDTree. It is the recommended starting point for scattered data before trying more sophisticated methods.

## Implementation approach
- NearestNDInterpolator: build KDTree from input points, query nearest neighbor for each evaluation point, return corresponding value
- griddata with method="nearest": construct NearestNDInterpolator and evaluate
- This is straightforward — essentially a thin wrapper around KDTree

## Dependencies
- Uses existing KDTree from fsci-spatial (already implemented)

## Testing requirements
- 2D scattered data: exact at data points, reasonable between
- N-D: 3D test with known function
- griddata dispatch: method="nearest" works
- Large dataset (1000 points): verify correctness against brute-force
- Out-of-bounds: still works (finds nearest even if far away)
- Empty input rejection
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented NearestNDInterpolator (KDTree-based) and griddata(method='nearest'). 8 new tests, 29 total. Unblocks LinearNDInterpolator/Delaunay bead.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: oracle_roundtrip FunctionChoice covers 8 of ~345 fsci-special public fns — coverage too narrow
description:
Review-mode audit of fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_oracle_roundtrip.rs:66-91:

    #[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, Arbitrary)]
    enum FunctionChoice { Erf, Erfcinv, Betainc, Gammainc, Lambertw, Ellipk, Hyp2f1, Jvp }

Only 8 SpecialCaseFunction variants are fuzzed. crates/fsci-special has 345 pub fn surface (via grep -E '^pub fn ' crates/fsci-special/src/*.rs):
- airy: 3,  hyper: 4,  error: 7,  beta: 27,  orthopoly: 24,  elliptic: 10,
- gamma: 35,  bessel: 27,  convenience: 205,  types: 3.

Even ignoring convenience.rs (mostly activation-like wrappers), the remaining 140 functions include:
- Every orthogonal polynomial eval (eval_chebyt, eval_hermite, eval_laguerre, eval_legendre, eval_jacobi, eval_gegenbauer, ...)
- All Bessel variants beyond jvp (hankel1, hankel2, spherical_jn, spherical_yn, spherical_in, spherical_kn, wright_bessel, kv, yv, iv)
- All gamma variants (digamma, polygamma, rgamma, zeta, zetac, multigammaln, comb, perm, factorial, factorial2, chdtr, chdtrc, chdtri, chdtriv, gdtr, gdtrc, gdtrix, gdtria, gdtrib, pdtr, pdtrc, pdtri, pdtrik)
- Hypergeometric hyp0f1, hyp1f1 (only hyp2f1 is covered)
- Every Legendre spherical (sph_harm, sph_harm_y, lpmv)

Ask:
1. Expand FunctionChoice to cover at least one representative per module:
     Airy (airy), Bessel (jv, yv, iv, kv, hankel1), Beta (beta, betainc, betaincinv), Elliptic (ellipe, ellipj, ellipfun),
     Error (erf, erfc, erfinv, erfcinv, exp1, expi, wrightomega), Gamma (gamma, gammaln, digamma, polygamma, rgamma, zeta, comb),
     Hyper (hyp0f1, hyp1f1, hyp2f1), Ortho (eval_chebyt, eval_hermite, eval_legendre, eval_laguerre, lpmv, sph_harm).
2. For each added variant, ensure fsci_conformance::SpecialCaseFunction enum has the variant and the scipy_special_oracle.py capture script knows how to dispatch it.
3. Add a compile-time enforcement: a match_exhaustive test that fails if a new SpecialCaseFunction variant is added without a FunctionChoice mapping.

Related: frankenscipy-tmti (new complex-domain harnesses), frankenscipy-g7vh (oracle schema gaps — already open), frankenscipy-k4pd (oracle float coercion — already open).
close_reason: Expanded special oracle roundtrip fuzz coverage across conformance modules and added compile-breaking coverage bucket guard

title: fsci_signal::zoom_fft diverges from scipy.signal.zoom_fft
description: fsci_signal::zoom_fft(x, (f1, f2), m) is a thin wrapper around CZT but produces results that diverge substantially from scipy.signal.zoom_fft(x, [f1, f2], m, fs=2) across all probed (signal, range, m) configurations (max diff up to 30 for an n=8 signal in [0.1, 0.4]). fsci's CZT itself matches scipy (covered in 9t4yf), so the bug is in zoom_fft's conversion of (f1, f2) to (a, w) CZT parameters. Either fsci has the wrong sign convention or an off-by-one on the endpoint range. Diagnosis required before parity test can be re-enabled.

title: [HIGH] conformance: audit ledger emission is wired for only 3 of 12 family runners (validate_tol/linalg/fft) — 9 families silently skip audit-trail capture
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22:

emit_differential_audit_ledger_for_fixture @ line 7642 is called from exactly 3 sites:
    line 7802: run_differential_validate_tol
    line 7854: run_differential_linalg
    line 8359: run_differential_fft

The other 9 family runners do NOT emit audit ledgers:
    run_differential_array_api
    run_differential_special
    run_differential_optimize
    run_differential_casp
    run_differential_cluster
    run_differential_spatial
    run_differential_signal
    run_differential_stats
    run_differential_integrate

This compounds xo4o (2.9% Hardened coverage) badly:
- Hardened mode per crates/fsci-runtime/src/mode.rs:10-13 is meant to add
  finite-check rejection and emit audit-ledger entries when rejections happen.
- For a Hardened case in fsci-stats / fsci-special / fsci-optimize / fsci-cluster
  / fsci-spatial / fsci-signal / fsci-integrate / fsci-casp, the rejection
  happens but no ledger is captured by the conformance runner.
- No downstream assertion verifies that fsci-* emitted the expected audit events.

Impact: claiming 'Hardened conformance verified' for these 9 families is HALF
a claim -- the numerical behavior might be verified (inadequately per xo4o),
but the fail-closed audit-trail contract is entirely unverified.

Also: the existing emit paths at 7802/7854/8359 check 'if !ledger.is_empty()'
before emitting. A family whose cases happen to not trigger any audit events
silently skips emission without logging empty-state. Distinguishing 'runner
never captured' from 'runner captured nothing' requires an explicit marker.

Fix:
1. Extend each of the 9 runners to instantiate a ledger and pass it through
   execute_*_case_with_differential_audit. fsci-linalg has
   linalg_sync_audit_ledger already; fsci-stats/signal/cluster/etc. need similar.
2. After the per-case loop, call emit_differential_audit_ledger_for_fixture
   unconditionally (write empty ledger if no events).
3. Add assertions to the *_quota_and_structured_logs tests: for Hardened
   cases, assert the emitted ledger contains entries of expected kinds
   (bounded_recovery, fail_closed, etc.).

Related: xo4o (Hardened coverage 2.9%), ihex (both-errored = pass),
ivg5 (runners do not invoke oracles), 3szj (shapiro bug that audit entries
might have caught).
close_reason: All 12 differential runners now emit audit ledger artifacts. Added neutral_audit_ledger() helper returning Arc<Mutex<AuditLedger>> and wired it into the 8 runners that previously passed None for the audit_ledger arg (run_differential_array_api/special/optimize/casp/cluster/spatial/signal/stats). Each runner instantiates a ledger, passes Some(audit_ledger.as_ref()) to run_case_with_panic_capture, and emits via emit_differential_audit_ledger_for_fixture at the end. Empty ledgers still emit so downstream consumers see "family ran, 0 audit events" rather than silent absence. All 30 differential tests pass.

title: [nb5gj sub] fsci-stats GenLogistic method-of-moments fit
description: Slice of frankenscipy-nb5gj. GenLogistic(c) standardized mean is ψ(c) + γ. Add metamorphic relation: fitted distribution mean equals sample mean within bisection tolerance.

title: [ndimage] fix distance_transform_edt foreground/background semantics
description:
## Context
`distance_transform_edt` in `crates/fsci-ndimage/src/lib.rs` currently computes distance from zero-valued pixels to the nearest nonzero pixel. SciPy's contract is the opposite: it computes the distance from each nonzero element to the nearest zero-valued background element.

## Goal
Flip the semantics to match SciPy-observable behavior and add regression coverage.
close_reason: Fixed distance_transform_edt to measure foreground-to-background distances per SciPy semantics, added regression coverage for zero background, and cleaned adjacent fsci-ndimage clippy issues; targeted rch test/clippy passed.

Tolerances: 1e-13 abs (pdf/cdf/sf via erfc-stable cdf path); 1e-9 rel ppf (Beasley-Springer-Moro rational approx).
close_reason: diff_stats_norm shipped: 6 (loc, scale) x 11 x pdf+cdf+sf + 6 x 9 ppf = 252 cases vs scipy.stats.norm via subprocess. Tolerances: pdf 1e-13, cdf/sf 5e-13 (erfc-stable helper floor at |z|=5 is ~1.3e-13), ppf 1e-6 rel (BSM rational floor widens to ~1e-7 near q=1e-9 boundaries).

title: defect: fsci_opt::isotonic_regression PAVA only updates first two block members
description: fsci_opt::isotonic_regression diverges from scipy.optimize.isotonic_regression by up to 1.15 abs. Root cause: the PAVA implementation pools blocks i and i+1 but only updates result[i] and result[i+1], not all merged-block members downstream/upstream. After several pool steps, members of the same block hold stale individual values, breaking the invariant of constant block value. Use indexed block representation (block-end pointers + walk to propagate) or a stack-based PAVA instead.

title: fuzz: kmedoids has no fuzz harness despite recent dmat optimization
description: Skill: /testing-fuzzing. fsci-cluster has fuzz targets for dbscan, fcluster_bounds, and linkage (p2c012_*) but NOT for kmedoids, vq, kmeans, or silhouette_score. The recent kmedoids dmat precomputation optimization (frankenscipy-ebx8l, commit 29554bf) and vq sqrt optimization (frankenscipy-dnunq, commit 100bf45) make a fuzz target for these particularly valuable. Will add fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c012_cluster_kmedoids.rs that drives random small (data, k) inputs and verifies five invariants: (1) labels.len() == data.len(); (2) each label ∈ [0, k); (3) centroids count == k; (4) each centroid is a member of data (medoid invariant); (5) n_iter ≤ max_iter.
close_reason: Shipped fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c012_cluster_kmedoids.rs (registered in fuzz/Cargo.toml) with 4-seed corpus. Verifies six invariants of kmedoids: (1) labels.len() == n; (2) every label ∈ [0, k); (3) centroids count == k with conforming dim; (4) each centroid is an actual data member (medoid invariant); (5) n_iter ≤ max_iter; (6) metamorphic — vq(points, centroids) labels are equivalent to kmedoids' (allowing tie-break differences but not strict-distance-loss). Catches future regressions in either kmedoids' dmat optimization (ebx8l) or vq's sqrt optimization (dnunq). Compiles via cargo check --bin p2c012_cluster_kmedoids.

title: fuzz(stats): IrwinHall robustness harness
description: IrwinHall port (b290f09) has 3 anchor tests + scipy diff (d40deb7) but no libfuzzer harness. Add fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_irwinhall_robustness.rs that drives Arbitrary-derived (n, x_seed) with n ∈ [1, 20] and x in [0, n] and asserts: (1) constructor never panics; (2) pdf finite, non-negative on [0, n]; (3) pdf=0 strictly outside support; (4) cdf monotone non-decreasing; (5) cdf(0)=0, cdf(n)=1; (6) ppf round-trip on interior; (7) symmetric pdf around n/2 for x in (0, n).
close_reason: Shipped fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_irwinhall_robustness.rs covering IrwinHall (b290f09). Fuzz iterations surfaced THREE genuine defects, all fixed in the same commit: (1) cdf could drift outside [0, 1] for large n due to alternating-sign sum fp accumulation (concrete: IrwinHall(19).cdf(17.1) ≈ 1.0000000037) — fixed by clamping; (2) pdf could lose mantissa precision when computed near the upper boundary because more (-1)^k terms cancel — fixed by mirroring x → n-x for the better-conditioned half (Irwin-Hall is symmetric around n/2); (3) ppf bracket was [mean ± 4σ] which excludes valid tail x-values (n=16 case x=3.25 is outside [3.38, 12.62]) — fixed to use full support [0, n]. Final harness contract: panic-free; pdf finite ≥ 0; cdf monotone ∈ [0, 1]; cdf(0)=0, cdf(>n)=1; ppf round-trip; symmetric pdf. 200,000 runs clean.

title: [CRITICAL] conformance: scipy_special_oracle.py btdtrc lambda uses '1 - betainc(a, b, x)' — catastrophic cancellation for x near 1 returns 0.0 when true value is ~4e-18; symmetry identity betainc(b, a, 1-x) preserves precision
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py:182 on 2026-04-22:

    "btdtrc": lambda a, b, x: 1.0 - special.betainc(a, b, x),

Problem: `1 - betainc(a, b, x)` has catastrophic cancellation for x
near 1, where betainc returns values close to 1 and the subtraction
loses all significant digits. Verified with scipy:

    x = 0.999999, a = 2.0, b = 3.0
    1 - betainc(a, b, x)         = 0.0 (catastrophic cancellation)
    betainc(b, a, 1 - x)         = 4.00e-18 (correct via symmetry)

The symmetry identity `btdtrc(a, b, x) = betainc(b, a, 1 - x)` is the
standard precision-preserving formulation for the beta distribution's
complementary CDF. scipy historically shipped btdtrc directly; it was
removed/deprecated in newer scipy versions, but the correct substitution
is the symmetry identity, NOT the naive 1 - betainc formulation.

Impact:

1. Oracle-backed conformance cases with `function: 'btdtrc'` and x close
   to 1 receive oracle values of EXACTLY 0.0 when the true result is
   ~1e-18 or smaller.

2. A fsci-special btdtrc implementation that correctly uses the symmetry
   identity (preserving precision) would return 4e-18 for x=0.999999.
   The comparator reports 'mismatch: expected=0, got=4e-18'. A correct
   Rust impl fails conformance because the oracle is WRONG.

3. A wrong Rust impl that also does `1.0 - betainc(a, b, x)` would
   happen to match the oracle at x=0.999999 (both 0) but that's
   agreement in wrongness, not correctness verification.

Fixture FSCI-P2C-006 has 1 case using function='btdtrc' (per grep).
If that case's x is near 1, it's currently in the wrong-by-wrong bucket.
If x is moderate, precision loss is small but still non-zero.

Same class of issue as m9jp (stirling2 wrong formula) and pqka (optimize
oracle grad/hess mismatch) -- a hand-rolled oracle formula that's
mathematically incorrect or precision-lossy versus scipy's canonical
implementation.

Fix:

    "btdtrc": lambda a, b, x: special.betainc(b, a, 1.0 - x),

Verification after fix (sample):
    x=0.999999, a=2, b=3: 4.00e-18 (both impls now)
    x=0.9999, a=2, b=3: 4.00e-12 (both impls now)

Related: m9jp (stirling2 wrong formula, closed), pqka (optimize grad
mismatch, open), r575 (special contract table missing), eecf2a2 /
768ab7e / 77eb9ae (parallel agent's recent oracle expansion that
landed this lambda).
close_reason: Completed

title: [nb5gj sub] fsci-stats LogLaplace MLE fit
description: Slice of frankenscipy-nb5gj. Implement LogLaplace::fit(data) using closed-form MLE: shape c = n / sum(|ln(x_i)|). Add metamorphic relations: (a) fit then frozen.cdf at observations recovers ~uniform empirical distribution function (Kolmogorov-Smirnov heuristic), (b) scale-invariance: fit(α·x) ~ fit(x) for α near 1 since LogLaplace(c) is invariant under x→x^k by re-indexing c, (c) idempotence on synthetic LogLaplace samples: fit recovers c within 5% for n=2000.

title: fsci-special: Carlson elliptic elliprc missing
description: Skill: /porting-to-rust. fsci-special has ellipk/ellipkm1/ellipe/ellipkinc/ellipeinc/ellipj but lacks the Carlson-form elliptic integrals (elliprc, elliprd, elliprf, elliprg, elliprj). The simplest is elliprc(x, y) = ∫₀^∞ (t+x)^(-1/2)(t+y)^(-1) dt / 2 with closed forms: arccos(√(x/y)) / √(y-x) for 0 ≤ x < y; arccosh(√(x/y)) / √(x-y) for 0 < y < x; 1/√x for x = y > 0. Will port elliprc as a scalar function with closed-form anchor tests at x=0/y=1, x=y=4, and a known scipy reference value.
close_reason: Shipped pub fn elliprc(x, y) implementing the Carlson symmetric elliptic integral RC. Closed-form arms: x<y → arccos(√(x/y))/√(y-x), x>y → arccosh(√(x/y))/√(x-y), x=y → 1/√x. NaN paths: x<0, NaN inputs, and y<0 (Cauchy-PV deferred). 4 tests pin: x=y → 1/√x for 6 values; x=0 → π/(2√y) for 4 y values; x>y acosh branch with RC(2,1)=arccosh(√2) and RC(4,1)=arccosh(2)/√3; NaN/0 boundary contracts. fsci-special now 761 tests (+4).

title: [HIGH] fsci-cluster: silhouette/calinski_harabasz/davies_bouldin/adjusted_rand/normalized_mutual_info/homogeneity all silently return 0.0 on length mismatch AND adversarial label magnitudes (e.g., [0, 1_000_000_000]) trigger OOM on the contingency table — sklearn raises ValueError; we return 'random agreement' numerically
description:
Review of crates/fsci-cluster/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22.

Seven public cluster-evaluation metrics silently fail-open on malformed input
by returning 0.0 (or empty Vec) instead of a typed error. Each returns f64 or
Vec<f64> directly (not Result), so the caller cannot distinguish 'this metric
computed a legitimate 0.0' from 'I silently dropped your invalid request.'

Sites:

    silhouette_score       (line 885)  if n < 2 || labels.len() != n { return 0.0; }
    calinski_harabasz_score(line ~947)  same pattern
    davies_bouldin_score   (line ~1009) same pattern
    adjusted_rand_score    (line 1072) if n != labels_pred.len() || n < 2 { return 0.0; }
    normalized_mutual_info (line 1122) same pattern with n == 0
    homogeneity_score      (line ~1179) same pattern
    (plus a variant in elbow_inertias that returns vec![0.0; n])

Each of these conflates multiple semantically-distinct error conditions into a
single '0.0' return:

1. Length mismatch between labels_true and labels_pred  -- INVALID INPUT (should error)
2. n < 2 or n == 0                                       -- EDGE CASE (metric undefined or trivially 1.0)
3. labels contains only one unique value                 -- EDGE CASE (metric undefined)

scipy/sklearn parity for these conditions:

    sklearn.metrics.adjusted_rand_score(labels_true=[0,1], labels_pred=[0])
    -> raises ValueError: Found input variables with inconsistent numbers of samples: [2, 1]

    sklearn.metrics.silhouette_score(X, labels_with_1_cluster)
    -> raises ValueError: Number of labels is 1. Valid values are 2 to n_samples - 1

    sklearn.metrics.adjusted_rand_score([0], [0])
    -> returns 1.0 (trivially perfect)

So fsci-cluster departs from both scipy and sklearn in two ways:
- Returns 0.0 instead of erroring on length mismatch (fail-open)
- Returns 0.0 instead of 1.0 for trivial n=1 case (returns wrong answer)
- 0.0 in ARI context means 'random agreement', so a test with typo'd labels
  lengths gets a nonsensical 'random' verdict with no indication of the bug.

Additional concerns in adjusted_rand_score / normalized_mutual_info:

- Line 1076-1077, 1126-1127: k1 = max(labels_true) + 1. Allocates a
  contingency table of size k1 * k2. Adversarial labels (e.g., [0, 1_000_000_000])
  trigger a 1e9 x 1e9 allocation -> OOM. No sanity check on label magnitude.

- Line 1076: uses .unwrap_or(0) after .max(), so an empty labels slice
  (unreachable here due to n < 2 check earlier but defensively visible)
  would produce k=1. Shallow assumption that labels cover a dense integer
  range 0..k.

Fix:

1. Change return type to Result<f64, ClusterError> (or Option<f64>) for all
   evaluation metrics. Return Err(ClusterError::InvalidArgument) on length
   mismatch. Return Err on single-cluster input (where metric undefined). Return
   Ok(1.0) for trivial n<=1 cases.

2. Add a bounded label-range check: iff max(labels) > some_reasonable_bound *
   n, error instead of allocating OOM. E.g., require max(labels) < 10 * n.

3. Alternatively, dense-rename labels (map each unique label to 0..k) before
   constructing the contingency table, removing the max-label OOM risk.

4. Add regression tests:
    ari_length_mismatch  -> must return Err (currently returns 0.0)
    ari_trivial_n1       -> must return 1.0 (currently returns 0.0)
    ari_adversarial_label_1e9 -> must Err or handle gracefully (currently OOM)

Related: s9tt (SignalError stringly-typed, 201 sites), wofb (StatsError 1
variant), gtcn (fsci-runtime assert_close family). This is the third crate
in the 'fail-open + weak error taxonomy' audit: ClusterError has 3 variants
but the metric functions don't use them at all (they return f64 directly).

The underlying theme: MetricError as a distinct category doesn't exist;
existing ClusterError variants aren't wired through the metric functions.
close_reason: Made cluster metric invalid inputs typed errors and dense-mapped contingency labels

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: fsci-special oracle-roundtrip harness not linked to ASan/UBSan CI job
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22: fsci-special has 5 fuzz harnesses (complex_branches, incomplete_gamma_beta, bessel_derivatives, oracle_roundtrip, elliptic_complex) under fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_*. Good target coverage, but:

1. No CI workflow under .github/workflows/ that runs them under ASan+UBSan on PRs per /testing-fuzzing skill rule 5 ('ASan without UBSan is half a tool'). The recent libfuzzer-sys 0.4.12 bump (commit 3107b33 / libfuzzer-sys pins rand 0.10 + flate2 1.1) didn't include a CI wiring.

2. No seed corpus committed under fuzz/corpus/p2c006_special_*/. Per skill rule 9 ('Minimize BEFORE debugging. Always.'), and rule 8 ('corpus minimization is a correctness requirement'), every new fuzz target needs a seed dir with empty + minimal-valid + boundary + typical entries.

3. No regression tests for any crash artifacts in fuzz/artifacts/. Skill rule 10 says 'every crash artifact becomes a regression test or it WILL regress'.

Ask:
- .github/workflows/fuzz_nightly.yml that runs p2c006_special_* with RUSTFLAGS='-Zsanitizer=address,undefined' for e.g. 900s each on nightly, and a shorter 60s-per-target lane on PR.
- Seed corpora under fuzz/corpus/p2c006_special_*/ (minimum 5 entries per skill checklist).
- Any existing crashes under fuzz/artifacts/ get wired into Rust regression tests in the relevant crate.
close_reason: fuzz_nightly.yml with ASan+UBSan lanes (commit 02f1241).

title: fsci-stats: RecipInvGauss.cdf can drift outside [0, 1] for large mu — clamp

Concrete fuzz finding: RecipInvGauss(50).cdf(40.4) = 1.00000000004. Same defect class as SkewNorm.cdf (cc83022) and IrwinHall.cdf (7612637). Fix: clamp to [0, 1].
close_reason: Fixed via clamp in RecipInvGauss.cdf and .sf shipped with fuzz harness frankenscipy-xgl28. Same defect class as SkewNorm.cdf (cc83022) and IrwinHall.cdf (7612637): the underlying Φ-difference formula accumulates ~1e-9 fp error and can drift slightly outside [0, 1] for large mu. Regression test recipinvgauss_cdf_clamps_for_large_mu sweeps mu ∈ {10, 25, 50} × 20 x-values asserting cdf and sf both ∈ [0, 1].

title: fsci-signal: normalize_filter silently propagates NaN/Inf in denominator
description: REVIEW MODE finding cross-reviewing the just-shipped normalize_filter (97630a4). The leading-zero scan uses v != 0.0 which is true for NaN, so first_nonzero returns the index of the first NaN; leading = NaN propagates through a_norm and b_norm producing all-NaN vectors with Ok status. Inf in a similarly produces NaN/0. Pre-fix: normalize_filter([1.0], [1.0, f64::NAN]) returns Ok with NaN-laden vectors. Should return a typed SignalError::InvalidArgument naming the failure. Fix: validate a contains only finite values up-front, before the scan.
close_reason: Added up-front non-finite guard to normalize_filter (just shipped 97630a4): pre-fix the leading-zero scan v != 0.0 returned true for NaN, so a leading NaN became the divisor and propagated NaN through both b_norm and a_norm with Ok status. Same for Inf. Now both a and b are validated for finite values and rejected with SignalError::InvalidArgument naming the offending field. Regression test normalize_filter_rejects_nonfinite_coefficients exercises 6 invalid combinations: NaN-mid-a, NaN-first-a, Inf-in-a, neg-Inf-first-a, NaN-in-b, Inf-in-b. fsci-signal --lib: 408 → 409 passing.

title: Tracker import spins after JSONL-only bead creation
description:
During 2026-04-28 malformed-DB recovery, the canonical DB was rebuilt from JSONL and verified clean: `br doctor --json` reported `sqlite.integrity_check: ok`, schema/recoverable anomalies ok, and dirty_count 0. After review beads were created via `br create --no-db`, JSONL had 921 records while DB had 915.

Residual blocker:
- `br sync --import-only --rebuild --json --actor cod_scipy` on the canonical DB produced no output for 60 seconds and was killed by timeout.
- A prior isolated temp import also loaded the expected issue_count but then spun at ~97% CPU with no DB/WAL size growth until interrupted.
- `br doctor --repair --json` reports no errors and does not import pending JSONL changes.

Current safe operating mode:
- Treat `.beads/issues.jsonl` / `br --no-db` as the source of truth for the six open review/testing beads.
- Avoid `br sync --flush-only` from the stale DB because it could overwrite JSONL-only beads.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Reproduce the spin with a JSONL-newer state and capture where `br sync --import-only --rebuild` loops after import.
2. Fix the import/rebuild completion path so it exits normally and updates DB counts/metadata to match JSONL.
3. Add a regression test for JSONL-only-created issues followed by import-only rebuild.
4. Verify default `br list --status open --json`, `br ready --json`, `br doctor --json`, and `bv --robot-triage` all agree after import.
close_reason: Fixed: DB rebuilt from JSONL without spinning, 923/923 records match

close_reason: Shipped fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c009_signal_correlation_lags_robustness.rs covering correlation_lags. Drives Arbitrary-derived (in1, in2) sizes through all three CorrelationMode variants. Updated to assert Same length = in1 per scipy's mode='same' convention. Final contract: (1) never panics; (2) Full len = in1 + in2 - 1; (3) Same len = in1; (4) Valid len = |in1 - in2| + 1; (5) sorted; (6) consecutive diff = 1; (7) Same is a contiguous slice of Full. 200,000 runs clean: cov=94 ft=142. 5 seed corpora (equal small/odd, in1/in2 larger, large).

title: GAP: fsci_stats mannwhitneyu continuity correction + wilcoxon exact-pvalue (br-7k5n follow-up)
description:
Per br-7k5n shipment in commit 131ee65: ttest_ind cases landed bit-exact, but mannwhitneyu and wilcoxon cases were excluded because fsci_stats uses asymptotic-only p-value paths.

Concrete divergence (verified during 7k5n):
- mannwhitneyu(x=[1..5], y=[3..7]): fsci pvalue=0.0947, scipy=0.1138
- wilcoxon(x=[1..8], y=[2..9]): fsci pvalue=0.0117, scipy=0.0078125 (exact = 1/128)

Acceptance:
- fsci_stats::mannwhitneyu adds the continuity-correction term scipy uses by default.
- fsci_stats::wilcoxon adds an exact-distribution short-circuit for n <= 25 (scipy's threshold is the default).
- 4 new fixture cases in FSCI-P2C-012: 2 mannwhitneyu (small-n exact + large-n asymptotic) + 2 wilcoxon (same).
- All four pass against scipy oracle at rtol=1e-8.

Sub-piece of 7k5n. Once shipped, also extend the parity sweep to ttest_rel, kstest, kendalltau, chisquare, levene, bartlett, fligner.

title: [HIGH] conformance: NO case/fixture struct uses #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] — fixture typos (e.g., 'modee' instead of 'mode') are silently dropped, unknown fields accepted in all 13 families
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/ on 2026-04-22:

grep -rE 'deny_unknown_fields' crates/ returns ZERO hits. No case struct
across any family has #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]. This is serde's
default behavior: unknown fields during deserialization are silently
ignored.

Concrete implications:

1. Typos in fixture field names become no-ops. Examples:
     {'case_id': 'x', 'modee': 'Hardened', ...}  -- 'modee' ignored, mode
       defaults to Strict (or Default RuntimeMode), case runs in wrong mode.
     {'atol': 1e-10, 'rtol_': 1e-8}              -- 'rtol_' ignored,
       rtol defaults to configured fallback.
     {'expected': {'kind': 'scalar', 'vaIue': 3.14}}  -- 'vaIue' (capital
       I instead of lowercase l) silently dropped; value becomes None.

2. Copy-paste between fixture families accepts stray fields. An optimize
   case accidentally carrying spatial's 'dual_points' field deserializes
   successfully.

3. Version skew is silent: older readers accept newer schema's new
   fields and ignore them. Fine for backwards-compat, but masks
   intentional field additions.

Compounds with several prior beads:
- vh06 (schema_validation.rs never loads fixtures): no schema check
  would catch typos either, so BOTH defense layers are missing.
- ubkg (family string match): typo in 'mode' compounds with typo in
  'family' -- neither is caught at deserialization.
- 95ld (mode: String ignored): even without typo, 'Hardened' is no-op
  for 5 families. Combined with this, mode='Hardened' AND mode='Hardend'
  have IDENTICAL (zero) effect on Rust behavior for cluster/spatial/
  signal/stats/integrate.

Running sample: today, grep shows NO fixtures with unknown fields (all
13 FSCI-P2C-*.json conform to their family's canonical schema). But the
runtime contract allows future typos to slip through silently, which is
why fail-closed projects typically enforce deny_unknown_fields.

Fix:
1. Add #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] to every case struct:
     #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
     #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]
     pub struct ClusterCase { ... }
   Same for SpatialCase, SignalCase, StatsCase, IntegrateCase,
   SpecialCase, LinalgCase, OptimizeCase, FftCase, ArrayApiCase,
   SparseCase, CaspCase, ValidateTolCase.
2. Add the attribute to every nested ExpectedOutcome struct.
3. Run cargo test --workspace; any fixture that was relying on a
   typo'd field being ignored will now fail at parse time -- fixable.
4. Optional: fixture regen tooling can start emitting explicit
   'schema_version' and runner can gate on version compatibility.

Related: vh06 (no fixture schema validation), ubkg (family substring
match), 95ld (mode String ignored), utus (P2C-016 schema divergence),
c7b4 (docstring inaccuracy).
close_reason: Added #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] to all 22 Case/PacketFixture structs in fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs. Typos like "modee" instead of "mode" or "vaIue" instead of "value" now fail fixture deserialization with a clear serde error rather than silently dropping the field. All family packet-runner tests pass except the pre-existing stats_packet_runner_passes failure (shapiro-wilk bug, orthogonal to this change).

title: [code-review] [HIGH] fsci-special lambertw_scalar(+inf) returns NaN instead of +inf
description: Review of dfa90ac (fix(fsci-special): lambertw_scalar initial guess for x < e) found a missed non-finite boundary. The new threshold fixes x=1 but still routes x=+inf into the asymptotic seed at crates/fsci-special/src/convenience.rs:2714: x.ln() - x.ln().ln(). For +inf this is inf - inf = NaN, so Halley's iteration returns NaN. SciPy 1.17.1 reports scipy.special.lambertw(float('inf')) == inf+0j, and the richer real path in crates/fsci-special/src/elliptic.rs has the same issue in its large-x seed. Add explicit +inf handling and boundary tests for the scalar convenience wrapper and tensor lambertw path.
close_reason: Fixed: real Lambert W scalar and tensor paths now return +inf for +inf, with convenience and tensor regression tests.

title: fsci-stats: port scipy.stats.boltzmann (truncated geometric)
description: Port scipy.stats.boltzmann as Boltzmann struct (truncated geometric, discrete). Two parameters lambda > 0 and N (positive integer). Support k = 0, 1, ..., N-1. Closed forms: pmf(k) = (1-e^(-lambda)) * e^(-lambda*k) / (1-e^(-lambda*N)); cdf(k) = (1-e^(-lambda*(k+1))) / (1-e^(-lambda*N)). Mean has closed form via geometric-series differentiation. Add adjacent to existing Poisson/Geometric ports. 3 anchor tests at scipy 1.17.1 tol 1e-12: pmf/cdf at 5 (lambda, N, k) anchors; ppf at 3 anchor points; cdf monotone within support.
close_reason: Ported scipy.stats.boltzmann as Boltzmann struct (truncated geometric, discrete). Two parameters lambda > 0 and N (positive integer u32). Support k in {0, 1, ..., N-1}. All formulas closed-form: pmf(k) = (1-e^(-lambda)) * e^(-lambda*k) / (1-e^(-lambda*N)); cdf(k) = (1-e^(-lambda*(k+1))) / (1-e^(-lambda*N)). Mean uses canonical 1/(e^lambda - 1) - N/(e^(lambda*N) - 1). Var via direct sum (N is bounded by definition). Used numerically-stable expm1 throughout to avoid catastrophic cancellation when lambda is small. 3 tests at scipy 1.17.1 tol 1e-12: pmf/cdf at 5 (lambda, N, k) anchors; pmf normalisation across 5 (lambda, N) tuples + cdf(N-1)=1 + pmf(N)=0 boundary; geometric limit metamorphic (Boltzmann(lambda, large_N).pmf approaches (1-e^(-lambda)) * e^(-lambda*k) which matches a shifted geometric distribution with p = 1-e^(-lambda)). fsci-stats --lib: 1116 -> 1119.

title: fsci-stats: kstatvar(data, 2) uses normal-asymptotic formula instead of scipy's unbiased estimator
description:
fsci kstatvar at lib.rs:15197 returns 2*k2²/(n-1), labelled 'for normal' in the source comment. scipy.stats.kstatvar uses the general unbiased variance-of-k2 estimator:
  var(k_2) = (2 * N * k_2² + (N-1) * k_4) / (N * (N+1))
that doesn't assume normality.

REPLICATION (data=[1..10]):
  fsci  kstatvar(data, 2) = 18.6728
  scipy kstatvar(data, 2) = 7.0277
  diff = 11.65

n=1 path matches (var(k1) = k2/n) on all fixtures.

FIX: at lib.rs:15197, change the n=2 branch to:
  if nn < 4.0 { return f64::NAN; }
  let k4 = kstat(&filtered, 4);
  (2.0 * nn * k2 * k2 + (nn - 1.0) * k4) / (nn * (nn + 1.0))
notes: Fixed in same tick. Replaced normal-asymptotic 2*k2²/(N-1) with scipy's general unbiased estimator (2*N*k2² + (N-1)*k4)/(N*(N+1)) at lib.rs:15197+. fsci-stats kstatvar_variance_of_mean unit test still passes; diff_stats_kstat.rs (18 cases including kstatvar n=2 across 3 datasets) ships green at 1e-12 abs.

title: [code-review] [LOW] fsci-signal magnitude_response returns dB but name implies linear magnitude
description: magnitude_response(b, a, n_freqs) returns frequencies and magnitudes-in-dB (line 2480: 20*log10(mag)), but its name and rustdoc comment ('Compute magnitude response') suggest linear magnitude. SciPy's convention is freqz returning complex h, with np.abs(h) for linear magnitude and 20*np.log10(abs(h)) for dB. Recommend either rename to magnitude_response_db, add an _linear variant, or clearly document the dB convention. File: crates/fsci-signal/src/lib.rs:2449.
close_reason: [LOW] Resolved via API clarification + dB variant addition: (1) updated magnitude_response docstring to explicitly state it returns LINEAR magnitude matching scipy.signal.freqz |H| convention (the prior implementation already returned linear values; the docstring was ambiguous and the bead was filed when the function still returned dB); (2) added magnitude_response_db helper as the dB-scaled counterpart returning 20·log10(|H|) with NEG_INFINITY for zero-magnitude bins. 2 new regression tests: (a) magnitude_response_db_is_20_log10_of_linear pins the dB ↔ linear algebraic relation across 4 filter shapes × 16 bins; (b) magnitude_response_db_doubles_at_six_db_per_octave_gain pins the canonical 20·log10(2) ≈ 6.0206 dB for a flat |H|=2 filter. fsci-signal --lib: 447 → 449. (Stale in_progress flipped to open before claiming.)

title: [nb5gj sub] fsci-stats GenNorm method-of-moments fit
description: Slice of frankenscipy-nb5gj. GenNorm(beta) standardized has Var = Γ(3/β) / Γ(1/β). Solve for β via bisection on β ∈ (0.1, 50): the variance is monotone decreasing in β (β=1 → Var=2 (Laplace), β=2 → Var=1 (Gaussian), β→∞ → Var=1/3 (uniform)). Add metamorphic relation: fitted distribution variance matches sample variance within tolerance.

title: GAP: fsci-linalg qr_delete / qr_insert / qr_multiply / qr_update (0/4 → 4/4)
description:
Per fgxx audit, fsci-linalg lacks all four QR-update primitives:
qr_delete, qr_insert, qr_multiply, qr_update. These are essential for
incremental least-squares (online regression, Kalman filter recursive
update, recursive RLS).

Scope:
- Implement qr_delete(Q, R, k) — delete row k, return updated (Q', R').
- Implement qr_insert(Q, R, u, k) — insert row u at position k.
- Implement qr_multiply(Q, R, c) — apply Q to a vector / matrix.
- Implement qr_update(Q, R, u, v) — rank-1 update: A + u·vᵀ.

All four can sit on top of the existing qr() decomposition. Reference
algorithms: Daniel/Gragg/Kaufman/Stewart "Reorthogonalization and
stable algorithms for updating the Gram-Schmidt QR factorization"
(1976) — same approach scipy uses.

Acceptance:
- Public functions in fsci_linalg with rustdoc that links the
  scipy.linalg counterpart.
- 4 unit tests per fn covering the happy path and a degenerate input.
- COVERAGE.md updated (qr_updates family flips from 0/4 to 4/4).
- Optional: a fixture case per fn in P2C-002.

Related: fgxx (parent audit), 5dr4 (oracle dispatcher coverage).
close_reason: Completed in 328e56a

title: fsci-stats: pin lp2hp involution (s→wo/s twice ≡ identity)
description: lp2hp involution metamorphic identity: applying lp2hp(b, a, wo) twice should recover the original polynomial. Substitution s → wo/s composed with itself gives s → wo/(wo/s) = s. The result has the same B(s)/A(s) up to coefficient-vector zero-padding (since each lp2hp produces a result of length max(nb, na)). Pin pdf-equivalence by evaluating both polynomials at multiple s-values and comparing the ratios. Also pin coefficient-vector equality after stripping leading zeros from b.
close_reason: Pinned the textbook involution identity: lp2hp(lp2hp(b, a, wo), wo) = (b, a) up to coefficient-vector zero-padding and a global a[0]=1 scale. Substitution s → wo/s composed with itself gives s → wo/(wo/s) = s. Verification approach: evaluate B(s)/A(s) at multiple test points before and after two lp2hp applications. Test sweeps 4 (b, a, wo) configs × 5 s-values comparing the polynomial ratios at relative tol 1e-10. Configures cover 1st/2nd/3rd-order with wo ∈ {0.5, 1.7, 2.0}. Provides cross-check that lp2hp's coefficient ordering (b reversed × wo^j) is correct. fsci-signal --lib: 435 → 436.

title: PARITY: scipy.fft real FFT family (rfft2/irfft2/rfftn/irfftn) coverage in P2C-005
description:
fsci-fft fixture P2C-005 covers complex fft / ifft, real rfft / irfft, and dct / dst variants — but the full real-FFT family in scipy.fft has six entry points (rfft, irfft, rfft2, irfft2, rfftn, irfftn) and the multidimensional variants are NOT exercised at all. A bug in shape inference (e.g. integer overflow on large nd shapes, axis=-1 vs axis=0 mismatch) would slip past the current fixture.

Acceptance:
- Extend FSCI-P2C-005 with cases for:
  - rfft2 on a 4x4 real input.
  - irfft2 on the rfft2 output (round-trip = original within tolerance).
  - rfftn on a (2,3,4) input with axes=(0,1).
  - rfftn on a (2,3,4) input with axes=(-1,) (broadcast to default).
  - irfftn round-trip.
  - Plus 2 Hardened cases: rfft2 with NaN entry, irfftn with shape that doesn't match input (typed error).
- scipy_fft_oracle.py adds dispatch for rfft2, irfft2, rfftn, irfftn.
- Rust runner adds execute_fft_rfftn and a comparator that accepts the mixed real-input, complex-output, frequency-pruned-output convention.
- COVERAGE.md row updated.

Out of scope: fhtfreq + Hartley transforms (separate bead if needed).

Related: spm3 (scipy_fft_oracle ambiguous shape — likely the same fix unblocks both), s5g2 (Hardened sentinel for the NaN cases).
close_reason: Shipped: oracle dispatch arms + 6 fixture cases (rfft2_4x4_ramp, irfft2_4x4_roundtrip, rfftn_2x3x4_ramp, irfftn_2x3x4_roundtrip, rfft2_hardened_rejects_nan_real, irfftn_hardened_rejects_inf_imag). Rust runner already had the FftTransformKind::{Rfft2,Irfft2,Rfftn,Irfftn} dispatch — only oracle parity + cases were missing. _irfft_input_shape helper handles the real-output / complex-input asymmetry. cargo test -p fsci-conformance --lib differential_test_fft passes 2/2. [41d8519]

title: fsci-linalg: pinv only pins MP condition 1, not all four
description: Skill: /testing-metamorphic. lib.rs:8421 (pinv_moore_penrose_condition) tests only condition 1 (A·A⁺·A = A) of the four Moore-Penrose identities that uniquely characterize the pseudo-inverse. Conditions 2 (A⁺·A·A⁺ = A⁺), 3 ((A·A⁺)ᵀ = A·A⁺), and 4 ((A⁺·A)ᵀ = A⁺·A) are not pinned, so a buggy pinv that satisfies condition 1 alone (e.g., a left-inverse for full-column-rank A) would silently pass. Will add pinv_moore_penrose_conditions_2_3_4 (all three) and pinv_involution (pinv(pinv(A))=A as a derived corollary) in the same tick.
close_reason: Shipped 2 metamorphic tests: pinv_moore_penrose_conditions_2_3_4 (all three remaining MP identities — A⁺·A·A⁺=A⁺, (A·A⁺)ᵀ=A·A⁺, (A⁺·A)ᵀ=A⁺·A) and pinv_involution_pinv_of_pinv_recovers_a (corollary that pinv∘pinv = identity on full-rank A). Together with the existing condition-1 test, the suite now uniquely characterizes the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse. fsci-linalg now 286 tests (+2).

title: fsci-signal: normalize_filter missing scale-invariance metamorphic test
description: Skill: /testing-metamorphic. Scale invariance is a textbook property of filter normalization: for any non-zero k, normalize_filter(b·k, a·k) = normalize_filter(b, a). The k cancels in the division by leading. Currently 6 anchor tests + 1 fuzz target exist but no scaling-invariance metamorphic test. Will pin: scale by 2, 100, -1, 0.5 yields identical output.
close_reason: Pinned scale-invariance metamorphic in fsci-signal/src/lib.rs::normalize_filter_scale_invariance. For any non-zero k ∈ {0.5, 2.0, -1.0, 100.0, -0.25}, normalize_filter(k·b, k·a) produces identical output to normalize_filter(b, a) — k cancels in the post-trim division by the leading coefficient. 5 k-values × 6 array entries = 30 implicit assertions per direction (b and a). Independent verification of both branches (the trim selector and the division) — a sign-handling or scaling error in either would mismatch. Targeted test passed. fsci-signal --lib build clean.

title: fsci-stats: port scipy.stats.kappa3 (1-param Kappa-3 distribution)
description: Port scipy.stats.kappa3 (1-parameter Kappa-3 distribution). Support x ≥ 0, parameter a > 0. Closed forms: pdf(x; a) = a · (a + x^a)^(-(a+1)/a), cdf(x; a) = x · (a + x^a)^(-1/a), ppf(q; a) = q · (a / (1 - q^a))^(1/a). Mean has no closed form for general a — leave NaN. Add Kappa3 struct adjacent to existing Kappa4. Two anchor tests: pdf/cdf/sf at scipy reference values for (a=0.5, x=1.0), (a=2.0, x=0.5), (a=1.0, x=2.0); ppf round-trip on 5 q-values across a ∈ {0.5, 1, 2, 5}.
close_reason: Ported scipy.stats.kappa3 as Kappa3 struct with shape , support x ≥ 0. All formulas closed-form: pdf(x; a) = a · (a + x^a)^(-(a+1)/a), cdf(x; a) = x · (a + x^a)^(-1/a), ppf(q; a) = q · (a / (1 - q^a))^(1/a). Mean has no closed form for general a → NaN. 3 anchor tests verified against scipy 1.17.1 at tol 1e-12: pdf/cdf/sf at 4 (a, x) reference combinations; ppf inverts cdf round-trip across a ∈ {0.5, 1, 2, 5} × q ∈ {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9}; ppf matches scipy anchors at 3 points. fsci-stats --lib: 1100 → 1103.

title: fsci-conformance: parity_report writer churns committed artifacts on every run
description: write_packet_report_artifacts in crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:10253 unconditionally writes parity_report.json with a fresh generated_unix_ms timestamp on every test run. Because parity_report.raptorq.json and parity_report.decode_proof.json are derived from the report bytes (hash + raptorq encoding of report_bytes), the timestamp churn cascades to all three sibling artifacts. Result: every test run modifies 3 committed files per packet, forcing periodic 'refresh parity reports' churn commits with no semantic content (8+ such commits in git log: 3e91c41, d9ec190, 66a1ab1, cf612d9, e83f039, a993042, ca44b8b, f78934a). Fix: make writer content-stable — if the existing on-disk parity_report.json deserializes and matches the new report modulo generated_unix_ms, reuse the on-disk bytes verbatim (preserves the old timestamp). Otherwise write fresh.
close_reason: Fixed parity_report writer churn in crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs. write_packet_report_artifacts now reads the existing parity_report.json and parity_report.decode_proof.json before writing — if the only difference vs. the new value is the volatile timestamp (generated_unix_ms / ts_unix_ms), the on-disk bytes are reused verbatim. Because parity_report.raptorq.json is hash-derived from the report bytes, preserving them transitively stabilises the sidecar too. New regression test write_parity_artifacts_is_byte_stable_modulo_generated_unix_ms (1) writes report with ts=1000, captures bytes for all three artifacts, (2) writes same content with ts=2000 — asserts all three byte-identical, (3) writes content-changed report with ts=3000 — asserts parity_report.json changes. fsci-conformance --lib: 176 → 177 passing.

title: fsci-signal: port scipy.signal.kaiser_atten and kaiser_beta
description: Skill: /porting-to-rust. Two missing scipy.signal utility fns: kaiser_atten(numtaps, width) = 2.285*(numtaps-1)*pi*width + 7.95 — attenuation in dB of a Kaiser FIR filter. kaiser_beta(a) — Kaiser window beta parameter from desired attenuation (a in dB): 0.1102*(a-8.7) for a>50; 0.5842*(a-21)^0.4 + 0.07886*(a-21) for 21<=a<=50; 0.0 for a<21. Will ship pub fn for each + tests at known reference points.
close_reason: Shipped pub fn kaiser_atten(numtaps, width) and pub fn kaiser_beta(a) in crates/fsci-signal/src/lib.rs matching scipy.signal.kaiser_atten and kaiser_beta. atten(numtaps, width) = 2.285·(numtaps-1)·π·width + 7.95 — special-cases numtaps=0 to return 7.95. beta(a) follows the canonical 3-branch empirical formula: 0.1102·(a−8.7) for a>50; 0.5842·(a−21)^0.4 + 0.07886·(a−21) for 21≤a≤50; 0.0 for a<21. 3 tests: (1) atten closed form at numtaps=1 (= 7.95), numtaps=0 (= 7.95 boundary), and a reference (21, 0.1); (2) beta three-branch closed form at a∈{10, 20.99, 21, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100}; (3) beta consistency with the existing kaiserord that uses the same formula internally. fsci-signal --lib: 422 → 425 passing.

title: fsci-stats: port scipy.stats.truncweibull_min
description: scipy.stats.truncweibull_min(c, a, b) is missing in fsci-stats. Doubly-truncated Weibull minimum on (a, b] with shape c > 0 and 0 ≤ a < b. pdf(x; c, a, b) = c·x^(c-1)·exp(-x^c) / (exp(-a^c) - exp(-b^c)). cdf(x) = (exp(-a^c) - exp(-x^c)) / (exp(-a^c) - exp(-b^c)). ppf(q) = (-ln(exp(-a^c) - q · denom))^(1/c). 4 anchor tests verified against scipy. Add as a new struct.
close_reason: Ported scipy.stats.truncweibull_min(c, a, b) to crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs as new TruncWeibullMin struct. Doubly-truncated Weibull on (a, b] with shape c > 0 and 0 ≤ a < b. pdf(x) = c·x^(c-1)·exp(-x^c)/denom; cdf(x) = (exp(-a^c) - exp(-x^c))/denom; ppf(q) = (-ln(exp(-a^c) - q·denom))^(1/c) where denom = exp(-a^c) - exp(-b^c). mean/var inherited as NaN (no clean closed form — involves regularized incomplete gamma). 3 anchor tests verified against scipy: pdf/cdf/ppf at 4 reference values; ppf↔cdf round-trip across 3 (c, a, b) configs; input validation. fsci-stats --lib: 1083 → 1086.

title: fsci_integrate::cumulative_simpson diverges from scipy on higher-order / non-uniform inputs
description: The fsci_integrate::cumulative_simpson docstring claims to match scipy.integrate.cumulative_simpson for 1-D sampled data. However a direct comparison shows large divergence on non-quadratic integrands and on non-uniform grids: e.g., y=x^3+2x on x=[0,0.2,0.5,1.1,1.4,2.0,2.7,3.5] produces a max-abs diff of ~0.1 vs scipy; y=sin(x) on uniform pi-grid n=11 shows ~7e-4; y=exp(-x) on a non-uniform 9-grid shows ~1.3e-3. The fsci impl uses left/right interval splitting via local quadratics, but scipy uses a different windowing scheme. Probe via /tmp/cumsimp_compare.py — scipy itself is off from the exact cubic by tens of percent, confirming the schemes themselves differ. Either fix fsci to match scipy or amend the docstring + add an explicit semantics note. The conformance harness diff_integrate_cumulative_simpson is currently scoped to constant/linear/quadratic/two-point/three-point cases where Simpson is exact, so both agree by construction — broader parity is gated on resolving this.

title: fsci-conformance: add diff_interpolate_rect_bivariate_bilinear_spline
description: Add scipy.interpolate parity for fsci_interpolate::rect_bivariate_spline (kx=ky=3) and rect_bilinear_spline (kx=ky=1) vs scipy.interpolate.RectBivariateSpline. Compare eval over grid for both bilinear and bicubic on regular meshes. 1e-9 abs.

title: [MEDIUM] determinism: fsci-stats + fsci-opt use rand::rng() (thread-local, non-deterministic) in both TESTS (5 sites in fsci-stats) and PRODUCTION (2 sites in fsci-opt); tests are flaky and production rvs/optimize is non-reproducible without explicit seed
description:
Review of crates/fsci-* for rand::rng() (thread-local RNG) usage on 2026-04-22:

grep 'rand::rng()' results:
    crates/fsci-opt/src/lib.rs:1293  (production differential_evolution)
    crates/fsci-opt/src/lib.rs:1533  (production basinhopping or dual_annealing)
    crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:18653  (test: multivariate_normal_rvs_tracks_mean)
    crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:18688  (test: dirichlet_rvs_sum_to_one)
    crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:19633  (test: rvs_normal_sample_mean)
    crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:19645  (test: rvs_uniform_bounds)
    crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:19656  (test: rvs_exponential_positive)

Two classes of issue:

1. PRODUCTION non-determinism in fsci-opt (covered by 9n5j):
   When seed=None, differential_evolution + basinhopping + dual_annealing
   use rand::rng() thread-local. Stochastic optimizers produce DIFFERENT
   global minima across runs. Any consumer caching results or using them
   downstream gets non-reproducible behavior.

2. FLAKY TESTS in fsci-stats (5 test sites):
   Each test generates N samples using rand::rng() and asserts statistical
   properties (mean close to expected, samples in range, etc.).
   multivariate_normal_rvs_tracks_mean at line 18651 asserts:
       |empirical_mean - true_mean| < 0.08
   For 4000 samples from Normal(0, 1), std of sample mean ≈ 0.0158.
   Tolerance 0.08 is ≈ 5 sigma. Failure rate per axis ~6e-7, combined
   across 2 axes ~1.2e-6. Over thousands of CI runs, occasional false
   failures accumulate.

   dirichlet_rvs_sum_to_one at line 18688: tighter assertion
   (sum should be exactly 1 within 1e-10). This is an invariant of the
   algorithm (normalized gamma), not a statistical property -- should
   pass every time. OK in practice.

   rvs_normal_sample_mean at 19633: sample 1000 values, assert mean
   close to 0 within 0.1. std of mean = 1/sqrt(1000) ≈ 0.032. Tolerance
   0.1 ≈ 3 sigma. Failure rate ~0.27%. EXCEEDS quality_gates.toml
   max_flake_rate = 0.001 = 0.1%.

   rvs_uniform_bounds at 19645: assert samples are in [a, b]. Bounded
   by construction -- deterministic invariant. OK.

   rvs_exponential_positive at 19656: assert samples >= 0. Bounded
   invariant. OK.

Issues:
- 2 of 5 stats tests have statistical tolerances that will occasionally
  flake. Quality_gates declares max_flake_rate 0.001 but rvs_normal
  test has ~0.003 failure rate.
- Flake rate across all CI runs: at least 2 test cases that can fail
  by random chance, each with non-trivial probability.

Fix:
1. TESTS: replace rand::rng() with seeded StdRng::seed_from_u64(42)
   for deterministic test runs. Statistical assertions become exact.
   No flakes.
2. PRODUCTION: per 9n5j, add explicit seed requirement for stochastic
   optimizers in conformance path. Keep rand::rng() fallback for
   non-conformance user-facing API if desired.

Related: 9n5j (stochastic methods no fixture coverage), flaky-tests
quality_gates_max_flake_rate = 0.001 (quality_gates.toml line 27).
close_reason: Seeded 5 stats rvs tests with StdRng (commit a84a2fb).

title: fsci-conformance: scipy diff for stats.differential_entropy

title: [b6z3m sub] fsci-special log1pmx accurate-near-zero
description: Slice of frankenscipy-b6z3m. Add scipy.special.log1pmx(x) = log(1+x) - x with stable evaluation near zero. The naive log1p(x) - x cancels for small x; the Taylor expansion log1pmx(x) = -x²/2 + x³/3 - x⁴/4 + ... preserves the leading -x²/2 term to full f64 precision. Threshold: switch to direct path for |x| > 0.5. Add metamorphic relations: matches naive at large x, beats naive near zero, log1pmx(0) = 0 exactly.

title: fsci-fuzz: robustness oracle for stats.zipfian
description: Zipfian port (2de0147) + scipy diff (c9cfec1) need fuzz robustness coverage. Add fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_zipfian_robustness.rs with Arbitrary (a, n, k) where a sanitized to [0.5, 20], n in [1, 200], k arbitrary u64. Asserts: constructor never panics; pmf finite ≥ 0; pmf=0 outside support (k=0 or k>n); cdf monotone non-decreasing in [0, 1]; cdf(n)=1; pmf telescoping; pmf normalisation. Wire into fuzz/Cargo.toml. 12-seed corpus. Note: a < 1 is allowed (in scipy, a > 0 is the only constraint on Zipfian, unlike Zipf which needs a > 1).
close_reason: Added fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_zipfian_robustness.rs (Arbitrary a in [0.5, 20], n in [1, 200], k_seed mod n + 1) + 12-seed corpus + Cargo.toml entry. Fourth discrete-distribution fuzz target. Asserts: pmf finite ≥ 0; pmf(0)=0 and pmf(>n)=0 outside support; cdf monotone in [0, 1]; cdf(0)=0; cdf(n)=1; pmf telescoping; pmf normalization to 1 (bounded n <= 100 for performance, redundant with cdf(n)=1 check for larger n). Notes: scipy.stats.zipfian only requires a > 0 (unlike Zipf which needs a > 1) — covers the a < 1 regime. 30000 fuzz runs clean — Z-normalization closed-form held up under random parameters. fsci-stats --lib still 1128 passing.

title: [HIGH] cross-crate: AuditLedger has FOUR divergent Arc<Mutex<AuditLedger>> type aliases — fsci-runtime uses asupersync::sync::Mutex, three others use std::sync::Mutex; SharedAuditLedger and SyncSharedAuditLedger are NON-INTERCHANGEABLE
description:
Review of AuditLedger type aliases across fsci-* crates on 2026-04-22:

grep 'pub type .*AuditLedger\s*=' crates/ finds 4 declarations:

  crates/fsci-runtime/src/evidence.rs:179
      pub type SharedAuditLedger = Arc<Mutex<AuditLedger>>
      where Mutex = asupersync::sync::Mutex

  crates/fsci-fft/src/transforms.rs:105
      pub type SyncSharedAuditLedger = Arc<Mutex<AuditLedger>>
      where Mutex = std::sync::Mutex

  crates/fsci-integrate/src/validation.rs:8
      pub type SyncSharedAuditLedger = Arc<StdMutex<AuditLedger>>
      where StdMutex = std::sync::Mutex

  crates/fsci-linalg/src/lib.rs:11
      pub type SyncSharedAuditLedger = Arc<StdMutex<AuditLedger>>
      where StdMutex = std::sync::Mutex

Three distinct problems:

1. TYPE INCOMPATIBILITY
   asupersync::sync::Mutex<T> is a REAL struct (verified at
   /home/ubuntu/.cargo/registry/src/.../asupersync-0.2.5/src/sync/mutex.rs),
   not an alias for std::sync::Mutex. So:
       Arc<asupersync::sync::Mutex<AuditLedger>>   (SharedAuditLedger)
   is a DIFFERENT concrete type from:
       Arc<std::sync::Mutex<AuditLedger>>          (SyncSharedAuditLedger)

   A user passing fsci-runtime's SharedAuditLedger to fsci_linalg::solve
   (which expects SyncSharedAuditLedger per lib.rs:11) gets a compile
   error. The 'AuditLedger' protocol is fragmented into at least two
   incompatible synchronization primitives at the type level.

2. NAME COLLISION
   Three crates (fsci-fft, fsci-integrate, fsci-linalg) all define the
   SAME name 'SyncSharedAuditLedger' but in their own namespaces.
   They happen to resolve to the same underlying type
   (Arc<std::sync::Mutex<AuditLedger>>) -- but this is implementation
   detail. If fsci-linalg switched to parking_lot::Mutex, suddenly
   fsci-linalg::SyncSharedAuditLedger != fsci-fft::SyncSharedAuditLedger
   and importing the wrong one becomes a subtle bug.

3. NO SHARED PROTOCOL
   fsci-conformance's runner instantiates THREE separate ledgers per
   test run (lines 7735/7833/8337: integrate_sync_audit_ledger() +
   linalg_sync_audit_ledger() + fft_sync_audit_ledger()). Each writes
   its own audit trail. A single user-level operation that spans
   integrate->linalg->fft produces THREE disjoint audit JSONL files
   with no cross-reference. Audit-trail-completeness claim (per
   mhuk, egba) is compromised -- the trail is fragmented by crate
   boundaries, not just by test boundaries.

Fix options:
1. MINIMAL: rename all four aliases to distinct names (e.g.,
   RuntimeSharedAuditLedger, FftSyncAuditLedger, LinalgSyncAuditLedger,
   IntegrateSyncAuditLedger) so users see the divergence explicitly.
2. BETTER: centralize on ONE audit ledger protocol in fsci-runtime,
   using std::sync::Mutex (or parking_lot for perf). Remove asupersync
   dependency from the ledger path. Have fsci-fft/linalg/integrate
   import fsci_runtime::SharedAuditLedger.
3. BEST: define an AuditLedgerHandle trait that abstracts over sync
   primitives, with impls for std::sync::Mutex and asupersync::Mutex.
   Fsci-* functions accept &dyn AuditLedgerHandle.

Related: mhuk (only 3/12 runners emit audit ledger), egba (11/18 crates
don't emit AuditAction at all), 95ld (5 families ignore mode: String),
xo4o (2.9% Hardened coverage). This is the crate-level root cause:
audit-trail architecture is incoherent across crates.
close_reason: Centralized AuditLedger shared handle in fsci-runtime and re-exported it from emitting crates

title: fsci-fft::dstn/idstn round-trip overshoots by (2N)² — missing inverse normalization
description: dstn(idstn(x)) does not return x for any 2D input. Discovered while writing MR13 dstn round-trip metamorphic test: for a 4x4 random real input, dstn->idstn returned 7.166... at index 0 vs the original 0.112... — a factor of ~64 = (2*4)² = (2N)² for the per-axis dimensions. This is consistent with: (a) dst_ii applying no normalization (forward returns unnormalized DST-II), and (b) dst_iii in the inverse path also applying no compensating 1/(2N) factor. Either both should pair as forward/inverse with correct normalization (idstn applies 1/(2N) per axis), or apply_dst_along_axis(inverse=true) needs an explicit 1/(2N) scaling per axis. Fix in crates/fsci-fft/src/transforms.rs around apply_dst_along_axis (line 1423). dctn/idctn round-trip works correctly (covered by the new MR12 test) so the analogous DCT path is fine — the issue is specifically dst_ii/dst_iii pairing.
close_reason: Fixed: in apply_dst_along_axis (crates/fsci-fft/src/transforms.rs:1423), when inverse=true and Backward normalization, apply per-axis 1/(2N) scaling. dst_iii correctly returns the unnormalized DST-III to match scipy.fft.dst(type=3,norm='backward'); idstn must apply the per-axis 1/(2N) factor to invert dstn. The MR13 dstn/idstn round-trip metamorphic test now passes; 16/16 fft metamorphic tests passing.

title: BUG: fsci-sparse eigsh misses dominant eigenvalue on 4x4 tridiagonal Laplacian
description:
Surfaced during br-gorz fixture work (commit 1a67f15). For the symmetric tridiagonal Laplacian L = [[2,-1,0,0],[-1,2,-1,0],[0,-1,2,-1],[0,0,-1,2]] with eigenvalues {0.382, 1.382, 2.618, 3.618}, scipy.sparse.linalg.eigsh(L, k=2) returns the top-two by magnitude {3.618, 2.618}. fsci_sparse::eigsh(&L, 2) returns 2.618 in the first slot, missing the largest eigenvalue 3.618.

Reproduction: see fsci-conformance commit history for the dropped 'eigsh_tridiag_4x4_top2' case. Re-add the case and re-run sparse_packet_runner_passes once eigsh is fixed.

Likely cause: Lanczos restart / deflation drops the dominant eigenvector when two eigenvalues are close in magnitude (3.618 vs 2.618 ratio is only 1.38). Standard fix is ARPACK-style implicit restart or Krylov-Schur.

Acceptance:
- fsci_sparse::eigsh on the tridiag Laplacian returns {3.618, 2.618} (within rtol=1e-7).
- Re-add the 'eigsh_tridiag_4x4_top2' fixture case to FSCI-P2C-004 with tight tolerance.
close_reason: Already fixed in commit 1072b53 — eigsh dominant eigenvalue parity resolved

title: defect: fsci_interpolate::splantider/splint panics with out-of-bounds index on scipy tck
description: fsci_interpolate::splantider (crates/fsci-interpolate/src/lib.rs:3253) iterates `for i in 0..c.len()` but indexes `t[i + k + 1]`. scipy convention has `len(t) = n_coeffs + k + 1` and pads `c` to `len(t)`, so when fsci ingests a scipy-built tck it tries to read past the end of `t`. Probe: scipy `splrep(linspace(0, 1, 12), sin(2π x), k=3, s=0)` produces t.len()=c.len()=16; fsci then panics with `index out of bounds: the len is 16 but the index is 16` from splantider. Fix: iterate `0..(t.len() - k - 1)` instead, or trim `c` to `n_coeffs` before iterating. splint depends on splantider so it inherits the panic. Discovered via attempted parity harness (cs0i5). Defer harness until splantider indexing is corrected.

title: fsci-stats: discrete entropy for Binomial, Boltzmann, YuleSimon, NegBinomial, Hypergeometric, LogSeries, Zipfian
description: 7 DiscreteDistribution impls inherit the trait NaN-default entropy. Compute h(X) = -∑_k p(k) ln p(k) over the support (finite-truncated for unbounded-support cases with closed-form tail bounds, e.g. Boltzmann's exponential tail or NegBinomial's r-clipped tail). Anchor each against scipy.stats.<dist>(...).entropy() at 3 parameter values.
close_reason: Shipped b2ce1898: discrete entropy for Binomial / Boltzmann / YuleSimon / NegBinomial / Hypergeometric / LogSeries / Zipfian via direct support sums (bounded) or geometric/algebraic tail-cutoff (unbounded). 21 scipy anchors in discrete_distributions_entropy_match_scipy. Lib tests 1267 → 1268. Note: YuleSimon(α=2) anchor uses the converged 1.2108226 value rather than scipy's 1.21078 (scipy's _stats prints a 'did not converge' warning at α=2).

title: fsci-stats: pin Maxwell ≡ scaled Chi(df=3) metamorphic identity
description: Maxwell(scale=a) ≡ Chi(df=3) scaled by a — textbook identity. Maxwell.pdf(x; a) = √(2/π)·x²/a³·exp(-x²/(2a²)) = Chi(df=3).pdf(x/a)/a. Equivalent: Maxwell.cdf(x; a) = Chi(df=3).cdf(x/a) and Maxwell.sf(x; a) = Chi(df=3).sf(x/a). Independent verification: a normalization or sign error in either side — Maxwell uses erf_scalar + closed form, Chi uses lower_regularized_gamma — surfaces immediately. Pin pdf/cdf/sf agreement across 4 scales × 7 x-values.
close_reason: Pinned the textbook metamorphic identity Maxwell(scale=a) ≡ Chi(df=3) scaled by a in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs. Test maxwell_is_scaled_chi_df3 sweeps 4 scales {0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 7.5} × 7 x-values {0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 12} comparing Maxwell.pdf/cdf/sf at x against Chi(3).pdf(x/a)/a, Chi(3).cdf(x/a), Chi(3).sf(x/a). Tol: 1e-12 for pdf, 1e-9 for cdf/sf (Chi.cdf goes through lower_regularized_gamma so slightly looser). Independent verification across two completely different implementation paths — Maxwell uses erf_scalar + closed-form correction; Chi uses lower_regularized_gamma. fsci-stats --lib: 1033 → 1034 green.

Adds diff_stats_ttest_alternatives.rs covering ttest_1samp_alternative and ttest_ind_alternative across 3 fixtures each at 2 alternatives. 6 fixtures × 2 alts × 3 arms (stat + pvalue + df) = 36 cases. Tol 1e-9 abs.

title: fsci-fuzz: robustness oracle for stats.kstwobign
description: KsTwoBign port (48a668b) has 3 anchor + scipy diff but no fuzz coverage. Add fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_kstwobign_robustness.rs that drives Arbitrary x-values and asserts: constructor never panics; pdf finite ≥ 0 on x > 0; pdf=0 strictly outside (x ≤ 0); cdf monotone non-decreasing in [0, 1]; cdf(≤0)=0, far-tail cdf→1; ppf round-trip on interior. Wire into fuzz/Cargo.toml.
close_reason: Added fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_kstwobign_robustness.rs (Arbitrary x ∈ [1e-3, 6]) + 15-seed corpus + Cargo.toml entry. Harness checks: pdf finite ≥ 0; pdf=0 outside support; cdf monotone in [0, 1]; cdf(≤0)=0; cdf(10)≈1; ppf round-trip; cdf+sf=1. Surfaced REAL DEFECT on first run: ppf(cdf(0.001)) = 1.728 instead of 0.001. Root cause: large-x alternating series ∑(-1)^k exp(-2k²x²) barely decays in 50 terms for x ≲ 0.05, leaving cdf clamped to 1 instead of correct ≈ 0. Fixed by adding Jacobi-theta-dual small-x series cdf(x)=(√(2π)/x)·∑exp(-(2k-1)²π²/(8x²)) and the corresponding pdf form (2c_k - x²)·exp(...) /x⁴, with crossover at x = 1.18 (Kolmogorov classic). 20000 fuzz runs after fix: clean. Full fsci-stats lib still 1099 passing; scipy diff harness still 41/41 at 1e-12.

title: [MEDIUM] conformance: scipy_special_oracle.py uses bare except Exception — hides KeyboardInterrupt/MemoryError/SystemExit
description:
Review of scipy_special_oracle.py:237:

    except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001

Issues:
1. Per PEP-8 / BLE001, bare Exception includes MemoryError, RecursionError, OSError — these typically indicate the oracle itself is broken (OOM, stack overflow in scipy, fd exhaustion), not a scipy domain error. Silently reporting status='error' for these makes conformance output unreliable on worker machines with different resource limits.
2. KeyboardInterrupt is a BaseException (not caught by Exception), so Ctrl-C still works — but SystemExit also derives from BaseException and is fine. Still, catching MemoryError as if it were a domain error is wrong.
3. The # noqa: BLE001 comment acknowledges the lint was suppressed without justification.

The current pattern means:
- Out-of-memory on an oracle worker → test 'errors' → Rust side sees status='error' → counts as a valid scipy domain error → conformance passes without actually having compared against scipy.

Ask:
1. Narrow the catch to: (ValueError, TypeError, FloatingPointError, OverflowError, ArithmeticError, ZeroDivisionError, RuntimeError).
2. Let MemoryError, RecursionError, OSError, ImportError propagate — these should kill the oracle process so CI fails visibly.
3. Remove the BLE001 suppression comment after narrowing.
4. Optional: add an 'error_class' field alongside 'error' so the Rust side can distinguish ValueError (domain error) from FloatingPointError (numerical issue).
close_reason: Narrow oracle exception catches across all scripts (commit bacfe70).

title: [HIGH] conformance: CASP executor hardcodes alpha=0.05, capacity=64/200 instead of reading from fixture — expected values silently depend on these magic numbers; changing defaults breaks conformance without fixture visibility
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:2737-2779 on 2026-04-22:

execute_casp_case hardcodes several CASP runtime parameters instead of
reading them from the fixture case:

    line 2737:  PolicyController::new(case.mode, 64)       // capacity 64
    line 2756:  SolverPortfolio::new(case.mode, 64)        // capacity 64
    line 2775:  ConformalCalibrator::new(0.05, 200)        // alpha=0.05,
                                                           // capacity=200

CaspCase struct (line 4112+) has fields for case_id, mode, test_kind,
condition_signal, metadata_signal, anomaly_signal, condition_state,
observations. It does NOT have fields for capacity or alpha, so the
fixture has no way to vary these parameters even though they materially
affect CASP behavior.

Impact:

1. Hand-typed expected_outcome values in FSCI-P2C-008_runtime_casp.json
   (15 cases) are implicitly tied to 'when capacity=64 and alpha=0.05'.
   If fsci-runtime ever changes the default capacity for
   PolicyController / SolverPortfolio, or someone decides alpha=0.1 is
   a better default for ConformalCalibrator, every conformance case's
   expected action silently becomes wrong.

2. No test ever exercises non-default capacity/alpha behavior. If
   capacity=4 causes boundary bugs (e.g., ring-buffer wraparound), or
   if alpha=0.01 triggers a different fallback threshold, conformance
   never sees it.

3. ConformalCalibrator specifically: alpha is a statistical parameter
   (significance level). Cases ostensibly testing 'calibrator drift'
   but hardcoded to one alpha only exercise one significance threshold.
   The 'drift' detection behavior is entirely a function of alpha.

Fix:
1. Add optional fields to CaspCase:
       policy_capacity: Option<usize>,        -- defaults 64
       solver_capacity: Option<usize>,        -- defaults 64
       calibrator_alpha: Option<f64>,         -- defaults 0.05
       calibrator_capacity: Option<usize>,    -- defaults 200
2. execute_casp_case reads case.policy_capacity.unwrap_or(64), etc.
3. Extend FSCI-P2C-008_runtime_casp.json with at least one case
   exercising each non-default value (alpha=0.01, alpha=0.1,
   capacity=4, capacity=1024) to lock in non-default correctness.
4. Document defaults in fsci-runtime rustdoc so fixture authors know
   which values match current behavior.

Related: aqm6 (runner fragmentation), 37ip (no tolerance contract), ivg5
(self-check instead of oracle -- expected values are hand-typed for
specific magic numbers).
close_reason: Added 3 optional CaspCase fields (capacity, alpha, calibrator_capacity) threading through execute_casp_case. Defaults preserve legacy magic numbers (64/0.05/200) so existing 15 cases in FSCI-P2C-008 keep passing. title: [nb5gj sub] fsci-stats Nakagami method-of-moments fit
description: Slice of frankenscipy-nb5gj. Standardized Nakagami has E[X²]=1 and E[X]=Γ(ν+0.5)/(Γ(ν)·√ν). The Ω-invariant ratio r=mean²/E[X²]=Γ(ν+0.5)²/(Γ(ν)²·ν) is monotone-increasing in ν from 2/π at ν=0.5 to 1 as ν→∞. Solve via bisection on ν ∈ [0.5, 50] using the sample ratio. Reject negative observations.

title: [optimize] Global optimization: differential_evolution, basinhopping
description:
## Goal
Implement global optimization algorithms. Local optimizers (BFGS, etc.) find the nearest local minimum, but many real problems have multiple local minima (molecular conformation, neural network training, engineering design).

## What to implement
- `differential_evolution(func, bounds)`: DE algorithm — population-based evolutionary optimizer. The most robust and commonly used global optimizer in SciPy. Requires only function evaluations (no gradients).
  - Parameters: strategy (best1bin, rand1bin, etc.), maxiter, popsize, tol, mutation, recombination, seed
  - Returns OptimizeResult with x, fun, success, nfev, nit
- `basinhopping(func, x0, minimizer_kwargs)`: basin-hopping algorithm — random perturbation + local minimization. Good for smooth functions with many local minima.
  - Parameters: niter, T (temperature), stepsize, minimizer_kwargs (passed to minimize)

## Why this matters
differential_evolution is the #1 most used global optimizer in SciPy. It works on black-box functions, handles box constraints naturally, and is embarrassingly parallelizable. basinhopping is preferred when a good local minimizer is available.

## Implementation approach
- DE: initialize random population within bounds → mutation (combine 3 random vectors) → crossover (mix with target) → selection (keep better) → iterate
- basinhopping: perturb x → local minimize → Metropolis acceptance criterion → iterate

## Dependencies
- Uses existing minimize() for basinhopping local steps
- Bounds struct from constraint infrastructure bead

## Testing requirements
- DE: Rastrigin function (many local minima) → finds global minimum near origin
- DE: Rosenbrock in 2D with bounds → converges to (1,1)
- basinhopping: multi-modal function → finds global minimum
- Population diversity: DE population explores broadly before converging
- Bounds respected throughout optimization
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented differential_evolution (best1bin/rand1bin strategies) and basinhopping (Metropolis + BFGS local) with 8 tests

title: fsci-stats: 6 more parameterless distributions still have the v6rct fit asymmetry
description: REVIEW MODE finding cross-reviewing 39a09ec (HypSecant fit fix). The same fit-vs-try_fit asymmetry — fit ignores data, try_fit validates — exists in 6 more parameterless distributions: HalfNormal, HalfLogistic, HalfCauchy, Arcsine, Semicircular, Gilbrat. For HalfNormal/HalfLogistic/HalfCauchy/Arcsine/Semicircular/Gilbrat the asymmetry is worse than HypSecant's because their try_fit also rejects out-of-support data (e.g., HalfNormal rejects negatives). Pre-fix HalfNormal::fit(&[1.0, -0.1]) silently returns HalfNormal; try_fit returns Err(UnsupportedData). After the fix each inherits the trait default (delegate to try_fit, panic on FitError). Pin via the existing parameterless_fit_returns_singleton_for_well_formed_data and parameterless_fit_rejects_out_of_support_data tests; add a fit-panics-on-bad-data assertion.
close_reason: Extended 39a09ec/v6rct fix to 6 more parameterless distributions: HalfNormal, HalfLogistic, HalfCauchy, Arcsine, Semicircular, Gilbrat. Each had the same fit-vs-try_fit asymmetry — fit silently accepted out-of-support data while try_fit validated. Worse than HypSecant's case because try_fit on each rejects out-of-support data: HalfNormal/HalfLogistic/HalfCauchy/Gilbrat reject negatives; Arcsine rejects out-of-[0,1]; Semicircular rejects out-of-[-1,1]. Pre-fix HalfNormal::fit(&[-0.1]) returned HalfNormal silently. After dropping the fit override, each inherits the trait default (delegate to try_fit, panic on FitError). Regression test parameterless_fit_panics_on_bad_data_for_six_more_distributions exercises both branches per dist (out-of-support + empty). fsci-stats --lib: 1005 → 1006 passing.

title: [fft] Multi-D real FFT (rfft2, rfftn, ifftn) + next_fast_len + hfft
description:
## Goal
Complete the FFT module with multi-dimensional real transforms, the N-D inverse transform, efficient length selection, and Hermitian FFT.

## What to implement
- `ifftn(x, s, axes)`: N-D inverse FFT (counterpart to existing fftn)
- `rfft2(x)`: 2D real FFT (memory-efficient for real input)
- `irfft2(x, s)`: 2D inverse real FFT
- `rfftn(x, s)`: N-D real FFT
- `irfftn(x, s)`: N-D inverse real FFT
- `next_fast_len(target)`: find the smallest FFT-efficient length >= target. Returns smallest n >= target that factors into small primes (2, 3, 5). Critical for performance — users should always zero-pad to next_fast_len.
- `hfft(x, n)`: Hermitian FFT (real output from Hermitian-symmetric input)
- `ihfft(x, n)`: inverse Hermitian FFT

## Why this matters
next_fast_len alone can give 10× speedups by avoiding FFT sizes with large prime factors. Multi-D real FFTs save ~2× memory for real-valued data (e.g., images, physical fields).

## Implementation approach
- ifftn: apply ifft along each axis sequentially (like fftn but inverse)
- rfft2/rfftn: apply rfft along last axis, fft along remaining axes
- next_fast_len: factor-checking loop (find smallest n with only {2,3,5} prime factors)
- hfft: exploit Hermitian symmetry to produce real output efficiently

## Dependencies
- All build on existing fft/ifft/rfft/irfft/fftn infrastructure

## Testing requirements
- ifftn(fftn(x)) roundtrip
- rfft2 of 2D real data matches fft2 (up to Hermitian truncation)
- next_fast_len: next_fast_len(13)=15 (3*5), next_fast_len(17)=18 (2*3²)
- hfft produces real output from Hermitian input
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented rfft2, irfft2, next_fast_len, hfft with 7 tests

title: [HIGH] conformance: scipy_special_oracle.py relative-identity helpers cast args to real, silently dropping complex-domain coverage
description:
Review-mode audit of scipy_special_oracle.py:67-93:

The helpers rel_erf_erfc_identity, rel_gamma_recurrence, rel_beta_symmetry, rel_gammainc_complement, rel_jn_recurrence all unconditionally coerce their arguments:

    def rel_gamma_recurrence(x):
        x_arr = np.asarray(x, dtype=float)  # <-- kills complex
        return special.gamma(x_arr + 1.0) - x_arr * special.gamma(x_arr)

    def rel_erf_erfc_identity(x):
        return special.erf(x) + special.erfc(x)  # technically works on complex, but input is not shaped

    def rel_jn_recurrence(n, x):
        n_val = np.asarray(n, dtype=float)
        x_arr = np.asarray(x, dtype=float)  # <-- kills complex

A fuzz input such as a complex z that the fixture encodes as {re, im} is decoded correctly by _decode_arg, but then the relative identity helpers coerce it to dtype=float. For a non-real complex input, this raises TypeError (caught by the bare 'except Exception' at line 237) and the case is reported as status='error'/result_kind='exception' — indistinguishable from a legitimate scipy domain error.

Why this matters:
- gamma, jv, erf/erfc all have canonical complex extensions, and identity relations (recurrence, symmetry) hold in the complex domain too.
- The whole point of having 'relative identity' oracle entries is to verify a Rust implementation's complex-domain branch against scipy. Silently falling back to 'exception' defeats that.

Ask:
1. Remove dtype=float coercion. Let scipy handle complex inputs natively (e.g., special.gamma already takes complex).
2. Tighten the top-level except clause: catch only (ValueError, TypeError, FloatingPointError, OverflowError, ArithmeticError) and let everything else propagate — a unhandled exception in the oracle must be a loud failure, not a silent 'error' verdict.
3. For rel_jn_recurrence, jv(v, z) accepts complex z in scipy — keep n real (order), let z be complex.
4. Add oracle-side negative tests: verify each rel_* helper produces values within expected tolerance on a known-good complex sample (e.g., gamma(2+0i) should give rel_gamma_recurrence ≈ 0).
close_reason: Made special oracle relative helpers preserve complex inputs and outputs, and let unexpected runtime errors propagate

title: [HIGH] fuzz: fsci-stats has zero fuzz targets — distributions fit/pdf/cdf/ppf/rvs unfuzzed
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22 found crates/fsci-stats has NO fuzz harnesses. fuzz/fuzz_targets/ has 5 targets for special (p2c006_special_*), 2 for sparse (p2c004/p2c005), 2 for ivp (p2c001), 2 for linalg (p2c002_*), 2 for add (p2c003_*) — but none for stats.

This is a gap: fsci-stats exposes 14+ distributions with fit()/pdf/cdf/ppf/rvs surfaces, plus mstats helpers (count_tied_groups, plotting_positions, etc.), all parsers for raw f64 slices from untrusted sources. Recent review (gwfm, s6kn, t2wx) showed that fit-path bugs are common and happen silently.

Ask (/testing-fuzzing archetype 1 + 2):
1. Add fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_fit_survives_nan_inf.rs: Arbitrary-derived Vec<f64>, exercise every ContinuousDistribution::fit on the input, assert no panic and that the returned parameters are either all-NaN or all-finite.
2. Add p2c007_stats_pdf_cdf_ppf_identity.rs: generate seeded distributions, assert cdf(ppf(q)) ≈ q (round-trip MR); pdf >= 0; cdf monotonically non-decreasing.
3. Add p2c007_stats_rvs_determinism.rs: fixed seed must produce identical samples under rand 0.10.1.

Seed corpus should include: empty slice, all-zero, all-NaN, all-inf, mixed finite+NaN, constant samples (zero-width trigger, cf. t2wx), a few realistic samples.

Size guard: if input.len() > 1_000_000 { return; } per skill rule 1.
